description: Structural reduction to modular matroids and simplification issues
full_text:
## Conventions

Let \(E\) be the ground set of \(M\). Write  
\[
L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)
\]
for the set of loops. This is the **bottom flat** of \(L(M)\); when \(M\) has loops, the bottom flat is not \(\varnothing\).

Let
\[
S=\operatorname{si}(M)
\]
be obtained by deleting loops and replacing each non-loop parallel class by one element. Let \(\pi:E\setminus L_0\to \bar E\) be the quotient map to the set \(\bar E\) of parallel classes.

---

# 1. Simplification inherits the order-reversing involution

Every flat \(F\in L(M)\) contains \(L_0\), and if \(e\in F\) then the whole parallel class of \(e\) lies in \(F\). Hence flats of \(M\) are exactly sets of the form
\[
F=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F),
\]
where \(\bar F\) is a flat of \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\).

Thus there is a canonical lattice isomorphism
\[
\psi:L(M)\longrightarrow L(S),
\qquad
\psi(F)=\pi(F\setminus L_0),
\]
with inverse
\[
\psi^{-1}(\bar F)=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F).
\]
In particular,
\[
\psi(L_0)=\varnothing,
\qquad
\psi(E)=\bar E.
\]

Let
\[
\alpha:L(M)\to L(M),
\qquad
F\mapsto F^\perp
\]
be the given order-reversing involution. Since \(L_0\) is the bottom flat and \(E\) is the top flat,
\[
\alpha(L_0)=E,
\qquad
\alpha(E)=L_0.
\]
Define
\[
\bar\alpha=\psi\circ \alpha\circ \psi^{-1}:L(S)\to L(S).
\]
Then \(\bar\alpha\) is again an order-reversing involution, and
\[
\bar\alpha(\varnothing)=\bar E,
\qquad
\bar\alpha(\bar E)=\varnothing.
\]

Because \(S\) is simple and \(L(S)\cong L(S)^{\mathrm{op}}\), the matroid \(S\) is its own adjoint in the sense of (A1). Therefore, by (A1),

\[
\boxed{\operatorname{si}(M)\text{ is modular}.}
\]

The degenerate case \(S=\varnothing\) is harmless: the one-element lattice is self-dual and the empty matroid is modular.

---

# 2. Component decomposition and action of the involution

By (A3), since \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\) is simple and modular, its connected components are of the following types:
\[
S=\bigoplus_{i\in I} S_i,
\]
where each \(S_i\) is either

\[
U_{1,1}
\]

or a finite projective geometry
\[
\operatorname{PG}(r_i-1,q_i).
\]

The flat lattice decomposes as a product:
\[
L(S)\cong \prod_{i\in I} L(S_i).
\]

Write a flat \(F\in L(S)\) as a tuple
\[
F=(F_i)_{i\in I}.
\]

## Atoms and coatoms

An atom of \(L(S)\) is a flat supported in one component:
\[
(0,\dots,0,p,0,\dots,0),
\]
where \(p\) is a point of some \(S_i\).

A coatom of \(L(S)\) is a flat of the form
\[
(1,\dots,1,H,1,\dots,1),
\]
where \(H\) is a hyperplane of some \(S_j\).

Because \(\bar\alpha\) is order-reversing, it maps atoms bijectively to coatoms.

For points \(p,q\) in the same projective-geometry component \(S_i\), the interval
\[
[0,p\vee q]
\]
is a projective line and has \(q_i+1\ge 3\) atoms. If \(p,q\) lie in different components, then \([0,p\vee q]\) is Boolean of rank \(2\), hence has exactly \(2\) atoms.

Applying \(\bar\alpha\), the interval \([0,p\vee q]\) is anti-isomorphic to
\[
[\bar\alpha(p)\wedge \bar\alpha(q),1].
\]
Thus two atoms in the same projective component must map to coatoms supported in the same component; otherwise the upper interval would have only two coatoms. Conversely, atoms in distinct components cannot all map into the same projective-geometry component, because then the corresponding upper interval would have at least three coatoms.

Therefore \(\bar\alpha\) permutes the connected components.

More precisely, there is an involutive permutation
\[
\sigma:I\to I,
\qquad
\sigma^2=\mathrm{id},
\]
and for each \(i\in I\) an order-reversing lattice isomorphism
\[
\delta_i:L(S_i)\longrightarrow L(S_{\sigma(i)})
\]
such that
\[
\bar\alpha(F)_{\sigma(i)}=\delta_i(F_i).
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\bar\alpha((F_i)_{i\in I})
=
\bigl(\delta_{\sigma^{-1}(j)}(F_{\sigma^{-1}(j)})\bigr)_{j\in I}.
\]

Since \(\bar\alpha^2=\mathrm{id}\), we also have
\[
\delta_{\sigma(i)}=\delta_i^{-1}.
\]

## Constraints on paired components

Because \(\delta_i\) reverses ranks, \(S_i\) and \(S_{\sigma(i)}\) must have the same rank. Moreover:

- A \(U_{1,1}\)-component can only be paired with another \(U_{1,1}\)-component.
- A component \(\operatorname{PG}(r-1,q)\) can only be paired with another \(\operatorname{PG}(r-1,q)\).
- If \(\sigma(i)=i\), then \(\delta_i\) is a polarity/order-reversing involution of \(L(S_i)\).
- If \(\sigma(i)=j\neq i\), then \(\delta_i\) is a projective duality from \(S_i\) to \(S_j\), and \(\delta_j=\delta_i^{-1}\).

In atom/coatom language:

- A point \(p\in S_i\) maps to a hyperplane \(\delta_i(p)\subseteq S_{\sigma(i)}\).
- A hyperplane \(H\subseteq S_i\) maps to a point \(\delta_i(H)\in S_{\sigma(i)}\).

---

# 3. Relationship between \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) and \(\operatorname{Dr}(\operatorname{si}(M))\)

Work in extended tropical coordinates \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\). Define the diagonal embedding
\[
\Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\longrightarrow \mathbb T^E
\]
by
\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty, & e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)}, & e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]

Then
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(M)=\Delta(\operatorname{Trop}(S)).
\]

Indeed, loops of \(M\) force coordinates to be \(\infty\), and a parallel pair \(e,f\) gives a two-element circuit, forcing \(x_e=x_f\). After imposing those equations, the remaining circuit conditions are exactly the circuit conditions for \(S\).

Now let \(\theta\) be a valuated matroid with
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(\theta)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(M).
\]
Equivalently, \(\theta\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).

The following facts are useful.

### Loop/parallel detection in a valuated matroid

For a valuated matroid \(\theta\):

1. An element \(e\) is a loop of \(\theta\) iff every point of \(\operatorname{Trop}(\theta)\) has \(x_e=\infty\).

2. If \(e,f\) are nonloops and \(\operatorname{Trop}(\theta)\subseteq \{x_e=x_f\}\), then \(e,f\) are parallel in the underlying matroid of \(\theta\), and their valuated parallel offset is zero.

The reason is that valuated cocircuits are points of \(\operatorname{Trop}(\theta)\). If \(e,f\) were not parallel, some cocircuit would contain exactly one of them, giving a point with one coordinate finite and the other \(\infty\). If \(e,f\) are parallel with nonzero offset, their valuated two-element circuit forces \(x_e-x_f\) to equal that nonzero offset, contradicting \(x_e=x_f\).

Applying this to the inclusion
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(\theta)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(M),
\]
we get:

- Every loop of \(M\) remains a loop of \(\theta\).
- Each parallel class of \(M\) is not split by \(\theta\): it becomes either an all-loop class, or a valuated parallel class with zero internal offsets.

Thus \(\theta\) descends uniquely to a valuated matroid \(\bar\theta\) on \(\bar E\), obtained by identifying each original parallel class and deleting the original loops. Moreover,
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(\theta)=\Delta(\operatorname{Trop}(\bar\theta)).
\]

Conversely, if \(\bar\theta\) is a valuated matroid on \(\bar E\), then its zero parallel extension to \(E\), with the elements of \(L_0\) added as loops, satisfies
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(\Delta_*\bar\theta)
=
\Delta(\operatorname{Trop}(\bar\theta)).
\]

Therefore
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(\theta)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(M)
\]
if and only if
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(\bar\theta)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(S).
\]

Hence the diagonal map induces an isomorphism of posets
\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{Dr}(S)\cong \operatorname{Dr}(M)
}
\]
given by
\[
X\longmapsto \Delta(X).
\]
The inverse sends a tropical linear subspace \(Y\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(M)\) to \(\Delta^{-1}(Y)\).

A correction to a tempting stronger statement: a quotient may create new parallelisms between different simplified elements. For example,
\[
U_{1,1}\oplus U_{1,1}=U_{2,2}
\]
has quotient \(U_{1,2}\), whose tropical space is the diagonal \(x_1=x_2\). Thus \(\operatorname{Dr}\) does not factor componentwise in any naive way.

---

# 4. Compatibility with the embedded flat subposet

Let \(F\in L(M)\), and let
\[
\bar F=\psi(F)\in L(S).
\]
Then
\[
F=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F).
\]

Consider the matroid
\[
N_F=M/F\oplus U_{0,F}
\]
on the ground set \(E\). Similarly define
\[
\bar N_{\bar F}=S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}
\]
on the ground set \(\bar E\).

The matroid \(N_F\) is exactly the zero parallel extension of \(\bar N_{\bar F}\), with the original loops added back as loops. One way to see this is through ranks. For \(A\subseteq E\setminus F\),
\[
r_{M/F}(A)
=
r_M(A\cup F)-r_M(F).
\]
Since \(M\) is a parallel extension of \(S\),
\[
r_M(A\cup F)=r_S(\pi(A)\cup \bar F),
\qquad
r_M(F)=r_S(\bar F),
\]
so
\[
r_{M/F}(A)=r_{S/\bar F}(\pi(A)).
\]
Adding \(U_{0,F}\) and \(U_{0,\bar F}\) gives the claimed compatibility on the full ground sets.

Therefore
\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta\bigl(\operatorname{Trop}(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\bigr).
}
\]

Thus the diagram
\[
L(M)^{\mathrm{op}}\hookrightarrow \operatorname{Dr}(M)
\]
corresponds under simplification to
\[
L(S)^{\mathrm{op}}\hookrightarrow \operatorname{Dr}(S).
\]

Moreover, the involution is preserved: if \(\alpha(F)=F^\perp\) and \(\bar\alpha=\psi\alpha\psi^{-1}\), then
\[
\psi(F^\perp)=\bar\alpha(\psi(F)).
\]

Consequently, existence of an order-reversing involution on \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) extending \(\alpha\) is equivalent to existence of an order-reversing involution on \(\operatorname{Dr}(S)\) extending \(\bar\alpha\).

---

# 5. Clean reduced version of the original question

After the reduction, one may assume:

1. \(M\) is loopless and simple.
2. \(M\) is modular.
3. Therefore
   \[
   M=\bigoplus_{i\in I} M_i,
   \]
   where each \(M_i\) is either \(U_{1,1}\) or \(\operatorname{PG}(r_i-1,q_i)\).
4. The prescribed flat-lattice involution has the form
   \[
   \alpha(F)_{\sigma(i)}=\delta_i(F_i),
   \]
   where \(\sigma:I\to I\) is an involution and each
   \[
   \delta_i:L(M_i)\to L(M_{\sigma(i)})
   \]
   is an order-reversing lattice isomorphism.

The remaining question is therefore:

> For a direct sum of singleton components and finite projective geometries, equipped with such a componentwise projective duality on its flat lattice, does there exist an order-reversing involution of \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) extending the prescribed involution on the embedded flat subposet?

One should not assume \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) factors over connected components: quotients of a direct sum can mix components, as already shown by \(U_{2,2}\twoheadrightarrow U_{1,2}\).

\[

\[

\qquad
F\mapsto F^\perp

\alpha(L_0)=E,

\[
\bar\alpha=\psi\circ \alpha\circ \psi^{-1}:L(S)\to L(S).
\]

\[
\bar\alpha(\varnothing)=\bar E,
\qquad
\bar\alpha(\bar E)=\varnothing.
\]

\[

\]

\[

\qquad
\sigma^2=\mathrm{id},

\[

\[
\bar\alpha((F_i)_{i\in I})
=
\bigl(\delta_{\sigma^{-1}(j)}(F_{\sigma^{-1}(j)})\bigr)_{j\in I}.

\[

\[

\]

\[

=
\Delta(\operatorname{Trop}(\bar\theta)).
\]

\[

\[

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{Dr}(S)\cong \operatorname{Dr}(M)

X\longmapsto \Delta(X).

\[
\bar F=\psi(F)\in L(S).
\]

F=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F).
\]

\[
N_F=M/F\oplus U_{0,F}
\]

\bar N_{\bar F}=S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}

r_M(A\cup F)=r_S(\pi(A)\cup \bar F),

---

# 5. Clean reduced version of the original question

After the reduction, one may assume:

1. \(M\) is loopless and simple.
2. \(M\) is modular.
3. Therefore
   \[
   M=\bigoplus_{i\in I} M_i,
   \]
   where each \(M_i\) is either \(U_{1,1}\) or \(\operatorname{PG}(r_i-1,q_i)\).
4. The prescribed flat-lattice involution has the form
   \[
   \alpha(F)_{\sigma(i)}=\delta_i(F_i),
   \]
   where \(\sigma:I\to I\) is an involution and each
   \[
   \delta_i:L(M_i)\to L(M_{\sigma(i)})
   \]
   is an order-reversing lattice isomorphism.

The remaining question is therefore:

> For a direct sum of singleton components and finite projective geometries, equipped with such a componentwise projective duality on its flat lattice, does there exist an order-reversing involution of \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) extending the prescribed involution on the embedded flat subposet?

One should not assume \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) factors over connected components: quotients of a direct sum can mix components, as already shown by \(U_{2,2}\twoheadrightarrow U_{1,2}\).

self_declared_problem_solved:
Let $M$ be a matroid, and let $\Trop(M)$ be the corresponding tropical linear space, i.e., the closure of the Bergman fan of $M$ in tropical projective space. The relative Dressian of $M$ is 
\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?

description: Codimension-one polarity and rank-three test
full_text:
## Setup and notation

Let \(r=\operatorname{rk} M\). Since \(M\) is simple, I identify elements of \(E\) with atoms of \(L(M)\). Let \(\mathcal H(M)\) denote the set of hyperplanes. For a flat \(G\), write

\[
\mathcal H_G:=\{H\in \mathcal H(M):G\subseteq H\}.
\]

The polarity \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) sends rank \(i\) flats to rank \(r-i\) flats, so it bijects hyperplanes with atoms.

---

## 1. The map \(\alpha:\operatorname{Trop}(M)\to \operatorname{Dr}^s_1(M)\)

In hyperplane-weight coordinates on \(\operatorname{Dr}^s_1(M)\), define

\[
\boxed{(\alpha(q))_H := q_{H^\perp}\qquad H\in \mathcal H(M).}
\]

Equivalently, on Bergman rays \(e_F\),

\[
(\alpha(e_F))_H=1
\iff H^\perp\subseteq F
\iff F^\perp\subseteq H
\iff H\in \mathcal H_{F^\perp}.
\]

Thus

\[
\boxed{\alpha(e_F)=e_{\mathcal H_{F^\perp}}.}
\]

Now check cones. If

\[
F_1\subsetneq F_2\subsetneq\cdots\subsetneq F_k,
\]

then polarity reverses the order:

\[
F_1^\perp\supsetneq F_2^\perp\supsetneq\cdots\supsetneq F_k^\perp.
\]

But \(G\mapsto \mathcal H_G\) is also order-reversing, since larger flats are contained in fewer hyperplanes. Hence

\[
\mathcal H_{F_1^\perp}\subsetneq
\mathcal H_{F_2^\perp}\subsetneq\cdots\subsetneq
\mathcal H_{F_k^\perp}.
\]

So chains of flats map to chains of linear subclasses in the correct inclusion order. Therefore \(\alpha\) is well-defined on every Bergman cone. In fact, in the ambient quotient coordinates it is just the linear coordinate relabeling \(H\mapsto H^\perp\).

The inverse is

\[
\boxed{(\alpha^{-1}(w))_e := w_{e^\perp}\qquad e\in E.}
\]

On rays of \(\operatorname{Dr}^s_1(M)\),

\[
\alpha^{-1}(e_{\mathcal H_G})=e_{G^\perp}.
\]

Indeed,

\[
(\alpha^{-1}(e_{\mathcal H_G}))_e
=1
\iff e^\perp\in\mathcal H_G
\iff G\subseteq e^\perp
\iff e\subseteq G^\perp.
\]

So \(\alpha\) is a PL fan isomorphism, with inverse given by \(e_{\mathcal H_G}\mapsto e_{G^\perp}\).

---

## 2. Effect on flat points and boundary rays

Let \(H\) be a hyperplane of \(M\). The flat point corresponding to \(H\) is the rank-one quotient

\[
P_H=\operatorname{Trop}(M/H\oplus U_{0,H}),
\]

represented in the Bergman fan by the ray \(e_H\). Then

\[
\alpha(e_H)=e_{\mathcal H_{H^\perp}}.
\]

By fact (B2), \(\mathcal H_{H^\perp}\) is the linear subclass of the principal elementary quotient associated to the flat \(H^\perp\). Since \(H^\perp\) is an atom, this is exactly the codimension-one flat tropical subspace corresponding to \(H^\perp\). Thus

\[
\boxed{\alpha(P_H)=L_{H^\perp}.}
\]

More generally, for any nonempty proper flat \(F\),

\[
\boxed{\alpha(e_F)=e_{\mathcal H_{F^\perp}}.}
\]

So a Bergman boundary ray indexed by \(F\) is sent to the codimension-one Dressian boundary ray indexed, under \(Qt^c_1(M)\cong L(M)\), by the polar flat \(F^\perp\). For a flag \(F_1\subset\cdots\subset F_k\), the corresponding cone maps to the cone indexed by

\[
\mathcal H_{F_1^\perp}\subset\cdots\subset\mathcal H_{F_k^\perp}.
\]

---

## 3. Incidence symmetry

I will prove the point-hyperplane incidence symmetry under the present assumptions:

\[
\boxed{p\in \alpha(q)\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad q\in \alpha(p)}
\]

for all \(p,q\in\operatorname{Trop}(M)\).

For \(q\in\operatorname{Trop}(M)\), let \(N_q=\alpha(q)\) be the corresponding rank \(r-1\) valuated quotient. Its Plücker coordinates are

\[
\nu_q(I)=
\begin{cases}
q_{\operatorname{cl}(I)^\perp}, & I\subseteq E,\ |I|=r-1,\ I\text{ independent in }M,\\
\infty, & I\text{ dependent in }M.
\end{cases}
\]

Then \(p\in \operatorname{Trop}(N_q)\) iff for every \(r\)-subset \(S\subseteq E\),

\[
\min_{i\in S}\bigl(p_i+\nu_q(S\setminus i)\bigr)
\]

is attained at least twice.

If \(S\) is a basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), then

\[
\nu_q(B\setminus b_i)=q_{\operatorname{cl}(B\setminus b_i)^\perp}.
\]

Define

\[
b_i^*:=\operatorname{cl}(B\setminus b_i)^\perp.
\]

The incidence equation for \(B\) becomes

\[
\boxed{
\min_i\bigl(p_{b_i}+q_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\text{ is attained at least twice.}
}
\tag{\(*\)}
\]

If \(S\) has rank \(r-1\), then it has a unique circuit \(C\), and the finite terms in the incidence equation are all of the form \(p_i+q_{\operatorname{cl}(S)^\perp}\) with \(i\in C\). Since \(p\in\operatorname{Trop}(M)\), the minimum of \(p_i\) on \(C\) is attained at least twice, so this equation is automatic. If \(\operatorname{rk}(S)<r-1\), all terms are \(\infty\). Therefore \(p\in\alpha(q)\) is equivalent to the basis equations \((*)\) for all bases \(B\).

Now modularity gives the key symmetry. For a basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), the set

\[
B^*:=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}
\]

is again a basis, and \((B^*)^*=B\). Indeed, for \(F_S=\operatorname{cl}\{b_i:i\in S\}\), modularity implies

\[
F_S\wedge F_T=F_{S\cap T}.
\]

Hence intersections of the coordinate hyperplanes \(\operatorname{cl}(B\setminus b_i)\) behave Booleanly, and polarity sends them to joins of the \(b_i^*\). In particular,

\[
\operatorname{cl}(B^*\setminus b_i^*)=b_i^\perp,
\]

so the star operation applied again gives back \(b_i\).

Thus the basis equation for \(p\in\alpha(q)\) on \(B\),

\[
\min_i(p_{b_i}+q_{b_i^*})
\]

is exactly the basis equation for \(q\in\alpha(p)\) on the dual basis \(B^*\),

\[
\min_i(q_{b_i^*}+p_{b_i}).
\]

Since \(B\mapsto B^*\) is a bijection on bases, all incidence equations are symmetric. Therefore

\[
\boxed{p\in\alpha(q)\iff q\in\alpha(p).}
\]

### Rank-three tests

- **\(U_{3,n}\).** For \(n>3\), \(U_{3,n}\) does not satisfy the assumptions: it is not modular, and its flat lattice cannot have an order-reversing involution because it has \(n\) atoms but \(\binom n2\) hyperplanes. The only admissible rank-three uniform case is \(U_{3,3}\), with Boolean polarity \(F^\perp=E\setminus F\). Then incidence is simply
  \[
  \min_i(p_i+q_i)\text{ attained twice},
  \]
  which is visibly symmetric.

- **Fano plane.** For a polarity of the Fano plane, \(\alpha(q)\) has line weights
  \[
  w_\ell=q_{\ell^\perp}.
  \]
  The concurrent-triple equations for \(\operatorname{Dr}^s_1(M)\) become the circuit equations for \(q\) on the polar line. Incidence for a noncollinear triangle \(a,b,c\) is
  \[
  \min\bigl(p_a+q_{(bc)^\perp},\ p_b+q_{(ac)^\perp},\ p_c+q_{(ab)^\perp}\bigr),
  \]
  and the polar triangle has vertices \((bc)^\perp,(ac)^\perp,(ab)^\perp\), so the same equation appears with \(p,q\) interchanged.

- **Arbitrary rank-three projective plane with polarity.** The same proof works. Concurrent lines through a point \(x\) polarize to collinear points on \(x^\perp\), and noncollinear triangles polarize to noncollinear triangles. Thus the three-term incidence equations are paired by triangle polarity.

---

## 4. Rank \(\le 3\): order-reversing involution on \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\)

Assume \(r=\operatorname{rk}M\le 3\). Then every nontrivial proper tropical subspace is either a point or a codimension-one subspace. Define \(\Phi\) by

\[
\Phi(\hat 0)=\hat 1=\operatorname{Trop}(M),
\qquad
\Phi(\hat 1)=\hat 0,
\]

\[
\boxed{\Phi(p)=\alpha(p)\quad\text{for points }p,}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{\Phi(L)=\alpha^{-1}(L)\quad\text{for codimension-one subspaces }L.}
\]

This is involutive because \(\alpha^{-1}\alpha=\mathrm{id}\) and \(\alpha\alpha^{-1}=\mathrm{id}\).

It is order-reversing. The only nontrivial incidence in rank \(3\) is a point \(p\) lying in a codimension-one subspace \(L\). Write \(L=\alpha(q)\). Then

\[
p\subseteq L
\iff p\in\alpha(q)
\iff q\in\alpha(p)
\iff \Phi(L)\subseteq \Phi(p),
\]

using incidence symmetry. The bottom/top cases are automatic.

Finally, \(\Phi\) extends the flat-lattice polarity. For a flat \(F\), let

\[
X_F:=\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}).
\]

If \(F=H\) is a hyperplane, then \(X_H\) is a point and

\[
\Phi(X_H)=\alpha(e_H)=X_{H^\perp}.
\]

If \(F=a\) is an atom, then \(X_a\) is codimension-one and

\[
\Phi(X_a)=\alpha^{-1}(X_a)=X_{a^\perp}.
\]

The cases \(F=\varnothing,E\) are precisely top and bottom. Hence

\[
\boxed{\Phi(X_F)=X_{F^\perp}}
\]

for all flats \(F\) when \(r\le 3\).

---

## 5. What is still needed for all ranks

The point-hyperplane incidence symmetry is not by itself enough to define \(\Phi\) on all of \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\). The needed extra theorem is:

> **Required all-rank polar theorem.**  
> For every rank \(k\) tropical linear subspace \(X\in\operatorname{Dr}_k(M)\), the set
> \[
> X^{\perp_\alpha}:=\{p\in\operatorname{Trop}(M):X\subseteq \alpha(p)\}
> \]
> is a tropical linear subspace in \(\operatorname{Dr}_{r-k}(M)\), and biduality holds:
> \[
> (X^{\perp_\alpha})^{\perp_\alpha}=X.
> \]

Equivalently, for every valuated quotient \(V\) of \(M\) of rank \(k\), one needs a canonical complementary valuated quotient \(V^{\perp_\alpha}\) of rank \(r-k\) such that

\[
p\in \operatorname{Trop}(V^{\perp_\alpha})
\iff
\operatorname{Trop}(V)\subseteq \alpha(p).
\]

The difficulty is that

\[
X^{\perp_\alpha}
=
\bigcap_{x\in X}\alpha(x)
\]

is an ordinary intersection of tropical hyperplanes. Ordinary intersections of tropical linear spaces need not have the expected dimension, need not be balanced, and need not themselves be tropical linear spaces. One would need a theorem identifying this incidence intersection with an appropriate stable intersection, or proving that it is independent of a choice of tropical spanning flag.

Thus the missing all-rank input is a stable-intersection/flag-completion theorem: every \(X\in\operatorname{Dr}_k(M)\) should admit compatible complete flags, reversing such a flag using the point-hyperplane polarity should give a complementary flag, and the resulting rank \(r-k\) member should be independent of the chosen flag. With that theorem, the formula

\[
\Phi(X)=\{p:X\subseteq \alpha(p)\}
\]

would give the desired order-reversing involution in all ranks.

  and the polar triangle has vertices \((bc)^\perp,(ac)^\perp,(ab)^\perp\), so the same equation appears with \(p,q\) interchanged.

- **Arbitrary rank-three projective plane with polarity.** The same proof works. Concurrent lines through a point \(x\) polarize to collinear points on \(x^\perp\), and noncollinear triangles polarize to noncollinear triangles. Thus the three-term incidence equations are paired by triangle polarity.

---

## 4. Rank \(\le 3\): order-reversing involution on \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\)

Assume \(r=\operatorname{rk}M\le 3\). Then every nontrivial proper tropical subspace is either a point or a codimension-one subspace. Define \(\Phi\) by

\[
\Phi(\hat 0)=\hat 1=\operatorname{Trop}(M),
\qquad
\Phi(\hat 1)=\hat 0,
\]

\[
\boxed{\Phi(p)=\alpha(p)\quad\text{for points }p,}
\]

and

\[
\boxed{\Phi(L)=\alpha^{-1}(L)\quad\text{for codimension-one subspaces }L.}
\]

This is involutive because \(\alpha^{-1}\alpha=\mathrm{id}\) and \(\alpha\alpha^{-1}=\mathrm{id}\).

It is order-reversing. The only nontrivial incidence in rank \(3\) is a point \(p\) lying in a codimension-one subspace \(L\). Write \(L=\alpha(q)\). Then

\[
p\subseteq L
\iff p\in\alpha(q)
\iff q\in\alpha(p)
\iff \Phi(L)\subseteq \Phi(p),
\]

using incidence symmetry. The bottom/top cases are automatic.

Finally, \(\Phi\) extends the flat-lattice polarity. For a flat \(F\), let

\[
X_F:=\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}).
\]

If \(F=H\) is a hyperplane, then \(X_H\) is a point and

\[
\Phi(X_H)=\alpha(e_H)=X_{H^\perp}.
\]

If \(F=a\) is an atom, then \(X_a\) is codimension-one and

\[
\Phi(X_a)=\alpha^{-1}(X_a)=X_{a^\perp}.
\]

The cases \(F=\varnothing,E\) are precisely top and bottom. Hence

\[
\boxed{\Phi(X_F)=X_{F^\perp}}
\]

for all flats \(F\) when \(r\le 3\).

---

## 5. What is still needed for all ranks

The point-hyperplane incidence symmetry is not by itself enough to define \(\Phi\) on all of \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\). The needed extra theorem is:

> **Required all-rank polar theorem.**  
> For every rank \(k\) tropical linear subspace \(X\in\operatorname{Dr}_k(M)\), the set
> \[
> X^{\perp_\alpha}:=\{p\in\operatorname{Trop}(M):X\subseteq \alpha(p)\}
> \]
> is a tropical linear subspace in \(\operatorname{Dr}_{r-k}(M)\), and biduality holds:
> \[
> (X^{\perp_\alpha})^{\perp_\alpha}=X.
> \]

Equivalently, for every valuated quotient \(V\) of \(M\) of rank \(k\), one needs a canonical complementary valuated quotient \(V^{\perp_\alpha}\) of rank \(r-k\) such that

\[
p\in \operatorname{Trop}(V^{\perp_\alpha})
\iff
\operatorname{Trop}(V)\subseteq \alpha(p).
\]

The difficulty is that

\[
X^{\perp_\alpha}
=
\bigcap_{x\in X}\alpha(x)
\]

is an ordinary intersection of tropical hyperplanes. Ordinary intersections of tropical linear spaces need not have the expected dimension, need not be balanced, and need not themselves be tropical linear spaces. One would need a theorem identifying this incidence intersection with an appropriate stable intersection, or proving that it is independent of a choice of tropical spanning flag.

Thus the missing all-rank input is a stable-intersection/flag-completion theorem: every \(X\in\operatorname{Dr}_k(M)\) should admit compatible complete flags, reversing such a flag using the point-hyperplane polarity should give a complementary flag, and the resulting rank \(r-k\) member should be independent of the chosen flag. With that theorem, the formula

\[
\Phi(X)=\{p:X\subseteq \alpha(p)\}
\]

would give the desired order-reversing involution in all ranks.

description: writeup (partial): Rank-at-most-three case for simple modular matroids with a flat-lattice polarity
full_text:
## Original Problem

Let $M$ be a matroid, and let $\Trop(M)$ be the corresponding tropical linear space, i.e., the closure of the Bergman fan of $M$ in tropical projective space. The relative Dressian of $M$ is 
\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?

## Problem Solved

We prove the following special-case theorem. Let \(E\) be a finite set, let \(M\) be a loopless simple matroid on \(E\) of rank \(r\le 3\), and assume that the lattice of flats \(\mathcal L(M)\) is modular, meaning that for all flats \(A,B\),
\[
\operatorname{rk}(A)+\operatorname{rk}(B)
=
\operatorname{rk}(A\cap B)+\operatorname{rk}(\operatorname{cl}(A\cup B)).
\]
Assume also that \(\mathcal L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution \(F\mapsto F^\perp\), i.e. an inclusion-reversing bijection with \((F^\perp)^\perp=F\). Let \(\operatorname{Trop}(M)\) denote the projective tropical linear space of \(M\), and let \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) denote the poset, ordered by inclusion, of projective tropical linear spaces \(L(N)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(M)\) arising from valuated matroid quotients \(N\) of \(M\). For a flat \(F\), put
\[
X_F:=\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}),
\]
where \(U_{0,F}\) is the rank-zero matroid of loops on \(F\), so that \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) is regarded as a matroid on the original ground set \(E\). Then there is an order-reversing involution
\[
\Phi:\operatorname{Dr}(M)\to \operatorname{Dr}(M)
\]
such that
\[
\Phi(X_F)=X_{F^\perp}
\]
for every flat \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\).

This is a relaxation of the original question: the original question asks about an arbitrary matroid with a flat-lattice polarity, while the theorem proved here assumes \(M\) is finite, loopless, simple, modular, and of rank at most \(3\).

## Proof

Let \(r=\operatorname{rk}(M)\). Since \(M\) is simple, we identify atoms of \(\mathcal L(M)\) with elements of \(E\). Since \(\mathcal L(M)\) is a finite graded lattice of rank \(r\), an order-reversing involution sends rank \(i\) flats to rank \(r-i\) flats. Thus, when \(r\ge 2\), it bijects hyperplanes with atoms.

We use three standard facts.

1. **Quotients and inclusion.** If \(v,w\) are valuated matroids on \(E\), then
   \[
   L(v)\subseteq L(w)
   \]
   precisely when \(v\) is a valuated matroid quotient of \(w\). In particular, elements of \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) are exactly valuated quotients of \(M\), ordered by inclusion of their tropical linear spaces.

2. **Uniqueness of Plücker vectors.** A projective tropical linear space determines its valuated Plücker vector up to adding a global scalar.

3. **Codimension-one identification.** For a simple modular matroid \(M\), codimension-one valuated quotients of \(M\) are encoded by weights \(w_H\) on hyperplanes \(H\in\mathcal H(M)\). Explicitly, the associated rank \(r-1\) valuated matroid has Plücker coordinates
   \[
   \nu(I)=
   \begin{cases}
   w_{\operatorname{cl}(I)}, & |I|=r-1,\ I\text{ independent in }M,\\
   \infty, & I\text{ dependent}.
   \end{cases}
   \]
   Under the polarity \(H\mapsto H^\perp\), this codimension-one relative Dressian is identified with \(\operatorname{Trop}(M)\).

Thus, for \(q=(q_e)_{e\in E}\in \operatorname{Trop}(M)\), define a codimension-one tropical subspace \(\alpha(q)\) by hyperplane weights
\[
\boxed{\alpha(q)_H:=q_{H^\perp}}.
\]
Equivalently, its rank \(r-1\) Plücker vector is
\[
\nu_q(I)=
\begin{cases}
q_{\operatorname{cl}(I)^\perp}, & |I|=r-1,\ I\text{ independent in }M,\\
\infty, & I\text{ dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]
The inverse map sends a codimension-one subspace with hyperplane weights \(w_H\) to the point \(q\in\operatorname{Trop}(M)\) with
\[
q_e=w_{e^\perp}.
\]
This is well-defined because tropical linear spaces determine their valuated Plücker vectors up to global scalar.

We next prove the key incidence symmetry. Assume \(2\le r\le 3\). Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be a basis of \(M\). Define
\[
b_i^*:=\operatorname{cl}(B\setminus\{b_i\})^\perp.
\]
Then \(B^*:=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}\) is again a basis, and
\[
\operatorname{cl}(B^*\setminus\{b_i^*\})=b_i^\perp.
\]
For \(r=2\), this is immediate: if \(B=\{a,b\}\), then \(a^*=b^\perp\), \(b^*=a^\perp\), and these are distinct atoms.

For \(r=3\), write \(B=\{a,b,c\}\). Then
\[
a^*=\operatorname{cl}(b,c)^\perp,\quad
b^*=\operatorname{cl}(a,c)^\perp,\quad
c^*=\operatorname{cl}(a,b)^\perp.
\]
Since \(b^*,c^*\le a^\perp\) and \(b^*\ne c^*\), their closure is \(a^\perp\). Similarly,
\[
\operatorname{cl}(a^*,c^*)=b^\perp,\qquad
\operatorname{cl}(a^*,b^*)=c^\perp.
\]
Also \(a^*\not\le a^\perp\), because otherwise applying \(\perp\) would give \(a\le \operatorname{cl}(b,c)\), contradicting that \(a,b,c\) is a basis. Hence \(a^*,b^*,c^*\) is a basis.

Now let \(p,q\in\operatorname{Trop}(M)\). We claim
\[
\boxed{p\in \alpha(q)\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad q\in \alpha(p).}
\]
By the tropical linear space equations for the rank \(r-1\) valuated matroid \(\alpha(q)\), the condition \(p\in\alpha(q)\) is that for every \(r\)-subset \(S\subseteq E\),
\[
\min_{i\in S}\bigl(p_i+\nu_q(S\setminus\{i\})\bigr)
\]
is attained at least twice.

If \(S=B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) is a basis, this becomes
\[
\boxed{
\min_i\bigl(p_{b_i}+q_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\text{ is attained at least twice.}
}
\]
If \(\operatorname{rk}(S)=r-1\), then \(S\) has a unique circuit \(C\). The only non-automatically-\(\infty\) terms occur for \(i\in C\), and they all have the same added quantity \(q_{\operatorname{cl}(S)^\perp}\). Hence the condition reduces to the circuit condition for \(p\in\operatorname{Trop}(M)\). If \(\operatorname{rk}(S)<r-1\), all terms are \(\infty\), so the condition is automatic.

Therefore \(p\in\alpha(q)\) is equivalent to the displayed basis equations for all bases \(B\). But the basis equation for \(B\),
\[
\min_i(p_{b_i}+q_{b_i^*}),
\]
is exactly the basis equation for \(B^*\) with \(p\) and \(q\) interchanged, because \((b_i^*)^*=b_i\). Since \(B\mapsto B^*\) is an involution on bases, the equations for \(p\in\alpha(q)\) and \(q\in\alpha(p)\) are identical. Thus incidence is symmetric.

We now define \(\Phi\). If \(r=0\), the Dressian has only one element and we take \(\Phi=\mathrm{id}\). If \(r=1\), then \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) consists only of the bottom and top elements, and we swap them.

Assume now \(2\le r\le 3\). Let \(\hat 0\) be the rank-zero tropical linear space and \(\hat 1=\operatorname{Trop}(M)\). Define
\[
\Phi(\hat 0)=\hat 1,\qquad \Phi(\hat 1)=\hat 0.
\]
For a point \(p\in\operatorname{Trop}(M)\), viewed as a rank-one element of \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\), set
\[
\Phi(p)=\alpha(p).
\]
For a codimension-one tropical subspace \(L\), set
\[
\Phi(L)=\alpha^{-1}(L).
\]
When \(r=2\), the rank-one and codimension-one cases coincide, and the two definitions agree because \(\alpha^{-1}=\alpha\).

The map \(\Phi\) is involutive by construction: \(\alpha^{-1}\alpha=\mathrm{id}\), \(\alpha\alpha^{-1}=\mathrm{id}\), and \(\hat 0,\hat 1\) are swapped.

It remains to prove that \(\Phi\) reverses inclusion. Let \(X\subseteq Y\) in \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\). Equal-rank inclusions are equal, because inclusion corresponds to valuated quotient and a same-rank quotient has the same Plücker vector up to scalar. The bottom/top cases are automatic. In rank \(3\), the only nontrivial strict inclusion is a point \(p\) contained in a codimension-one subspace \(L\). Write \(L=\alpha(q)\). Then
\[
p\subseteq L
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
p\in\alpha(q)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
q\in\alpha(p)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\Phi(L)\subseteq \Phi(p),
\]
where the middle equivalence is the incidence symmetry proved above. Thus \(\Phi\) is order-reversing.

Finally we verify that \(\Phi\) extends the flat-lattice polarity. For a flat \(F\), write
\[
X_F:=\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}).
\]
The flats \(\varnothing\) and \(E\) map to \(\hat 1\) and \(\hat 0\), and \(\Phi\) swaps them, matching \(\varnothing^\perp=E\) and \(E^\perp=\varnothing\).

Let \(H\) be a hyperplane. Then \(X_H\) is a point whose rank-one coordinates may be normalized as
\[
q^H_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty, & e\in H,\\
0, & e\notin H.
\end{cases}
\]
For any hyperplane \(K\),
\[
\alpha(q^H)_K=q^H_{K^\perp}.
\]
Thus \(\alpha(q^H)_K=\infty\) exactly when \(K^\perp\le H\), equivalently when \(H^\perp\le K\). These are precisely the hyperplane weights of the codimension-one flat tropical subspace \(X_{H^\perp}\). Hence
\[
\Phi(X_H)=X_{H^\perp}.
\]

Similarly, let \(a\) be an atom. The codimension-one subspace \(X_a\) has hyperplane weights
\[
w^a_K=
\begin{cases}
\infty, & a\le K,\\
0, & a\nleq K.
\end{cases}
\]
Therefore \(\alpha^{-1}(X_a)\) has point coordinates
\[
q_e=w^a_{e^\perp}.
\]
So \(q_e=\infty\) exactly when \(a\le e^\perp\), equivalently when \(e\le a^\perp\). This is exactly the point \(X_{a^\perp}\). Hence
\[
\Phi(X_a)=X_{a^\perp}.
\]
Since, for \(r\le 3\), every proper nonempty flat is either an atom or a hyperplane, this proves
\[
\Phi(X_F)=X_{F^\perp}
\]
for every flat \(F\). The theorem follows.

The only non-automatically-\(\infty\) terms occur for \(i\in C\), and they all have the same added quantity \(q_{\operatorname{cl}(S)^\perp}\). Hence the condition reduces to the circuit condition for \(p\in\operatorname{Trop}(M)\). If \(\operatorname{rk}(S)<r-1\), all terms are \(\infty\), so the condition is automatic.

Therefore \(p\in\alpha(q)\) is equivalent to the displayed basis equations for all bases \(B\). But the basis equation for \(B\),
\[
\min_i(p_{b_i}+q_{b_i^*}),
\]
is exactly the basis equation for \(B^*\) with \(p\) and \(q\) interchanged, because \((b_i^*)^*=b_i\). Since \(B\mapsto B^*\) is an involution on bases, the equations for \(p\in\alpha(q)\) and \(q\in\alpha(p)\) are identical. Thus incidence is symmetric.

We now define \(\Phi\). If \(r=0\), the Dressian has only one element and we take \(\Phi=\mathrm{id}\). If \(r=1\), then \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) consists only of the bottom and top elements, and we swap them.

Assume now \(2\le r\le 3\). Let \(\hat 0\) be the rank-zero tropical linear space and \(\hat 1=\operatorname{Trop}(M)\). Define
\[
\Phi(\hat 0)=\hat 1,\qquad \Phi(\hat 1)=\hat 0.
\]
For a point \(p\in\operatorname{Trop}(M)\), viewed as a rank-one element of \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\), set
\[
\Phi(p)=\alpha(p).
\]
For a codimension-one tropical subspace \(L\), set
\[
\Phi(L)=\alpha^{-1}(L).
\]
When \(r=2\), the rank-one and codimension-one cases coincide, and the two definitions agree because \(\alpha^{-1}=\alpha\).

The map \(\Phi\) is involutive by construction: \(\alpha^{-1}\alpha=\mathrm{id}\), \(\alpha\alpha^{-1}=\mathrm{id}\), and \(\hat 0,\hat 1\) are swapped.

It remains to prove that \(\Phi\) reverses inclusion. Let \(X\subseteq Y\) in \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\). Equal-rank inclusions are equal, because inclusion corresponds to valuated quotient and a same-rank quotient has the same Plücker vector up to scalar. The bottom/top cases are automatic. In rank \(3\), the only nontrivial strict inclusion is a point \(p\) contained in a codimension-one subspace \(L\). Write \(L=\alpha(q)\). Then
\[
p\subseteq L
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
p\in\alpha(q)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
q\in\alpha(p)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\Phi(L)\subseteq \Phi(p),
\]
where the middle equivalence is the incidence symmetry proved above. Thus \(\Phi\) is order-reversing.

Finally we verify that \(\Phi\) extends the flat-lattice polarity. For a flat \(F\), write
\[
X_F:=\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}).
\]
The flats \(\varnothing\) and \(E\) map to \(\hat 1\) and \(\hat 0\), and \(\Phi\) swaps them, matching \(\varnothing^\perp=E\) and \(E^\perp=\varnothing\).

Let \(H\) be a hyperplane. Then \(X_H\) is a point whose rank-one coordinates may be normalized as
\[
q^H_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty, & e\in H,\\
0, & e\notin H.
\end{cases}
\]
For any hyperplane \(K\),
\[
\alpha(q^H)_K=q^H_{K^\perp}.
\]
Thus \(\alpha(q^H)_K=\infty\) exactly when \(K^\perp\le H\), equivalently when \(H^\perp\le K\). These are precisely the hyperplane weights of the codimension-one flat tropical subspace \(X_{H^\perp}\). Hence
\[
\Phi(X_H)=X_{H^\perp}.
\]

Similarly, let \(a\) be an atom. The codimension-one subspace \(X_a\) has hyperplane weights
\[
w^a_K=
\begin{cases}
\infty, & a\le K,\\
0, & a\nleq K.
\end{cases}
\]
Therefore \(\alpha^{-1}(X_a)\) has point coordinates
\[
q_e=w^a_{e^\perp}.
\]
So \(q_e=\infty\) exactly when \(a\le e^\perp\), equivalently when \(e\le a^\perp\). This is exactly the point \(X_{a^\perp}\). Hence
\[
\Phi(X_a)=X_{a^\perp}.
\]
Since, for \(r\le 3\), every proper nonempty flat is either an atom or a hyperplane, this proves
\[
\Phi(X_F)=X_{F^\perp}
\]
for every flat \(F\). The theorem follows.
self_declared_problem_solved: Let E be finite, let M be a loopless simple modular matroid on E of rank r <= 3, and suppose the flat lattice L(M) has an order-reversing involution F -> F^perp. Let Trop(M) be the projective tropical linear space of M, and let Dr(M) be the inclusion poset of projective tropical linear spaces inside Trop(M) arising from valuated matroid quotients of M. For each flat F, write X_F = Trop(M/F direct-sum U_{0,F}) on the same ground set E. Then there exists an order-reversing involution Phi: Dr(M) -> Dr(M) such that Phi(X_F) = X_{F^perp} for every flat F. This is weaker than the original problem because it assumes M is finite, loopless, simple, modular, and of rank at most 3.
advisor_statement: Rank-at-most-three case for simple modular matroids with a flat-lattice polarity

description: All-rank flat-coordinate polarity theorem
full_text:
Here is the strongest coordinate result I can justify cleanly. It proves the candidate polar is an **involutive rank-reversing map on the underlying set** of the relative Dressian and that it extends the flat polarity. The remaining gap is the **mixed-rank flag/order-reversal lemma**, stated precisely at the end.

Throughout, “the minimum is attained twice” means “there is no unique finite minimum”; if all relevant terms are \(\infty\), the relation is considered satisfied.

---

## 1. Circuit-incidence and flat-constancy

Let \(p\) be rank \(k\), and let \(M\) be the constant rank \(r\) valuated matroid. The flag incidence relations for \(p\) to be a valuated quotient of \(M\) are equivalent to:

\[
\tag{CI}
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p_{A\cup c}
\quad\text{has no unique finite minimum}
\]

for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\).

Indeed, given \(C\), choose \(U\) so that \(T=C\cup U\) has size \(r+1\) and \(T-i\) is an \(M\)-basis exactly for \(i\in C\). The flag relation for \(A,T\) gives (CI). Conversely, for any \(T\) of size \(r+1\), if \(\operatorname{rk}_M(T)<r\), the incidence relation is trivial; if \(\operatorname{rk}_M(T)=r\), then \(T\) has a unique circuit \(C_T\), and the incidence relation reduces to (CI) for \(C_T\).

Now (CI) implies:

### Lemma: \(p_I\) is constant on \(M\)-bases of the same flat.

First, if \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent and \(|I|=k\), choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and \(c\in C\). Put \(A=I\setminus c\). Then \(C\setminus A=\{c\}\), so (CI) forces \(p_I=\infty\). Thus every finite \(p\)-basis is \(M\)-independent.

Next, suppose \(I=A\cup a\) and \(I'=A\cup b\) are \(M\)-independent \(k\)-sets with the same \(M\)-closure. Then \(a\in\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup b)\), so there is an \(M\)-circuit

\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]

containing both \(a\) and \(b\). Hence \(C\setminus A=\{a,b\}\), and (CI) gives

\[
p_{A\cup a}=p_{A\cup b}.
\]

The basis graph of \(M|F\) is connected for every rank \(k\) flat \(F\), so \(p_I\) is constant over all \(M\)-bases \(I\) of \(F\).

Thus define

\[
\bar p(F):=p_I
\]

for any \(M\)-basis \(I\) of the rank \(k\) flat \(F\). If all such \(I\) have \(p_I=\infty\), then \(\bar p(F)=\infty\).

---

## 2. The coordinate polar and involutivity

Define a rank \(r-k\) candidate \(p^\perp\) by

\[
\boxed{
p^\perp_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp), & J \text{ is } M\text{-independent of size } r-k,\\
\infty, & J \text{ is } M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}}
\]

This is automatically constant on \(M\)-bases of the same rank \(r-k\) flat.

Moreover, once \(p^\perp\) is known to be a valuated quotient of \(M\), the construction is exactly involutive:

\[
(p^\perp)^\perp_I
=
\overline{p^\perp}(\operatorname{cl}_M(I)^\perp)
=
\bar p((\operatorname{cl}_M(I)^\perp)^\perp)
=
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(I))
=
p_I.
\]

So there is no scalar ambiguity except the usual projective one: adding a global constant to \(p\) adds the same constant to \(p^\perp\).

It also extends the flat polarity. If \(p^F\) is the constant valuated matroid of

\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F},
\]

then \(\bar p^F(G)\) is finite exactly when \(G\) is a complement of \(F\) in \(L(M)\). Since \(G\) is a complement of \(F\) iff \(G^\perp\) is a complement of \(F^\perp\), one gets

\[
\boxed{(p^F)^\perp=p^{F^\perp}.}
\]

---

## 3. Plücker relations for \(p^\perp\)

The key local statement is the following dual four-hyperplane relation.

### Local dual Plücker lemma

Let \(Y\) be a rank \(k+2\) flat of \(M\). Let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Define

\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j), & H_i\ne H_j,\\
\infty, & H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]

Then

\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\ [13]+[24],\ [14]+[23]\bigr)
\]

has no unique finite minimum.

Sketch of proof:

- If two \(H_i\)’s are equal, say \(H_1=H_2\), then \([12]=\infty\), while

  \[
  [13]+[24]=[13]+[14]=[14]+[23],
  \]

  so the relation is immediate.

- Assume the \(H_i\) are distinct. Put \(T=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4\). Since \(Y\) has rank \(k+2\), one has \(\operatorname{rk}(T)\in\{k-2,k-1,k\}\).

  If \(\operatorname{rk}(T)=k\), then every pairwise meet \(H_i\wedge H_j\) equals \(T\), so all three sums are equal.

  If \(\operatorname{rk}(T)=k-2\), then in the rank \(4\) interval \([T,Y]\), the four hyperplanes are in general position. Let

  \[
  P_i=\bigwedge_{j\ne i} H_j.
  \]

  Then each \(P_i\) has rank \(k-1\), and

  \[
  H_i\wedge H_j=P_m\vee P_n
  \]

  where \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\). Choosing a basis \(A\) of \(T\) and elements \(x_i\) with \(\operatorname{cl}(A\cup x_i)=P_i\), the ordinary tropical Plücker relation for \(p\) on \(A,x_1,x_2,x_3,x_4\) gives exactly the desired relation.

  If \(\operatorname{rk}(T)=k-1\), then \([T,Y]\) has rank \(3\). The function

  \[
  w(P)=\bar p(P)
  \]

  on the atoms \(P\) of \([T,Y]\) is a tropical point of that rank-three modular matroid, by the circuit-incidence relations from Section 1. Applying the rank-three codimension-one polarity from t2, the polar rank-two valuated matroid has coordinates

  \[
  u_{ab}=w(\delta(a\vee b)),
  \]

  and its rank-two Plücker relation is precisely the displayed relation for the four hyperplanes \(H_i/T\).

Now take a Plücker relation for \(p^\perp\). Let

\[
S\subseteq E,\qquad |S|=r-k-2,
\]

and let \(a,b,c,d\notin S\). If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, the relation is trivial. Otherwise put

\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S),\qquad Y=X^\perp,
\]

so \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+2\). For \(i\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define

\[
H_i=Y\wedge i^\perp=(X\vee i)^\perp.
\]

Then

\[
p^\perp_{S\cup i\cup j}
=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j), & S\cup i\cup j \text{ is } M\text{-independent},\\
\infty, & \text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]

Thus the Plücker relation for \(p^\perp\) is exactly the local dual Plücker relation above. Hence:

\[
\boxed{p^\perp \text{ is a valuated matroid of rank } r-k.}
\]

---

## 4. \(p^\perp\) is again a valuated quotient of \(M\)

It remains to prove the circuit-incidence relations for \(p^\perp\).

Let \(A\subseteq E\) have size \(r-k-1\), and let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit. If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, all terms \(p^\perp_{A\cup c}\) are \(\infty\), so assume \(A\) is independent. Put

\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A),\qquad Y=X^\perp.
\]

Then \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+1\). For \(c\notin X\),

\[
p^\perp_{A\cup c}
=
\bar p((X\vee c)^\perp)
=
\bar p(Y\wedge c^\perp).
\]

The interval \([X,E]\), i.e. the contraction \(M/X\), is anti-isomorphic to \([0,Y]\) by

\[
Z\longmapsto Z^\perp.
\]

The restriction of \(p\) to the hyperplanes of \(Y\) is a codimension-one valuated quotient of \(M|Y\). By the codimension-one polarity from t2, the function

\[
u_X(X\vee c):=\bar p((X\vee c)^\perp)
\]

is a tropical point of the contraction \(M/X\).

Now \(C\setminus X\) is a union of circuits in \(M/X\). Hence a unique finite minimum of \(u_X\) on \(C\setminus X\) would restrict to a unique finite minimum on some circuit of \(M/X\), impossible because \(u_X\in\operatorname{Trop}(M/X)\). Elements of \(C\cap X\) contribute \(\infty\). Therefore

\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p^\perp_{A\cup c}
\]

has no unique finite minimum. By the equivalence in Section 1, \(p^\perp\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).

Thus the coordinate construction gives a rank-reversing involution on the underlying set of \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\):

\[
\boxed{
p\longmapsto p^\perp.
}
\]

---

## 5. Remaining missing lemma: full order reversal for arbitrary flags

What is still not proved is the mixed-rank flag statement.

Let \(p,q\) be valuated quotients of \(M\) of ranks \(k\le \ell\), with

\[
\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(q),
\]

equivalently \(p\) is a valuated quotient of \(q\). To prove that the coordinate polar is an order-reversing involution, one must prove

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{Trop}(q^\perp)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(p^\perp).
}
\]

In coordinates, the missing statement is:

### Missing flag-duality lemma

For every \(A\subseteq E\) with \(|A|=r-\ell-1\) and every \(B\subseteq E\) with \(|B|=r-k+1\), the minimum

\[
\min_{i\in B\setminus A}
\left(
q^\perp_{A\cup i}
+
p^\perp_{B\setminus i}
\right)
\]

has no unique finite minimum.

Equivalently, after substituting the coordinate definitions,

\[
\min_{i\in B\setminus A}
\left(
\bar q(\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup i)^\perp)
+
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus i)^\perp)
\right)
\]

with the usual \(\infty\)-convention for dependent sets, should follow from the original flag incidence relations for \(p\subseteq q\).

This lemma would immediately finish the original problem: it would show that

\[
\Phi(\operatorname{Trop}(p)):=\operatorname{Trop}(p^\perp)
\]

is order-reversing, involutive, and extends \(F\mapsto F^\perp\).

I do not see a counterexample. The lemma holds in the Boolean case by ordinary valuated matroid duality, in rank \(\le 3\) by t2, and in the boundary case \(q=M\) by Section 4 above. The unresolved part is the genuinely mixed-rank flag incidence when neither side is the ambient \(M\).

p^\perp_{S\cup i\cup j}
=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j), & S\cup i\cup j \text{ is } M\text{-independent},\\
\infty, & \text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]

Thus the Plücker relation for \(p^\perp\) is exactly the local dual Plücker relation above. Hence:

\[
\boxed{p^\perp \text{ is a valuated matroid of rank } r-k.}
\]

---

## 4. \(p^\perp\) is again a valuated quotient of \(M\)

It remains to prove the circuit-incidence relations for \(p^\perp\).

Let \(A\subseteq E\) have size \(r-k-1\), and let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit. If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, all terms \(p^\perp_{A\cup c}\) are \(\infty\), so assume \(A\) is independent. Put

\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A),\qquad Y=X^\perp.
\]

Then \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+1\). For \(c\notin X\),

\[
p^\perp_{A\cup c}
=
\bar p((X\vee c)^\perp)
=
\bar p(Y\wedge c^\perp).
\]

The interval \([X,E]\), i.e. the contraction \(M/X\), is anti-isomorphic to \([0,Y]\) by

\[
Z\longmapsto Z^\perp.
\]

The restriction of \(p\) to the hyperplanes of \(Y\) is a codimension-one valuated quotient of \(M|Y\). By the codimension-one polarity from t2, the function

\[
u_X(X\vee c):=\bar p((X\vee c)^\perp)
\]

is a tropical point of the contraction \(M/X\).

Now \(C\setminus X\) is a union of circuits in \(M/X\). Hence a unique finite minimum of \(u_X\) on \(C\setminus X\) would restrict to a unique finite minimum on some circuit of \(M/X\), impossible because \(u_X\in\operatorname{Trop}(M/X)\). Elements of \(C\cap X\) contribute \(\infty\). Therefore

\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p^\perp_{A\cup c}
\]

has no unique finite minimum. By the equivalence in Section 1, \(p^\perp\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).

Thus the coordinate construction gives a rank-reversing involution on the underlying set of \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\):

\[
\boxed{
p\longmapsto p^\perp.
}
\]

---

## 5. Remaining missing lemma: full order reversal for arbitrary flags

What is still not proved is the mixed-rank flag statement.

Let \(p,q\) be valuated quotients of \(M\) of ranks \(k\le \ell\), with

\[
\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(q),
\]

equivalently \(p\) is a valuated quotient of \(q\). To prove that the coordinate polar is an order-reversing involution, one must prove

\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{Trop}(q^\perp)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(p^\perp).
}
\]

In coordinates, the missing statement is:

### Missing flag-duality lemma

For every \(A\subseteq E\) with \(|A|=r-\ell-1\) and every \(B\subseteq E\) with \(|B|=r-k+1\), the minimum

\[
\min_{i\in B\setminus A}
\left(
q^\perp_{A\cup i}
+
p^\perp_{B\setminus i}
\right)
\]

has no unique finite minimum.

Equivalently, after substituting the coordinate definitions,

\[
\min_{i\in B\setminus A}
\left(
\bar q(\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup i)^\perp)
+
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus i)^\perp)
\right)
\]

with the usual \(\infty\)-convention for dependent sets, should follow from the original flag incidence relations for \(p\subseteq q\).

This lemma would immediately finish the original problem: it would show that

\[
\Phi(\operatorname{Trop}(p)):=\operatorname{Trop}(p^\perp)
\]

is order-reversing, involutive, and extends \(F\mapsto F^\perp\).

I do not see a counterexample. The lemma holds in the Boolean case by ordinary valuated matroid duality, in rank \(\le 3\) by t2, and in the boundary case \(q=M\) by Section 4 above. The unresolved part is the genuinely mixed-rank flag incidence when neither side is the ambient \(M\).

self_declared_problem_solved:
Let $M$ be a matroid, and let $\Trop(M)$ be the corresponding tropical linear space, i.e., the closure of the Bergman fan of $M$ in tropical projective space. The relative Dressian of $M$ is 
\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?

description: Local dual Plücker lemma and rank-four stress test
full_text:
I believe the local lemma is **true** under the stated hypotheses. The degeneracies collapse into a small number of modular-rank cases; the only genuinely non-Plücker case is a rank-three “edge relation,” which follows from the circuit-incidence relations for the localized rank-one quotient.

Throughout, extend \(p\) to all flats by
\[
p(F)=\infty\quad\text{if } \operatorname{rk}F\ne k.
\]

## Local lemma

Let
\[
K_{ij}:=H_i\wedge H_j.
\]
If two hyperplanes are equal, say \(H_1=H_2\), then \(p(K_{12})=\infty\), and the other two sums are identical:
\[
p(K_{13})+p(K_{24})
=
p(K_{23})+p(K_{14}).
\]
So repeated hyperplanes are harmless.

Assume now that \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) are distinct. Then \(H_i\vee H_j=Y\), and modularity gives
\[
\operatorname{rk}(K_{ij})
=(k+1)+(k+1)-(k+2)=k.
\]
Thus all six \(K_{ij}\) are rank-\(k\) flats.

Put
\[
Z:=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4,
\qquad
d:=\operatorname{rk}(Y)-\operatorname{rk}(Z).
\]
Intersecting with a hyperplane lowers rank by at most one in a modular lattice, so \(d\le 4\). Since the \(H_i\) are distinct, \(d\ge 2\). Hence \(d=2,3,\) or \(4\).

### Case \(d=2\)

Here \(\operatorname{rk}Z=k\). Since each \(K_{ij}\) contains \(Z\) and has rank \(k\), all six pairwise meets equal \(Z\). Therefore all three displayed sums are equal to \(2p(Z)\).

### Case \(d=4\): the Boolean-position case is forced

Here \(\operatorname{rk}Z=k-2\). Define
\[
T_i:=\bigwedge_{j\ne i}H_j.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}T_i=k-1\). Indeed, intersecting three hyperplanes lowers rank by at most three, so \(\operatorname{rk}T_i\ge k-1\); if \(\operatorname{rk}T_i\ge k\), then intersecting \(T_i\) with the remaining hyperplane \(H_i\) would give rank at least \(k-1\), contradicting \(\operatorname{rk}Z=k-2\).

Moreover,
\[
T_i\wedge T_j=Z,
\]
and for \(\{a,b,c,d\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\),
\[
T_a\vee T_b=H_c\wedge H_d.
\]
Choose a basis \(B\) of \(Z\), and choose \(t_i\in T_i\setminus Z\) so that \(B\cup\{t_i\}\) spans \(T_i\). Then \(B\cup\{t_a,t_b\}\) spans \(T_a\vee T_b\). Applying the ordinary tropical Plücker relation for the rank-\(k\) valuated matroid \(p\) to \(B,t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4\) gives exactly
\[
\min\begin{cases}
p(K_{12})+p(K_{34}),\\
p(K_{13})+p(K_{24}),\\
p(K_{14})+p(K_{23})
\end{cases}
\]
attained at least twice.

So in modular rank \(4\), the supposedly “generic” tetrahedral situation is not merely generic: if the total meet has rank \(k-2\), then the triple meets automatically have rank \(k-1\), and the ordinary Plücker relation applies.

### Case \(d=3\): reduction to a rank-three edge lemma

Here \(\operatorname{rk}Z=k-1\). Contract by \(Z\) inside \(Y\). After simplification, the interval \([Z,Y]\) becomes a loopless simple modular rank-three matroid \(P\). The hyperplanes \(H_i/Z\) become hyperplanes \(L_i\) of \(P\), and the pairwise meets \(K_{ij}\) correspond to points
\[
x_{ij}:=L_i\wedge L_j.
\]

Choose a basis \(B\) of \(Z\). The contraction \(p/B\) is a rank-one valuated quotient of \(M/B\), unless all relevant values are \(\infty\), in which case the desired relation is trivial. Because \(p\) is a quotient of \(M\), this contraction satisfies the circuit-incidence relations in the rank-three interval. Thus the function
\[
w(x):=p(Z\vee x)
\]
on points \(x\) of \(P\) lies in \(\operatorname{Trop}(P)\).

So it remains to prove the following rank-three edge lemma.

## Rank-three edge lemma

Let \(P\) be a loopless simple modular rank-three matroid, let \(w\in\operatorname{Trop}(P)\), and let \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) be hyperplanes of \(P\). Then
\[
\min\begin{cases}
w(L_1\wedge L_2)+w(L_3\wedge L_4),\\
w(L_1\wedge L_3)+w(L_2\wedge L_4),\\
w(L_1\wedge L_4)+w(L_2\wedge L_3)
\end{cases}
\]
is attained at least twice.

If some \(L_i=L_j\), this is again trivial because two of the three sums become equal and the repeated meet contributes \(\infty\). So assume the \(L_i\) are distinct.

By the fine Bergman fan description, after adding a constant, \(w\) has the form
\[
w=\lambda \mathbf 1_a+\mu \mathbf 1_B
\]
for a flag \(a\le B\), where \(a\) is a point, \(B\) is a hyperplane, and \(\lambda,\mu\in[0,\infty]\). One-flat cases are included by allowing one coefficient to be \(0\).

First suppose none of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\). Put
\[
y_i:=L_i\wedge B.
\]
Then \(L_i\wedge L_j\le B\) iff \(y_i=y_j\), and \(L_i\wedge L_j=a\) iff \(y_i=y_j=a\). Thus each perfect matching of \(\{1,2,3,4\}\) receives a cost only from pairs whose \(y\)-colors agree. For four colored objects, the minimum matching cost is never unique: the color multiplicities are \(1+1+1+1\), \(2+1+1\), \(2+2\), \(3+1\), or \(4\), and in each case either all three matchings tie or two avoid the repeated color equally.

Now suppose one hyperplane equals \(B\), say \(L_1=B\). For \(j=2,3,4\), put \(y_j=L_j\wedge B\). The three matching costs are
\[
c(y_2)+[y_3=y_4]c(y_3),\quad
c(y_3)+[y_2=y_4]c(y_2),\quad
c(y_4)+[y_2=y_3]c(y_2),
\]
where \(c(a)=\lambda+\mu\) and \(c(y)=\mu\) for \(y\ne a\). If \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) are all distinct, at least two of the three costs are \(\mu\). If exactly two are equal, the two matchings pairing \(B\) with one of those equal colors tie for the minimum. If all three are equal, all three sums are equal. Hence the minimum is again attained at least twice.

This proves the rank-three edge lemma, and therefore the original local lemma.

## Rank \(4\), \(k=2\)

In \(PG(3,q)\), the statement says: for four planes \(\Pi_1,\Pi_2,\Pi_3,\Pi_4\), the six intersection lines satisfy the tropical opposite-edge relation
\[
\min\begin{cases}
p(\Pi_1\cap \Pi_2)+p(\Pi_3\cap \Pi_4),\\
p(\Pi_1\cap \Pi_3)+p(\Pi_2\cap \Pi_4),\\
p(\Pi_1\cap \Pi_4)+p(\Pi_2\cap \Pi_3)
\end{cases}
\]
with the minimum attained at least twice.

The cases are exactly:

1. **No common point.** The four planes form a tetrahedron. Their triple intersections are four vertices, and the opposite-edge relation is the ordinary rank-two Plücker relation for those four vertices.

2. **Common point.** Contract the common point. The four planes become four lines in a projective plane, and the relation is the rank-three edge lemma above.

3. **Common line.** All six pairwise intersections are the same line, so all three sums are equal.

4. **Repeated planes.** Two sums are identical, and the repeated meet contributes \(\infty\).

Thus there is no counterexample in \(PG(3,2)\). The same proof covers mixed modular direct sums such as a projective plane plus a point: the interval generated by the four hyperplanes has modular rank \(2,3,\) or \(4\), and the corresponding case above applies.

For the desired rank-four polarity, if \(p\) is rank \(2\), define
\[
p^\perp(L):=p(L^\perp)
\]
on rank-two flats \(L\). For four points \(a_1,a_2,a_3,a_4\), put \(H_i=a_i^\perp\). Then
\[
(a_i\vee a_j)^\perp=H_i\wedge H_j,
\]
so the rank-two Plücker relation for \(p^\perp\) is exactly the local lemma for \(p\). Hence the lemma verifies the **middle-rank Plücker part** of the rank-four involution. What remains for the full poset involution is the global incidence/order-reversal statement, not this local Plücker check.

\[

\[

p(K_{12})+p(K_{34}),\\

\[

\]

Because \(p\) is a quotient of \(M\), this contraction satisfies the circuit-incidence relations in the rank-three interval. Thus the function
\[
w(x):=p(Z\vee x)
\]
on points \(x\) of \(P\) lies in \(\operatorname{Trop}(P)\).

Let \(P\) be a loopless simple modular rank-three matroid, let \(w\in\operatorname{Trop}(P)\), and let \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) be hyperplanes of \(P\). Then
\[
\min\begin{cases}
w(L_1\wedge L_2)+w(L_3\wedge L_4),\\
w(L_1\wedge L_3)+w(L_2\wedge L_4),\\
w(L_1\wedge L_4)+w(L_2\wedge L_3)
\end{cases}
\]
is attained at least twice.

By the fine Bergman fan description, after adding a constant, \(w\) has the form
\[
w=\lambda \mathbf 1_a+\mu \mathbf 1_B
\]

First suppose none of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\). Put
\[
y_i:=L_i\wedge B.
\]
Then \(L_i\wedge L_j\le B\) iff \(y_i=y_j\), and \(L_i\wedge L_j=a\) iff \(y_i=y_j=a\). Thus each perfect matching of \(\{1,2,3,4\}\) receives a cost only from pairs whose \(y\)-colors agree. Now suppose one hyperplane equals \(B\), say \(L_1=B\). For \(j=2,3,4\), put \(y_j=L_j\wedge B\). The three matching costs are
\[
c(y_2)+[y_3=y_4]c(y_3),\quad
c(y_3)+[y_2=y_4]c(y_2),\quad
c(y_4)+[y_2=y_3]c(y_2),
\]
where \(c(a)=\lambda+\mu\) and \(c(y)=\mu\) for \(y\ne a\). If \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) are all distinct, at least two of the three costs are \(\mu\). If exactly two are equal, the two matchings pairing \(B\) with one of those equal colors tie for the minimum. If all three are equal, all three sums are equal. Hence the minimum is again attained at least twice.

This proves the rank-three edge lemma, and therefore the original local lemma.

## Rank \(4\), \(k=2\)

In \(PG(3,q)\), the statement says: for four planes \(\Pi_1,\Pi_2,\Pi_3,\Pi_4\), the six intersection lines satisfy the tropical opposite-edge relation
\[
\min\begin{cases}
p(\Pi_1\cap \Pi_2)+p(\Pi_3\cap \Pi_4),\\
p(\Pi_1\cap \Pi_3)+p(\Pi_2\cap \Pi_4),\\
p(\Pi_1\cap \Pi_4)+p(\Pi_2\cap \Pi_3)
\end{cases}
\]
with the minimum attained at least twice.

The cases are exactly:

1. **No common point.** The four planes form a tetrahedron. Their triple intersections are four vertices, and the opposite-edge relation is the ordinary rank-two Plücker relation for those four vertices.

2. **Common point.** Contract the common point. The four planes become four lines in a projective plane, and the relation is the rank-three edge lemma above.

3. **Common line.** All six pairwise intersections are the same line, so all three sums are equal.

4. **Repeated planes.** Two sums are identical, and the repeated meet contributes \(\infty\).

For the desired rank-four polarity, if \(p\) is rank \(2\), define
\[
p^\perp(L):=p(L^\perp)
\]
on rank-two flats \(L\). For four points \(a_1,a_2,a_3,a_4\), put \(H_i=a_i^\perp\). Then
\[
(a_i\vee a_j)^\perp=H_i\wedge H_j,
\]
so the rank-two Plücker relation for \(p^\perp\) is exactly the local lemma for \(p\). Hence the lemma verifies the **middle-rank Plücker part** of the rank-four involution. What remains for the full poset involution is the global incidence/order-reversal statement, not this local Plücker check.

self_declared_problem_solved:
Let $M$ be a matroid, and let $\Trop(M)$ be the corresponding tropical linear space, i.e., the closure of the Bergman fan of $M$ in tropical projective space. The relative Dressian of $M$ is 
\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?

description: Point-annihilator characterization of the polar
full_text:
The point-annihilator theorem **does succeed**. The clean way around the mixed-rank flag-duality lemma is to restrict to each Boolean apartment determined by an \(M\)-basis; on that apartment, the coordinate polar \(p^\dagger\) is just the ordinary valuated-matroid dual of \(p\).

Below, all tropical orthogonality/minimum conditions use the convention “no unique finite minimum.”

---

## Theorem

Let \(M\) be finite simple modular of rank \(r\), with flat-lattice polarity \(F\mapsto F^\perp\). Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\), and let \(p^\dagger\) be the coordinate polar from t3:
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp),& J \text{ \(M\)-independent},\ |J|=r-k,\\
\infty,& J \text{ \(M\)-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]
For \(x\in \operatorname{Trop}(M)\), define
\[
\alpha(x)_H=x_{H^\perp}
\]
for hyperplanes \(H\) of \(M\). Then
\[
\boxed{
x\in \operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(\alpha(x)).
}
\]

The boundary cases \(k=0,r\) are immediate, so assume \(1\le k\le r-1\).

---

## 1. Boolean apartments and ordinary duality

Let
\[
B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}
\]
be an \(M\)-basis. Define its polar basis
\[
b_i^\star:=\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus b_i)^\perp.
\]
By modularity and polarity, \(B^\star=\{b_1^\star,\dots,b_r^\star\}\) is again an \(M\)-basis, and for every \(S\subseteq [r]\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^\star:i\in S\}
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}^{\perp}.
\]

Let \(p|_B\) be the ordinary valuated matroid on \([r]\) defined by
\[
(p|_B)_T:=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}},\qquad |T|=k.
\]
Since \(p\) is a quotient of \(M\), every \(M\)-basis spans the underlying matroid of \(p\), so \(p|_B\) has rank \(k\).

Now restrict \(p^\dagger\) to the polar basis \(B^\star\). If \(|S|=r-k\), then
\[
(p^\dagger|_{B^\star})_S
=
p^\dagger_{\{b_i^\star:i\in S\}}
=
\bar p\!\left(\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^\star:i\in S\}^{\perp}\right)
=
\bar p\!\left(\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}\right)
=
p_{\{b_i:i\notin S\}}.
\]
Thus
\[
\boxed{
p^\dagger|_{B^\star}=(p|_B)^*
}
\]
as an ordinary valuated matroid dual on the Boolean ground set \([r]\).

We also use the standard Boolean annihilator fact:

> If \(\mu\) is a valuated matroid on a finite set and \(\mu^*\) its ordinary dual, then
> \[
> u\in \operatorname{Trop}(\mu^*)
> \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
> \operatorname{Trop}(\mu)\subseteq H_u,
> \]
> where
> \[
> H_u=\{v:\min_i(u_i+v_i)\text{ is attained at least twice}\}.
> \]
> This follows because the circuits of \(\mu^*\) are the cocircuits of \(\mu\), and \(\operatorname{Trop}(\mu)\) is the tropical span of its valuated cocircuits.

---

## 2. Direction \(x\in \operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger)\Rightarrow \operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(\alpha(x))\)

Assume \(x\in \operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger)\). Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_i\}\), and set
\[
u_B:=(x_{b_i^\star})_{i=1}^r.
\]
Since \(x\in \operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger)\), its restriction to \(B^\star\) lies in
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger|_{B^\star})
=
\operatorname{Trop}((p|_B)^*).
\]
By the Boolean annihilator fact,
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(p|_B)\subseteq H_{u_B}.
\]

Now let \(C_A\) be any valuated cocircuit of \(p\), with
\[
(C_A)_e=p_{A\cup e}
\]
for \(|A|=k-1\). Since cocircuits of \(p\) lie in \(\operatorname{Trop}(p)\), the restriction
\[
(C_A)|_B=(p_{A\cup b_i})_i
\]
lies in \(\operatorname{Trop}(p|_B)\). Therefore
\[
\min_i\bigl(p_{A\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^\star}\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimum.

But this is exactly the basis-incidence equation for
\[
C_A\in \operatorname{Trop}(\alpha(x)).
\]
Because this holds for every \(M\)-basis \(B\), every valuated cocircuit of \(p\) lies in \(\operatorname{Trop}(\alpha(x))\).

Finally, \(\operatorname{Trop}(p)\) is the tropical span of the valuated cocircuits of \(p\), and \(\operatorname{Trop}(\alpha(x))\) is tropically convex. Hence
\[
\boxed{
\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(\alpha(x)).
}
\]

---

## 3. Direction \(\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(\alpha(x))\Rightarrow x\in \operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger)\)

Assume
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(\alpha(x)).
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_i\}\), and again put
\[
u_B=(x_{b_i^\star})_i.
\]

We claim
\[
u_B\in \operatorname{Trop}((p|_B)^*).
\]
By the Boolean annihilator fact, it is enough to show that \(u_B\) is tropically orthogonal to every valuated cocircuit of \(p|_B\).

A cocircuit of \(p|_B\) has the form
\[
D_S=(p_{S\cup b_i})_i
\]
for some \((k-1)\)-subset \(S\subseteq B\). This is precisely the restriction to \(B\) of the \(p\)-cocircuit
\[
C_S=(p_{S\cup e})_{e\in E}.
\]
Since \(C_S\in \operatorname{Trop}(p)\), the containment assumption gives
\[
C_S\in \operatorname{Trop}(\alpha(x)).
\]
Testing membership in \(\operatorname{Trop}(\alpha(x))\) on the basis \(B\) gives
\[
\min_i\bigl(p_{S\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^\star}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimum. That is exactly
\[
D_S\perp u_B.
\]
Thus \(u_B\) is orthogonal to every cocircuit of \(p|_B\), so
\[
u_B\in \operatorname{Trop}((p|_B)^*)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger|_{B^\star}).
\]

Now let \(J\subseteq E\) be any \(M\)-independent subset of size
\[
|J|=r-k+1.
\]
Extend \(J\) to an \(M\)-basis \(B^\star\). From the previous paragraph,
\[
x|_{B^\star}\in \operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger|_{B^\star}).
\]
Therefore the ordinary circuit equation for \(J\subseteq B^\star\) gives
\[
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(x_j+p^\dagger_{J\setminus j}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimum. Substituting the definition of \(p^\dagger\),
\[
p^\dagger_{J\setminus j}
=
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J\setminus j)^\perp),
\]
we obtain exactly the coordinate condition
\[
\min_{j\in J}
\left(
x_j+\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J\setminus j)^\perp)
\right)
\]
with no unique finite minimum.

These are precisely the independent-\(J\) point equations for
\[
x\in \operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]
If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent, the equation is automatic from \(x\in \operatorname{Trop}(M)\): either all terms are \(\infty\), or the finite terms differ by a constant on the unique \(M\)-circuit in \(J\). Hence
\[
\boxed{
x\in \operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger).
}
\]

This proves the theorem.

---

## 4. Consequence: full order reversal

Define
\[
\Psi(\operatorname{Trop}(p))
:=
\{x\in \operatorname{Trop}(M):\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(\alpha(x))\}.
\]
By the theorem,
\[
\Psi(\operatorname{Trop}(p))=\operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]
Therefore, if
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(q),
\]
then
\[
\Psi(\operatorname{Trop}(q))
\subseteq
\Psi(\operatorname{Trop}(p)),
\]
because any hyperplane containing \(\operatorname{Trop}(q)\) also contains \(\operatorname{Trop}(p)\).

Using t3’s involutivity
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p,
\]
we obtain an order-reversing involution
\[
\boxed{
\Phi(\operatorname{Trop}(p)):=\operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger)
}
\]
on \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\). The flat-extension property from t3 gives
\[
\Phi\!\left(\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}),
\]
so \(\Phi\) extends the prescribed flat-lattice polarity.

\]
