description: writeup (partial): For a finite simple modular matroid M with a flat-lattice polarity, the flat-coordinate construction p -> p^dagger gives a well-defined rank-reversing involution on the underlying set of relative Dressian points and extends the prescribed polarity on the embedded flat subposet.
full_text:
## Original Problem

Let $M$ be a matroid, and let $\Trop(M)$ be the corresponding tropical linear space, i.e., the closure of the Bergman fan of $M$ in tropical projective space. The relative Dressian of $M$ is 
\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?

## Problem Solved

Let \(M\) be a finite simple matroid of rank \(r\) on ground set \(E\), assume that its lattice of flats \(L(M)\) is modular, and assume that \(L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution
\[
F\longmapsto F^\perp
\]
satisfying \((F^\perp)^\perp=F\). Let \(0\le k\le r\). In this write-up, a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\) means a function
\[
p:\binom{E}{k}\to \mathbb R\cup\{\infty\},
\]
not identically \(\infty\), considered up to adding one global real constant to all finite values, satisfying the tropical Plücker relations and the following circuit-incidence relations: for every circuit \(C\) of \(M\) and every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\), with the convention that \(p_T=\infty\) unless \(T\) is a \(k\)-subset, the minimum of
\[
\{p_{A\cup\{c\}}:c\in C\}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

The theorem proved here is the following partial result. First, every such \(p\) satisfies \(p_I=\infty\) for every \(M\)-dependent \(k\)-subset \(I\), and \(p_I\) depends only on the flat \(\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\) for every \(M\)-independent \(k\)-subset \(I\). Write \(\bar p(F)\) for this common value when \(F\) is a rank-\(k\) flat, and set \(\bar p(F)=\infty\) for flats of rank other than \(k\). Define a function \(p^\dagger\) on \((r-k)\)-subsets \(J\subseteq E\) by
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\bigr),& J\text{ is }M\text{-independent},\\
\infty,& J\text{ is }M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]
Then \(p^\dagger\) is a rank-\((r-k)\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\), the operation \(p\mapsto p^\dagger\) descends to projective valuated matroids, and
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p
\]
projectively, in fact exactly for any fixed representative.

Moreover, for every flat \(F\in L(M)\), let \(p^F\) denote the constant valuated matroid of the matroid \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) on \(E\), i.e. the rank-\((r-\operatorname{rk}F)\) valuated matroid with value \(0\) on bases of \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) and value \(\infty\) elsewhere. Then
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}
\]
projectively, indeed exactly with the above \(0/\infty\) representatives.

This is a relaxation of the original problem. It proves a set-level rank-reversing involution on valuated quotients of \(M\), and it proves compatibility with the embedded flat subposet. It does not prove that this involution reverses arbitrary inclusions of tropical linear subspaces, and therefore it does not prove the existence of an order-reversing involution on the whole relative Dressian poset.

## Proof

### 1. Conventions and elementary modular facts

All minima below are interpreted in \(\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\). A finite family has its minimum “attained at least twice” if either all entries are \(\infty\), or the smallest finite value occurs for at least two indices. Thus a singleton family satisfies this condition exactly when its only entry is \(\infty\).

For a function \(p\) defined on \(k\)-subsets, we extend notation by setting \(p_T=\infty\) for every subset \(T\subseteq E\) with \(|T|\ne k\).

Because \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) is an order-reversing involution of the finite ranked lattice \(L(M)\), it satisfies
\[
(F\vee G)^\perp=F^\perp\wedge G^\perp,\qquad
(F\wedge G)^\perp=F^\perp\vee G^\perp,
\]
and
\[
\operatorname{rk}(F^\perp)=r-\operatorname{rk}(F).
\]
Indeed, a maximal chain from \(\hat 0\) to \(F\) is carried to a maximal chain from \(F^\perp\) to \(\hat 1\).

We shall use the following elementary consequence of modularity.

**Lemma 1.1: line-closed sets are flats.**  
Let \(N\) be a finite simple modular matroid. Suppose \(S\) is a set of atoms of \(L(N)\) with the property that whenever \(a,b\in S\), every atom on the rank-two flat \(a\vee b\) also lies in \(S\). Then \(S\) is the set of atoms of a flat.

**Proof.** Let \(x\) be an atom in the closure of \(S\). Choose atoms \(a_1,\dots,a_m\in S\) minimal such that
\[
x\le a_1\vee\cdots\vee a_m.
\]
If \(m=1\), then \(x=a_1\in S\). Suppose \(m\ge2\), and put
\[
P=a_1\vee\cdots\vee a_{m-1},\qquad L=a_m\vee x.
\]
By minimality, \(x\not\le P\) and \(a_m\not\le P\). Also \(x\le P\vee a_m\), so \(P\vee L=P\vee a_m\). Since \(L\) has rank \(2\), modularity gives
\[
\operatorname{rk}(P\wedge L)
=
\operatorname{rk}P+\operatorname{rk}L-\operatorname{rk}(P\vee L)
=
(m-1)+2-m
=
1.
\]
Thus \(P\wedge L\) is an atom \(b\). By induction on \(m\), \(b\in S\). Since \(b,a_m\in S\) and \(x\) lies on the line \(b\vee a_m=L\), line-closedness gives \(x\in S\). Hence \(S\) is closed. \(\square\)

We shall also use the standard Bergman criterion for rank-one quotients.

**Lemma 1.2: tropical point criterion.**  
Let \(N\) be a finite matroid on a set of atoms \(A(N)\), and let \(w:A(N)\to\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\), not identically \(\infty\). Then \(w\) satisfies
\[
\min_{x\in C} w_x
\]
with no unique finite minimizer for every circuit \(C\) of \(N\) if and only if, for every real number \(\tau\), the upper level set
\[
A_\tau=\{x\in A(N):w_x\ge \tau\}
\]
is a flat of \(N\).

**Proof.** If some \(A_\tau\) is not a flat, then there is \(e\notin A_\tau\) with \(e\in\operatorname{cl}(A_\tau)\). Hence there is a circuit
\[
C\subseteq A_\tau\cup\{e\}
\]
containing \(e\). Then \(w_e<\tau\le w_x\) for all \(x\in C\setminus\{e\}\), so \(e\) is the unique finite minimizer on \(C\).

Conversely, if some circuit \(C\) has a unique finite minimizer \(e\), choose \(\tau\) strictly between \(w_e\) and the second-smallest value on \(C\). Then \(C\setminus\{e\}\subseteq A_\tau\), but \(e\notin A_\tau\) and \(e\in\operatorname{cl}(C\setminus\{e\})\). Thus \(A_\tau\) is not a flat. \(\square\)

### 2. Valuated quotients and flat-constancy

A rank-\(k\) valuated matroid on \(E\) is a function
\[
p:\binom{E}{k}\to\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\},
\]
not identically \(\infty\), satisfying the tropical Plücker relations: for every \((k-2)\)-subset \(S\subseteq E\) and distinct \(a,b,c,d\notin S\),
\[
\min\left(
p_{S\cup\{a,b\}}+p_{S\cup\{c,d\}},
p_{S\cup\{a,c\}}+p_{S\cup\{b,d\}},
p_{S\cup\{a,d\}}+p_{S\cup\{b,c\}}
\right)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. For \(k=0\) or \(k=1\), these relations are vacuous.

A rank-\(k\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\) is a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid \(p\) satisfying the circuit-incidence relations
\[
\min_{c\in C} p_{A\cup\{c\}}
\]
with no unique finite minimizer for every circuit \(C\) of \(M\) and every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\).

We first prove the essential flat-constancy property.

**Lemma 2.1: flat-constancy.**  
Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\). Then:

1. If \(I\subseteq E\) is an \(M\)-dependent \(k\)-subset, then \(p_I=\infty\).
2. If \(I,I'\subseteq E\) are \(M\)-independent \(k\)-subsets with
   \[
   \operatorname{cl}_M(I)=\operatorname{cl}_M(I'),
   \]
   then \(p_I=p_{I'}\).

**Proof.** The case \(k=0\) is trivial, so assume \(k\ge1\).

First let \(I\) be an \(M\)-dependent \(k\)-subset. Choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and an element \(c\in C\). Put \(A=I\setminus\{c\}\). Then every element of \(C\setminus\{c\}\) already lies in \(A\), so all terms in
\[
\{p_{A\cup\{x\}}:x\in C\}
\]
are \(\infty\) except possibly the term \(p_{A\cup\{c\}}=p_I\). The circuit-incidence relation therefore forces \(p_I=\infty\).

Now suppose
\[
I=A\cup\{a\},\qquad I'=A\cup\{b\}
\]
are \(M\)-independent \(k\)-subsets with the same \(M\)-closure. Since \(a\in\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup\{b\})\) but \(a\notin\operatorname{cl}_M(A)\), there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing \(a\). This circuit must also contain \(b\), because otherwise \(C\subseteq A\cup\{a\}=I\), contradicting independence of \(I\). Therefore the only possibly finite terms in the circuit-incidence relation for \(C\) and \(A\) are
\[
p_{A\cup\{a\}},\qquad p_{A\cup\{b\}}.
\]
The condition that the minimum have no unique finite minimizer implies
\[
p_{A\cup\{a\}}=p_{A\cup\{b\}}.
\]

Finally, any two bases of the restriction \(M|F\) of a matroid to a rank-\(k\) flat \(F\) are connected by a sequence of single basis exchanges. Applying the adjacent-basis argument along such a sequence shows that \(p_I\) is constant on all \(M\)-bases \(I\) of \(F\). \(\square\)

Thus for every rank-\(k\) flat \(F\), we may define
\[
\bar p(F):=p_I
\]
for any \(M\)-basis \(I\) of \(F\). For flats \(F\) with \(\operatorname{rk}F\ne k\), set
\[
\bar p(F):=\infty.
\]

### 3. The definition of \(p^\dagger\)

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\). Define \(p^\dagger\) on \((r-k)\)-subsets \(J\subseteq E\) by
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\bigr),& J\text{ is }M\text{-independent},\\
\infty,& J\text{ is }M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]
If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent of size \(r-k\), then \(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)\) has rank \(r-k\), so \(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\) has rank \(k\), and the expression is meaningful.

The function \(p^\dagger\) is not identically \(\infty\). Indeed, since \(p\) is not identically \(\infty\), there is a \(k\)-subset \(I\) with \(p_I<\infty\). By Lemma 2.1, \(I\) is \(M\)-independent. Let \(F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\). Choose a basis \(J\) of the rank-\((r-k)\) flat \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
p^\dagger_J=\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)=\bar p(F)=p_I<\infty.
\]

It remains to prove that \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the tropical Plücker relations and the quotient circuit-incidence relations.

### 4. A rank-three edge lemma

We first prove the rank-three local fact needed later.

**Lemma 4.1: rank-three edge lemma.**  
Let \(P\) be a finite simple modular matroid of rank \(3\). Let
\[
w:A(P)\to\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}
\]
be a tropical point of \(P\), meaning that the minimum of \(w\) on every circuit of \(P\) has no unique finite minimizer. Extend \(w\) to non-atoms by setting \(w(F)=\infty\) if \(F\) is not an atom. For any four hyperplanes, i.e. rank-two flats,
\[
L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4
\]
of \(P\), the minimum of
\[
w(L_1\wedge L_2)+w(L_3\wedge L_4),
\]
\[
w(L_1\wedge L_3)+w(L_2\wedge L_4),
\]
\[
w(L_1\wedge L_4)+w(L_2\wedge L_3)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

**Proof.** If two of the \(L_i\) are equal, say \(L_1=L_2\), then \(L_1\wedge L_2=L_1\) is not an atom, so \(w(L_1\wedge L_2)=\infty\), while the other two sums are equal:
\[
w(L_1\wedge L_3)+w(L_2\wedge L_4)
=
w(L_1\wedge L_4)+w(L_2\wedge L_3).
\]
Thus the assertion is immediate. Hence assume the \(L_i\) are distinct.

If all values of \(w\) are \(\infty\), the result is trivial. Otherwise add a global constant so that the minimum value of \(w\) is \(0\). By Lemma 1.2, the positive upper level sets of \(w\) are flats. Since \(P\) has rank \(3\), the nonempty proper flats in a chain have the form
\[
a\le B
\]
where \(a\) is a point and \(B\) is a hyperplane. Hence, after choosing such a flag, there exist \(\lambda,\mu\in[0,\infty]\) such that
\[
w(x)=\lambda\mathbf 1_{\{a\}}(x)+\mu\mathbf 1_B(x).
\]
Thus \(w(a)=\lambda+\mu\), \(w(x)=\mu\) for \(x\in B\setminus\{a\}\), and \(w(x)=0\) for \(x\notin B\).

First suppose none of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\). Put
\[
y_i=L_i\wedge B.
\]
For \(i\ne j\), the point \(L_i\wedge L_j\) lies on \(B\) exactly when \(y_i=y_j\), and in that case
\[
L_i\wedge L_j=y_i.
\]
Thus the cost of pairing \(i\) and \(j\) is \(0\) if \(y_i\ne y_j\), is \(\mu\) if \(y_i=y_j\ne a\), and is \(\lambda+\mu\) if \(y_i=y_j=a\).

We are therefore looking at the three perfect matchings of four colored objects \(y_1,y_2,y_3,y_4\), where a matched pair contributes a nonnegative cost only when its two colors agree. If all four colors are distinct, all three matching costs are \(0\). If exactly one pair of colors agrees, the two matchings avoiding that pair tie for the minimum. If the colors have multiplicities \(2+2\), the two cross-matchings tie for the minimum. If the multiplicities are \(3+1\), all three matchings have the same cost. If all four colors agree, all three matchings again have the same cost. Hence no unique finite minimum occurs.

Now suppose one of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\), say \(L_1=B\). For \(j=2,3,4\), put
\[
y_j=L_j\wedge B.
\]
Define
\[
c(y)=
\begin{cases}
\lambda+\mu,& y=a,\\
\mu,& y\ne a.
\end{cases}
\]
The three matching sums are
\[
c(y_2)+[y_3=y_4]c(y_3),
\]
\[
c(y_3)+[y_2=y_4]c(y_2),
\]
\[
c(y_4)+[y_2=y_3]c(y_2).
\]
If \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) are all distinct, at least two of them are not \(a\), so at least two of the three sums equal \(\mu\), the minimum. If exactly two of \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) are equal, then the two matchings pairing \(B\) with one of those equal colors have the same cost and are minimal. If all three are equal, all three sums are equal. This proves the lemma. \(\square\)

### 5. The local dual Plücker lemma

We now prove the key local relation.

**Lemma 5.1: local dual Plücker lemma.**  
Let \(Y\) be a flat of \(M\) of rank \(k+2\). Let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\), not necessarily distinct. Set
\[
K_{ij}=H_i\wedge H_j.
\]
Then, with the convention \(\bar p(F)=\infty\) unless \(\operatorname{rk}F=k\), the minimum of
\[
\bar p(K_{12})+\bar p(K_{34}),
\]
\[
\bar p(K_{13})+\bar p(K_{24}),
\]
\[
\bar p(K_{14})+\bar p(K_{23})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

**Proof.** If two hyperplanes are equal, say \(H_1=H_2\), then \(K_{12}=H_1\) has rank \(k+1\), so \(\bar p(K_{12})=\infty\). The other two sums are equal:
\[
\bar p(K_{13})+\bar p(K_{24})
=
\bar p(K_{23})+\bar p(K_{14}).
\]
Thus the result follows. Hence assume \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) are distinct.

Since \(L(M)\) is modular, two distinct hyperplanes of \(Y\) meet in rank \(k\). Therefore every \(K_{ij}\) has rank \(k\). Put
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Intersecting with a hyperplane lowers rank by at most one, so
\[
\operatorname{rk}Z\ge k-2.
\]
Because \(H_1\) and \(H_2\) are distinct, \(H_1\wedge H_2\) has rank \(k\), so \(\operatorname{rk}Z\le k\). Thus there are three cases.

#### Case 1: \(\operatorname{rk}Z=k\)

Every \(K_{ij}\) contains \(Z\) and has rank \(k\), hence \(K_{ij}=Z\) for all \(i,j\). All three sums are equal to \(2\bar p(Z)\).

#### Case 2: \(\operatorname{rk}Z=k-2\)

For each \(i\), define
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\ne i}H_j.
\]
We claim \(\operatorname{rk}T_i=k-1\). Since \(T_i\) is the intersection of three hyperplanes in \(Y\), its rank is at least \(k-1\). If \(\operatorname{rk}T_i\ge k\), then intersecting \(T_i\) with the remaining hyperplane \(H_i\) would give rank at least \(k-1\), contradicting \(\operatorname{rk}Z=k-2\). Hence \(\operatorname{rk}T_i=k-1\).

Let \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\). Then \(T_m,T_n\subseteq H_i\wedge H_j=K_{ij}\). The flats \(T_m\) and \(T_n\) are distinct rank-\((k-1)\) flats over \(Z\), so their join has rank \(k\). Therefore
\[
T_m\vee T_n=K_{ij}.
\]

Choose a basis \(B\) of \(Z\), and for each \(i\) choose an element \(t_i\in T_i\setminus Z\) such that \(B\cup\{t_i\}\) spans \(T_i\). Then
\[
B\cup\{t_m,t_n\}
\]
is an \(M\)-basis of \(K_{ij}\). By flat-constancy,
\[
p_{B\cup\{t_m,t_n\}}=\bar p(K_{ij}).
\]
Apply the ordinary tropical Plücker relation for the valuated matroid \(p\) to the \((k-2)\)-set \(B\) and the four elements \(t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4\). The three Plücker sums become exactly
\[
\bar p(K_{12})+\bar p(K_{34}),\quad
\bar p(K_{13})+\bar p(K_{24}),\quad
\bar p(K_{14})+\bar p(K_{23}).
\]
Thus the desired minimum has no unique finite minimizer.

#### Case 3: \(\operatorname{rk}Z=k-1\)

The interval \([Z,Y]\) is a rank-three modular geometric lattice. Let \(P\) be the simple rank-three matroid whose lattice of flats is \([Z,Y]\). The hyperplanes \(H_i\) become hyperplanes \(L_i=H_i/Z\) of \(P\), and the pairwise meets \(K_{ij}\) become points
\[
L_i\wedge L_j=K_{ij}/Z.
\]

Choose a basis \(B\) of \(Z\). For each atom \(x\) of \(P\), define
\[
w(x)=\bar p(Z\vee x).
\]
If all these values are \(\infty\), then the desired relation is trivial. Otherwise \(w\) is a tropical point of \(P\). Indeed, let \(D\) be a circuit of \(P\), and choose representatives \(e_x\in E\) for the atoms \(x\in D\). The set
\[
B\cup\{e_x:x\in D\}
\]
is dependent, whereas replacing \(D\) by any proper subset gives an independent set. Hence it contains an \(M\)-circuit \(C\) with
\[
C\setminus B=\{e_x:x\in D\}.
\]
Applying the quotient circuit-incidence relation for \(p\) with the \((k-1)\)-set \(B\), we get that
\[
\min_{x\in D} p_{B\cup\{e_x\}}
=
\min_{x\in D}\bar p(Z\vee x)
=
\min_{x\in D}w(x)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Thus \(w\) is a tropical point.

Now Lemma 4.1 applied to the four hyperplanes \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) gives exactly the desired relation for the six flats \(K_{ij}\). \(\square\)

### 6. The tropical Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\)

Let
\[
\ell=r-k.
\]
We prove that \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the rank-\(\ell\) tropical Plücker relations.

If \(\ell<2\), there are no Plücker relations to check. Assume \(\ell\ge2\). Let \(S\subseteq E\) have size \(\ell-2\), and let \(a,b,c,d\notin S\) be distinct. We must prove that the minimum of
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup\{a,b\}}+p^\dagger_{S\cup\{c,d\}},
\]
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup\{a,c\}}+p^\dagger_{S\cup\{b,d\}},
\]
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup\{a,d\}}+p^\dagger_{S\cup\{b,c\}}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, then every set \(S\cup\{i,j\}\) is \(M\)-dependent, so all six \(p^\dagger\)-terms are \(\infty\).

Assume \(S\) is \(M\)-independent, and put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S).
\]
If one of \(a,b,c,d\), say \(a\), lies in \(X\), then every \(\ell\)-subset appearing in the Plücker relation that contains \(a\) is \(M\)-dependent. Each of the three Plücker sums contains exactly one such term, so all three sums are \(\infty\). Hence we may assume
\[
a,b,c,d\notin X.
\]

Set
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Since \(\operatorname{rk}X=\ell-2=r-k-2\), we have \(\operatorname{rk}Y=k+2\). For \(x\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define
\[
H_x=(X\vee x)^\perp=Y\wedge x^\perp.
\]
Because \(x\notin X\), \(X\vee x\) has rank \(\ell-1\), so \(H_x\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\).

For any pair \(x,y\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), if \(S\cup\{x,y\}\) is \(M\)-independent, then
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(S\cup\{x,y\})=X\vee x\vee y,
\]
and hence
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(S\cup\{x,y\})^\perp
=
(X\vee x\vee y)^\perp
=
H_x\wedge H_y.
\]
Thus
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup\{x,y\}}=\bar p(H_x\wedge H_y).
\]
If \(S\cup\{x,y\}\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \(X\vee x=X\vee y\), so \(H_x=H_y\), and \(H_x\wedge H_y\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\), hence has rank \(k+1\). Therefore
\[
\bar p(H_x\wedge H_y)=\infty=p^\dagger_{S\cup\{x,y\}}.
\]
So in all cases,
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup\{x,y\}}=\bar p(H_x\wedge H_y).
\]

The desired Plücker relation for \(p^\dagger\) is therefore exactly the local dual Plücker relation of Lemma 5.1 applied to the four hyperplanes \(H_a,H_b,H_c,H_d\) of \(Y\). Hence \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the tropical Plücker relations.

### 7. A codimension-one polarity lemma for quotient incidence

We next prove the tool needed for the quotient circuit-incidence relations.

**Lemma 7.1.**  
Let \(N\) and \(N'\) be finite simple modular matroids of the same rank \(n\). Suppose
\[
\delta:L(N')\to L(N)
\]
is an order-reversing lattice isomorphism. Let \(q\) be a rank-\((n-1)\) valuated matroid quotient of \(N\). For every hyperplane \(H\) of \(N\), write \(\bar q(H)\) for the common value of \(q\) on bases of \(H\). Define a function \(u\) on atoms \(a\) of \(N'\) by
\[
u_a=\bar q(\delta(a)).
\]
Then \(u\) is a tropical point of \(N'\).

**Proof.** By Lemma 1.2, it is enough to show that for every real \(\tau\), the upper level set
\[
A_\tau=\{a\in A(N'):\ u_a\ge \tau\}
\]
is a flat of \(N'\). By Lemma 1.1, it is enough to show that \(A_\tau\) is line-closed.

Let \(a,b\in A_\tau\), and let \(c\) be an atom on the line \(a\vee b\). If \(c=a\) or \(c=b\), there is nothing to prove. Otherwise, \(a,b,c\) are distinct atoms on a rank-two flat of \(N'\). Applying \(\delta\), the flats
\[
\delta(a),\quad \delta(b),\quad \delta(c)
\]
are distinct hyperplanes of \(N\), all containing
\[
Z=\delta(a\vee b)=\delta(a)\wedge\delta(b),
\]
which has rank \(n-2\).

Choose a basis \(B\) of \(Z\), and choose elements \(x_a,x_b,x_c\) of \(N\) such that
\[
\operatorname{cl}_N(B\cup\{x_a\})=\delta(a),
\]
\[
\operatorname{cl}_N(B\cup\{x_b\})=\delta(b),
\]
\[
\operatorname{cl}_N(B\cup\{x_c\})=\delta(c).
\]
In the rank-two contraction \(N/Z\), the three corresponding atoms form a circuit. Equivalently, there is an \(N\)-circuit \(C\subseteq B\cup\{x_a,x_b,x_c\}\) with
\[
C\setminus B=\{x_a,x_b,x_c\}.
\]
Apply the quotient circuit-incidence relation for \(q\) with the \((n-2)\)-set \(B\). The minimum of
\[
q_{B\cup\{x_a\}},\quad q_{B\cup\{x_b\}},\quad q_{B\cup\{x_c\}}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. By flat-constancy,
\[
q_{B\cup\{x_a\}}=\bar q(\delta(a))=u_a,
\]
and similarly for \(b\) and \(c\).

Since \(u_a,u_b\ge\tau\), if \(u_c<\tau\), then \(u_c\) would be the unique finite minimum among the three displayed values. This is impossible. Hence \(u_c\ge\tau\), so \(c\in A_\tau\). Therefore \(A_\tau\) is line-closed, hence a flat. Thus \(u\) is a tropical point of \(N'\). \(\square\)

### 8. The quotient circuit-incidence relations for \(p^\dagger\)

We now prove that \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid quotient of \(M\).

Let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit, and let
\[
A\subseteq E,\qquad |A|=\ell-1=r-k-1.
\]
We must show that
\[
\min_{c\in C} p^\dagger_{A\cup\{c\}}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, then every \(A\cup\{c\}\) is \(M\)-dependent or has the wrong size, so all terms are \(\infty\). Hence assume \(A\) is \(M\)-independent, and put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A).
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}X=\ell-1=r-k-1\). Set
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Thus \(\operatorname{rk}Y=k+1\).

For \(c\in X\), the set \(A\cup\{c\}\) is \(M\)-dependent or has size \(\ell-1\), so
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup\{c\}}=\infty.
\]
For \(c\notin X\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup\{c\})=X\vee c,
\]
and therefore
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup\{c\}}
=
\bar p((X\vee c)^\perp)
=
\bar p(Y\wedge c^\perp).
\]

Consider the restriction \(M|Y\), which has rank \(k+1\). Define \(q\) on \(k\)-subsets of \(Y\) by restriction:
\[
q_I=p_I,\qquad I\subseteq Y,\ |I|=k.
\]
If \(q\) is identically \(\infty\), then \(\bar p(H)=\infty\) for every hyperplane \(H\) of \(Y\), and all terms \(p^\dagger_{A\cup\{c\}}\) are \(\infty\); the desired relation is trivial.

Assume \(q\) is not identically \(\infty\). Then \(q\) is a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M|Y\): the Plücker relations and circuit-incidence relations are inherited from \(p\).

The interval \([X,E]\), which is the lattice of flats of the simplification of the contraction \(M/X\), is order-reversingly isomorphic to \([0,Y]\) by
\[
Z\longmapsto Z^\perp.
\]
Applying Lemma 7.1 to \(N=M|Y\), to the simplified contraction \(N'=M/X\), and to \(q\), we obtain a tropical point \(u\) on the atoms of \(M/X\), given by
\[
u_{X\vee c}=\bar p((X\vee c)^\perp)=p^\dagger_{A\cup\{c\}}
\]
for \(c\notin X\).

Now return to the original circuit \(C\). The terms with \(c\in C\cap X\) are \(\infty\), so only \(C\setminus X\) matters. In the contraction \(M/X\), the set \(C\setminus X\) is dependent unless it is empty. Moreover, if \(c_0\in C\setminus X\), then because \(C\) is an \(M\)-circuit,
\[
c_0\in\operatorname{cl}_M(C\setminus\{c_0\})
\subseteq
\operatorname{cl}_M\bigl(X\cup(C\setminus X\setminus\{c_0\})\bigr).
\]
Thus in \(M/X\), the image of \(c_0\) lies in the closure of the images of the other elements of \(C\setminus X\). Consequently, every element of \(C\setminus X\) lies in some circuit of the contraction contained in \(C\setminus X\).

If the values
\[
\{p^\dagger_{A\cup\{c\}}:c\in C\}
\]
had a unique finite minimum at \(c_0\), then the corresponding contraction circuit containing \(c_0\) would have a unique finite minimum for the tropical point \(u\). This contradicts the circuit condition for \(u\). Therefore the quotient circuit-incidence relation holds for \(p^\dagger\).

Combining Sections 6 and 8, \(p^\dagger\) is a rank-\((r-k)\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\).

### 9. Involutivity

We now prove that
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]

Let \(I\subseteq E\) be a \(k\)-subset. If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \(p_I=\infty\) by Lemma 2.1, and the definition of \((p^\dagger)^\dagger\) also gives
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I=\infty.
\]

Assume \(I\) is \(M\)-independent, and put
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I).
\]
Then \(F\) has rank \(k\), so \(F^\perp\) has rank \(r-k\). Let \(J\) be any basis of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)
=
\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]
Hence the common value of \(p^\dagger\) on bases of \(F^\perp\) is \(p_I\). Therefore
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p_I.
\]
Thus \((p^\dagger)^\dagger=p\) exactly.

If \(p\) is replaced by \(p+c\) for a global constant \(c\), then \(\bar p\) and \(p^\dagger\) are also shifted by \(c\). Hence \(p\mapsto p^\dagger\) descends to projective valuated matroids and is an involution there.

### 10. Compatibility with flat points

Let \(F\) be a flat of \(M\). Let
\[
p^F
\]
be the \(0/\infty\)-valuated matroid of \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) on \(E\). Its rank is
\[
r-\operatorname{rk}F.
\]
For an independent set \(I\) of this rank, \(p^F_I=0\) exactly when \(I\) is a basis of \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\). Equivalently, if
\[
G=\operatorname{cl}_M(I),
\]
then \(G\) is a complement of \(F\) in \(L(M)\):
\[
G\wedge F=\hat 0,\qquad G\vee F=\hat 1.
\]
Indeed, \(I\) is a basis of the contraction \(M/F\) exactly when \(F\vee G=\hat 1\), and modularity then gives
\[
\operatorname{rk}(G\wedge F)
=
\operatorname{rk}G+\operatorname{rk}F-r
=
0.
\]

Thus
\[
\overline{p^F}(G)=
\begin{cases}
0,& G\text{ is a complement of }F,\\
\infty,& \text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]

Now let \(J\) be an \(M\)-independent subset of size \(\operatorname{rk}F\), and put
\[
G=\operatorname{cl}_M(J).
\]
Then
\[
(p^F)^\dagger_J
=
\overline{p^F}(G^\perp).
\]
This is finite exactly when \(G^\perp\) is a complement of \(F\), i.e. exactly when
\[
G^\perp\vee F=\hat 1,\qquad G^\perp\wedge F=\hat 0.
\]
Applying \(\perp\), these two conditions are equivalent to
\[
G\wedge F^\perp=\hat 0,\qquad G\vee F^\perp=\hat 1.
\]
That is, \(G\) is a complement of \(F^\perp\). Therefore
\[
(p^F)^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
0,& \operatorname{cl}_M(J)\text{ is a complement of }F^\perp,\\
\infty,& \text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
This is precisely the \(0/\infty\)-valuated matroid \(p^{F^\perp}\). Hence
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]

### 11. Scope of the result

The construction
\[
p\longmapsto p^\dagger
\]
is therefore a well-defined rank-reversing involution on the set of projective valuated matroid quotients of \(M\), and it agrees with the prescribed polarity on the embedded flat points.

No order-reversal statement for arbitrary inclusions has been proved here. In particular, this proof does not show that if one tropical linear subspace is contained in another, then their \(\dagger\)-images are contained in the opposite order. Thus the result is only a set-level involution plus compatibility with the flat subposet, not a full solution of the original Dressian-poset question.

```json
{

The relative Dressian of $M$ is \n\\[ \\Dr(M) :=\\{\\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\\Trop M$ }\\}, \\]\nwhich is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. \n\nSuppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\\mapsto F^\\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\\cLL(M)^{\\op} \\subset \\Dr(M)$ given by $F\\mapsto \\Trop(M/F\\oplus U_{0,F})$.\n\n\\medskip\n\\noindent \\textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\\Phi$ on $\\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\\mapsto F^\\perp$?",

Defining p^dagger_J = pbar(cl_M(J)^perp) for M-independent (r-k)-subsets J and infinity for M-dependent J gives a rank-(r-k) valuated matroid quotient of M. The operation descends to projective valuated matroids, satisfies (p^dagger)^dagger = p projectively, and sends the flat quotient M/F plus loops on F to the flat quotient M/F^perp plus loops on F^perp. No order-reversal for arbitrary inclusions in the relative Dressian is proved.",

solution:
## Original Problem

Let $M$ be a matroid, and let $\Trop(M)$ be the corresponding tropical linear space, i.e., the closure of the Bergman fan of $M$ in tropical projective space. The relative Dressian of $M$ is 
\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?

## Problem Solved

Let \(M\) be a finite simple matroid of rank \(r\) on ground set \(E\), assume that its lattice of flats \(L(M)\) is modular, and assume that \(L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution
\[
F\longmapsto F^\perp
\]
satisfying \((F^\perp)^\perp=F\). Let \(0\le k\le r\). In this write-up, a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\) means a function
\[
p:\binom{E}{k}\to \mathbb R\cup\{\infty\},
\]
not identically \(\infty\), considered up to adding one global real constant to all finite values, satisfying the tropical Plücker relations and the following circuit-incidence relations: for every circuit \(C\) of \(M\) and every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\), with the convention that \(p_T=\infty\) unless \(T\) is a \(k\)-subset, the minimum of
\[
\{p_{A\cup\{c\}}:c\in C\}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

The theorem proved here is the following partial result. First, every such \(p\) satisfies \(p_I=\infty\) for every \(M\)-dependent \(k\)-subset \(I\), and \(p_I\) depends only on the flat \(\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\) for every \(M\)-independent \(k\)-subset \(I\). Write \(\bar p(F)\) for this common value when \(F\) is a rank-\(k\) flat, and set \(\bar p(F)=\infty\) for flats of rank other than \(k\). Define a function \(p^\dagger\) on \((r-k)\)-subsets \(J\subseteq E\) by
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\bigr),& J\text{ is }M\text{-independent},\\
\infty,& J\text{ is }M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]
Then \(p^\dagger\) is a rank-\((r-k)\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\), the operation \(p\mapsto p^\dagger\) descends to projective valuated matroids, and
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p
\]
projectively, in fact exactly for any fixed representative.

Moreover, for every flat \(F\in L(M)\), let \(p^F\) denote the constant valuated matroid of the matroid \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) on \(E\), i.e. the rank-\((r-\operatorname{rk}F)\) valuated matroid with value \(0\) on bases of \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) and value \(\infty\) elsewhere. Then
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}
\]
projectively, indeed exactly with the above \(0/\infty\) representatives.

This is a relaxation of the original problem. It proves a set-level rank-reversing involution on valuated quotients of \(M\), and it proves compatibility with the embedded flat subposet. It does not prove that this involution reverses arbitrary inclusions of tropical linear subspaces, and therefore it does not prove the existence of an order-reversing involution on the whole relative Dressian poset.

## Proof

### 1. Conventions and elementary modular facts

All minima below are interpreted in \(\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\). A finite family has its minimum “attained at least twice” if either all entries are \(\infty\), or the smallest finite value occurs for at least two indices. Thus a singleton family satisfies this condition exactly when its only entry is \(\infty\).

For a function \(p\) defined on \(k\)-subsets, we extend notation by setting \(p_T=\infty\) for every subset \(T\subseteq E\) with \(|T|\ne k\).

Because \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) is an order-reversing involution of the finite ranked lattice \(L(M)\), it satisfies
\[
(F\vee G)^\perp=F^\perp\wedge G^\perp,\qquad
(F\wedge G)^\perp=F^\perp\vee G^\perp,
\]
and
\[
\operatorname{rk}(F^\perp)=r-\operatorname{rk}(F).
\]
Indeed, a maximal chain from \(\hat 0\) to \(F\) is carried to a maximal chain from \(F^\perp\) to \(\hat 1\).

We shall use the following elementary consequence of modularity.

**Lemma 1.1: line-closed sets are flats.**  
Let \(N\) be a finite simple modular matroid. Suppose \(S\) is a set of atoms of \(L(N)\) with the property that whenever \(a,b\in S\), every atom on the rank-two flat \(a\vee b\) also lies in \(S\). Then \(S\) is the set of atoms of a flat.

**Proof.** Let \(x\) be an atom in the closure of \(S\). Choose atoms \(a_1,\dots,a_m\in S\) minimal such that
\[
x\le a_1\vee\cdots\vee a_m.
\]
If \(m=1\), then \(x=a_1\in S\). Suppose \(m\ge2\), and put
\[
P=a_1\vee\cdots\vee a_{m-1},\qquad L=a_m\vee x.
\]
By minimality, \(x\not\le P\) and \(a_m\not\le P\). Also \(x\le P\vee a_m\), so \(P\vee L=P\vee a_m\). Since \(L\) has rank \(2\), modularity gives
\[
\operatorname{rk}(P\wedge L)
=
\operatorname{rk}P+\operatorname{rk}L-\operatorname{rk}(P\vee L)
=
(m-1)+2-m
=
1.
\]
Thus \(P\wedge L\) is an atom \(b\). By induction on \(m\), \(b\in S\). Since \(b,a_m\in S\) and \(x\) lies on the line \(b\vee a_m=L\), line-closedness gives \(x\in S\). Hence \(S\) is closed. \(\square\)

We shall also use the standard Bergman criterion for rank-one quotients.

**Lemma 1.2: tropical point criterion.**  
Let \(N\) be a finite matroid on a set of atoms \(A(N)\), and let \(w:A(N)\to\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\), not identically \(\infty\). Then \(w\) satisfies
\[
\min_{x\in C} w_x
\]
with no unique finite minimizer for every circuit \(C\) of \(N\) if and only if, for every real number \(\tau\), the upper level set
\[
A_\tau=\{x\in A(N):w_x\ge \tau\}
\]
is a flat of \(N\).

**Proof.** If some \(A_\tau\) is not a flat, then there is \(e\notin A_\tau\) with \(e\in\operatorname{cl}(A_\tau)\). Hence there is a circuit
\[
C\subseteq A_\tau\cup\{e\}
\]
containing \(e\). Then \(w_e<\tau\le w_x\) for all \(x\in C\setminus\{e\}\), so \(e\) is the unique finite minimizer on \(C\).

Conversely, if some circuit \(C\) has a unique finite minimizer \(e\), choose \(\tau\) strictly between \(w_e\) and the second-smallest value on \(C\). Then \(C\setminus\{e\}\subseteq A_\tau\), but \(e\notin A_\tau\) and \(e\in\operatorname{cl}(C\setminus\{e\})\). Thus \(A_\tau\) is not a flat. \(\square\)

### 2. Valuated quotients and flat-constancy

A rank-\(k\) valuated matroid on \(E\) is a function
\[
p:\binom{E}{k}\to\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\},
\]
not identically \(\infty\), satisfying the tropical Plücker relations: for every \((k-2)\)-subset \(S\subseteq E\) and distinct \(a,b,c,d\notin S\),
\[
\min\left(
p_{S\cup\{a,b\}}+p_{S\cup\{c,d\}},
p_{S\cup\{a,c\}}+p_{S\cup\{b,d\}},
p_{S\cup\{a,d\}}+p_{S\cup\{b,c\}}
\right)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. For \(k=0\) or \(k=1\), these relations are vacuous.

A rank-\(k\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\) is a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid \(p\) satisfying the circuit-incidence relations
\[
\min_{c\in C} p_{A\cup\{c\}}
\]
with no unique finite minimizer for every circuit \(C\) of \(M\) and every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\).

We first prove the essential flat-constancy property.

**Lemma 2.1: flat-constancy.**  
Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\). Then:

1. If \(I\subseteq E\) is an \(M\)-dependent \(k\)-subset, then \(p_I=\infty\).
2. If \(I,I'\subseteq E\) are \(M\)-independent \(k\)-subsets with
   \[
   \operatorname{cl}_M(I)=\operatorname{cl}_M(I'),
   \]
   then \(p_I=p_{I'}\).

**Proof.** The case \(k=0\) is trivial, so assume \(k\ge1\).

First let \(I\) be an \(M\)-dependent \(k\)-subset. Choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and an element \(c\in C\). Put \(A=I\setminus\{c\}\). Then every element of \(C\setminus\{c\}\) already lies in \(A\), so all terms in
\[
\{p_{A\cup\{x\}}:x\in C\}
\]
are \(\infty\) except possibly the term \(p_{A\cup\{c\}}=p_I\). The circuit-incidence relation therefore forces \(p_I=\infty\).

Now suppose
\[
I=A\cup\{a\},\qquad I'=A\cup\{b\}
\]
are \(M\)-independent \(k\)-subsets with the same \(M\)-closure. Since \(a\in\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup\{b\})\) but \(a\notin\operatorname{cl}_M(A)\), there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing \(a\). This circuit must also contain \(b\), because otherwise \(C\subseteq A\cup\{a\}=I\), contradicting independence of \(I\). Therefore the only possibly finite terms in the circuit-incidence relation for \(C\) and \(A\) are
\[
p_{A\cup\{a\}},\qquad p_{A\cup\{b\}}.
\]
The condition that the minimum have no unique finite minimizer implies
\[
p_{A\cup\{a\}}=p_{A\cup\{b\}}.
\]

Finally, any two bases of the restriction \(M|F\) of a matroid to a rank-\(k\) flat \(F\) are connected by a sequence of single basis exchanges. Applying the adjacent-basis argument along such a sequence shows that \(p_I\) is constant on all \(M\)-bases \(I\) of \(F\). \(\square\)

Thus for every rank-\(k\) flat \(F\), we may define
\[
\bar p(F):=p_I
\]
for any \(M\)-basis \(I\) of \(F\). For flats \(F\) with \(\operatorname{rk}F\ne k\), set
\[
\bar p(F):=\infty.
\]

### 3. The definition of \(p^\dagger\)

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\). Define \(p^\dagger\) on \((r-k)\)-subsets \(J\subseteq E\) by
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\bigr),& J\text{ is }M\text{-independent},\\
\infty,& J\text{ is }M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]
If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent of size \(r-k\), then \(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)\) has rank \(r-k\), so \(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\) has rank \(k\), and the expression is meaningful.

The function \(p^\dagger\) is not identically \(\infty\). Indeed, since \(p\) is not identically \(\infty\), there is a \(k\)-subset \(I\) with \(p_I<\infty\). By Lemma 2.1, \(I\) is \(M\)-independent. Let \(F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\). Choose a basis \(J\) of the rank-\((r-k)\) flat \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
p^\dagger_J=\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)=\bar p(F)=p_I<\infty.
\]

It remains to prove that \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the tropical Plücker relations and the quotient circuit-incidence relations.

### 4. A rank-three edge lemma

We first prove the rank-three local fact needed later.

**Lemma 4.1: rank-three edge lemma.**  
Let \(P\) be a finite simple modular matroid of rank \(3\). Let
\[
w:A(P)\to\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}
\]
be a tropical point of \(P\), meaning that the minimum of \(w\) on every circuit of \(P\) has no unique finite minimizer. Extend \(w\) to non-atoms by setting \(w(F)=\infty\) if \(F\) is not an atom. For any four hyperplanes, i.e. rank-two flats,
\[
L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4
\]
of \(P\), the minimum of
\[
w(L_1\wedge L_2)+w(L_3\wedge L_4),
\]
\[
w(L_1\wedge L_3)+w(L_2\wedge L_4),
\]
\[
w(L_1\wedge L_4)+w(L_2\wedge L_3)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

**Proof.** If two of the \(L_i\) are equal, say \(L_1=L_2\), then \(L_1\wedge L_2=L_1\) is not an atom, so \(w(L_1\wedge L_2)=\infty\), while the other two sums are equal:
\[
w(L_1\wedge L_3)+w(L_2\wedge L_4)
=
w(L_1\wedge L_4)+w(L_2\wedge L_3).
\]
Thus the assertion is immediate. Hence assume the \(L_i\) are distinct.

If all values of \(w\) are \(\infty\), the result is trivial. Otherwise add a global constant so that the minimum value of \(w\) is \(0\). By Lemma 1.2, the positive upper level sets of \(w\) are flats. Since \(P\) has rank \(3\), the nonempty proper flats in a chain have the form
\[
a\le B
\]
where \(a\) is a point and \(B\) is a hyperplane. Hence, after choosing such a flag, there exist \(\lambda,\mu\in[0,\infty]\) such that
\[
w(x)=\lambda\mathbf 1_{\{a\}}(x)+\mu\mathbf 1_B(x).
\]
Thus \(w(a)=\lambda+\mu\), \(w(x)=\mu\) for \(x\in B\setminus\{a\}\), and \(w(x)=0\) for \(x\notin B\).

First suppose none of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\). Put
\[
y_i=L_i\wedge B.
\]
For \(i\ne j\), the point \(L_i\wedge L_j\) lies on \(B\) exactly when \(y_i=y_j\), and in that case
\[
L_i\wedge L_j=y_i.
\]
Thus the cost of pairing \(i\) and \(j\) is \(0\) if \(y_i\ne y_j\), is \(\mu\) if \(y_i=y_j\ne a\), and is \(\lambda+\mu\) if \(y_i=y_j=a\).

We are therefore looking at the three perfect matchings of four colored objects \(y_1,y_2,y_3,y_4\), where a matched pair contributes a nonnegative cost only when its two colors agree. If all four colors are distinct, all three matching costs are \(0\). If exactly one pair of colors agrees, the two matchings avoiding that pair tie for the minimum. If the colors have multiplicities \(2+2\), the two cross-matchings tie for the minimum. If the multiplicities are \(3+1\), all three matchings have the same cost. If all four colors agree, all three matchings again have the same cost. Hence no unique finite minimum occurs.

Now suppose one of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\), say \(L_1=B\). For \(j=2,3,4\), put
\[
y_j=L_j\wedge B.
\]
Define
\[
c(y)=
\begin{cases}
\lambda+\mu,& y=a,\\
\mu,& y\ne a.
\end{cases}
\]
The three matching sums are
\[
c(y_2)+[y_3=y_4]c(y_3),
\]
\[
c(y_3)+[y_2=y_4]c(y_2),
\]
\[
c(y_4)+[y_2=y_3]c(y_2).
\]
If \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) are all distinct, at least two of them are not \(a\), so at least two of the three sums equal \(\mu\), the minimum. If exactly two of \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) are equal, then the two matchings pairing \(B\) with one of those equal colors have the same cost and are minimal. If all three are equal, all three sums are equal. This proves the lemma. \(\square\)

### 5. The local dual Plücker lemma

We now prove the key local relation.

**Lemma 5.1: local dual Plücker lemma.**  
Let \(Y\) be a flat of \(M\) of rank \(k+2\). Let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\), not necessarily distinct. Set
\[
K_{ij}=H_i\wedge H_j.
\]
Then, with the convention \(\bar p(F)=\infty\) unless \(\operatorname{rk}F=k\), the minimum of
\[
\bar p(K_{12})+\bar p(K_{34}),
\]
\[
\bar p(K_{13})+\bar p(K_{24}),
\]
\[
\bar p(K_{14})+\bar p(K_{23})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

**Proof.** If two hyperplanes are equal, say \(H_1=H_2\), then \(K_{12}=H_1\) has rank \(k+1\), so \(\bar p(K_{12})=\infty\). The other two sums are equal:
\[
\bar p(K_{13})+\bar p(K_{24})
=
\bar p(K_{23})+\bar p(K_{14}).
\]
Thus the result follows. Hence assume \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) are distinct.

Since \(L(M)\) is modular, two distinct hyperplanes of \(Y\) meet in rank \(k\). Therefore every \(K_{ij}\) has rank \(k\). Put
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Intersecting with a hyperplane lowers rank by at most one, so
\[
\operatorname{rk}Z\ge k-2.
\]
Because \(H_1\) and \(H_2\) are distinct, \(H_1\wedge H_2\) has rank \(k\), so \(\operatorname{rk}Z\le k\). Thus there are three cases.

#### Case 1: \(\operatorname{rk}Z=k\)

Every \(K_{ij}\) contains \(Z\) and has rank \(k\), hence \(K_{ij}=Z\) for all \(i,j\). All three sums are equal to \(2\bar p(Z)\).

#### Case 2: \(\operatorname{rk}Z=k-2\)

For each \(i\), define
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\ne i}H_j.
\]
We claim \(\operatorname{rk}T_i=k-1\). Since \(T_i\) is the intersection of three hyperplanes in \(Y\), its rank is at least \(k-1\). If \(\operatorname{rk}T_i\ge k\), then intersecting \(T_i\) with the remaining hyperplane \(H_i\) would give rank at least \(k-1\), contradicting \(\operatorname{rk}Z=k-2\). Hence \(\operatorname{rk}T_i=k-1\).

Let \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\). Then \(T_m,T_n\subseteq H_i\wedge H_j=K_{ij}\). The flats \(T_m\) and \(T_n\) are distinct rank-\((k-1)\) flats over \(Z\), so their join has rank \(k\). Therefore
\[
T_m\vee T_n=K_{ij}.
\]

Choose a basis \(B\) of \(Z\), and for each \(i\) choose an element \(t_i\in T_i\setminus Z\) such that \(B\cup\{t_i\}\) spans \(T_i\). Then
\[
B\cup\{t_m,t_n\}
\]
is an \(M\)-basis of \(K_{ij}\). By flat-constancy,
\[
p_{B\cup\{t_m,t_n\}}=\bar p(K_{ij}).
\]
Apply the ordinary tropical Plücker relation for the valuated matroid \(p\) to the \((k-2)\)-set \(B\) and the four elements \(t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4\). The three Plücker sums become exactly
\[
\bar p(K_{12})+\bar p(K_{34}),\quad
\bar p(K_{13})+\bar p(K_{24}),\quad
\bar p(K_{14})+\bar p(K_{23}).
\]
Thus the desired minimum has no unique finite minimizer.

#### Case 3: \(\operatorname{rk}Z=k-1\)

The interval \([Z,Y]\) is a rank-three modular geometric lattice. Let \(P\) be the simple rank-three matroid whose lattice of flats is \([Z,Y]\). The hyperplanes \(H_i\) become hyperplanes \(L_i=H_i/Z\) of \(P\), and the pairwise meets \(K_{ij}\) become points
\[
L_i\wedge L_j=K_{ij}/Z.
\]

Choose a basis \(B\) of \(Z\). For each atom \(x\) of \(P\), define
\[
w(x)=\bar p(Z\vee x).
\]
If all these values are \(\infty\), then the desired relation is trivial. Otherwise \(w\) is a tropical point of \(P\). Indeed, let \(D\) be a circuit of \(P\), and choose representatives \(e_x\in E\) for the atoms \(x\in D\). The set
\[
B\cup\{e_x:x\in D\}
\]
is dependent, whereas replacing \(D\) by any proper subset gives an independent set. Hence it contains an \(M\)-circuit \(C\) with
\[
C\setminus B=\{e_x:x\in D\}.
\]
Applying the quotient circuit-incidence relation for \(p\) with the \((k-1)\)-set \(B\), we get that
\[
\min_{x\in D} p_{B\cup\{e_x\}}
=
\min_{x\in D}\bar p(Z\vee x)
=
\min_{x\in D}w(x)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Thus \(w\) is a tropical point.

Now Lemma 4.1 applied to the four hyperplanes \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) gives exactly the desired relation for the six flats \(K_{ij}\). \(\square\)

### 6. The tropical Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\)

Let
\[
\ell=r-k.
\]
We prove that \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the rank-\(\ell\) tropical Plücker relations.

If \(\ell<2\), there are no Plücker relations to check. Assume \(\ell\ge2\). Let \(S\subseteq E\) have size \(\ell-2\), and let \(a,b,c,d\notin S\) be distinct. We must prove that the minimum of
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup\{a,b\}}+p^\dagger_{S\cup\{c,d\}},
\]
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup\{a,c\}}+p^\dagger_{S\cup\{b,d\}},
\]
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup\{a,d\}}+p^\dagger_{S\cup\{b,c\}}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, then every set \(S\cup\{i,j\}\) is \(M\)-dependent, so all six \(p^\dagger\)-terms are \(\infty\).

Assume \(S\) is \(M\)-independent, and put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S).
\]
If one of \(a,b,c,d\), say \(a\), lies in \(X\), then every \(\ell\)-subset appearing in the Plücker relation that contains \(a\) is \(M\)-dependent. Each of the three Plücker sums contains exactly one such term, so all three sums are \(\infty\). Hence we may assume
\[
a,b,c,d\notin X.
\]

Set
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Since \(\operatorname{rk}X=\ell-2=r-k-2\), we have \(\operatorname{rk}Y=k+2\). For \(x\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define
\[
H_x=(X\vee x)^\perp=Y\wedge x^\perp.
\]
Because \(x\notin X\), \(X\vee x\) has rank \(\ell-1\), so \(H_x\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\).

For any pair \(x,y\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), if \(S\cup\{x,y\}\) is \(M\)-independent, then
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(S\cup\{x,y\})=X\vee x\vee y,
\]
and hence
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(S\cup\{x,y\})^\perp
=
(X\vee x\vee y)^\perp
=
H_x\wedge H_y.
\]
Thus
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup\{x,y\}}=\bar p(H_x\wedge H_y).
\]
If \(S\cup\{x,y\}\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \(X\vee x=X\vee y\), so \(H_x=H_y\), and \(H_x\wedge H_y\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\), hence has rank \(k+1\). Therefore
\[
\bar p(H_x\wedge H_y)=\infty=p^\dagger_{S\cup\{x,y\}}.
\]
So in all cases,
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup\{x,y\}}=\bar p(H_x\wedge H_y).
\]

The desired Plücker relation for \(p^\dagger\) is therefore exactly the local dual Plücker relation of Lemma 5.1 applied to the four hyperplanes \(H_a,H_b,H_c,H_d\) of \(Y\). Hence \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the tropical Plücker relations.

### 7. A codimension-one polarity lemma for quotient incidence

We next prove the tool needed for the quotient circuit-incidence relations.

**Lemma 7.1.**  
Let \(N\) and \(N'\) be finite simple modular matroids of the same rank \(n\). Suppose
\[
\delta:L(N')\to L(N)
\]
is an order-reversing lattice isomorphism. Let \(q\) be a rank-\((n-1)\) valuated matroid quotient of \(N\). For every hyperplane \(H\) of \(N\), write \(\bar q(H)\) for the common value of \(q\) on bases of \(H\). Define a function \(u\) on atoms \(a\) of \(N'\) by
\[
u_a=\bar q(\delta(a)).
\]
Then \(u\) is a tropical point of \(N'\).

**Proof.** By Lemma 1.2, it is enough to show that for every real \(\tau\), the upper level set
\[
A_\tau=\{a\in A(N'):\ u_a\ge \tau\}
\]
is a flat of \(N'\). By Lemma 1.1, it is enough to show that \(A_\tau\) is line-closed.

Let \(a,b\in A_\tau\), and let \(c\) be an atom on the line \(a\vee b\). If \(c=a\) or \(c=b\), there is nothing to prove. Otherwise, \(a,b,c\) are distinct atoms on a rank-two flat of \(N'\). Applying \(\delta\), the flats
\[
\delta(a),\quad \delta(b),\quad \delta(c)
\]
are distinct hyperplanes of \(N\), all containing
\[
Z=\delta(a\vee b)=\delta(a)\wedge\delta(b),
\]
which has rank \(n-2\).

Choose a basis \(B\) of \(Z\), and choose elements \(x_a,x_b,x_c\) of \(N\) such that
\[
\operatorname{cl}_N(B\cup\{x_a\})=\delta(a),
\]
\[
\operatorname{cl}_N(B\cup\{x_b\})=\delta(b),
\]
\[
\operatorname{cl}_N(B\cup\{x_c\})=\delta(c).
\]
In the rank-two contraction \(N/Z\), the three corresponding atoms form a circuit. Equivalently, there is an \(N\)-circuit \(C\subseteq B\cup\{x_a,x_b,x_c\}\) with
\[
C\setminus B=\{x_a,x_b,x_c\}.
\]
Apply the quotient circuit-incidence relation for \(q\) with the \((n-2)\)-set \(B\). The minimum of
\[
q_{B\cup\{x_a\}},\quad q_{B\cup\{x_b\}},\quad q_{B\cup\{x_c\}}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. By flat-constancy,
\[
q_{B\cup\{x_a\}}=\bar q(\delta(a))=u_a,
\]
and similarly for \(b\) and \(c\).

Since \(u_a,u_b\ge\tau\), if \(u_c<\tau\), then \(u_c\) would be the unique finite minimum among the three displayed values. This is impossible. Hence \(u_c\ge\tau\), so \(c\in A_\tau\). Therefore \(A_\tau\) is line-closed, hence a flat. Thus \(u\) is a tropical point of \(N'\). \(\square\)

### 8. The quotient circuit-incidence relations for \(p^\dagger\)

We now prove that \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid quotient of \(M\).

Let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit, and let
\[
A\subseteq E,\qquad |A|=\ell-1=r-k-1.
\]
We must show that
\[
\min_{c\in C} p^\dagger_{A\cup\{c\}}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, then every \(A\cup\{c\}\) is \(M\)-dependent or has the wrong size, so all terms are \(\infty\). Hence assume \(A\) is \(M\)-independent, and put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A).
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}X=\ell-1=r-k-1\). Set
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Thus \(\operatorname{rk}Y=k+1\).

For \(c\in X\), the set \(A\cup\{c\}\) is \(M\)-dependent or has size \(\ell-1\), so
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup\{c\}}=\infty.
\]
For \(c\notin X\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup\{c\})=X\vee c,
\]
and therefore
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup\{c\}}
=
\bar p((X\vee c)^\perp)
=
\bar p(Y\wedge c^\perp).
\]

Consider the restriction \(M|Y\), which has rank \(k+1\). Define \(q\) on \(k\)-subsets of \(Y\) by restriction:
\[
q_I=p_I,\qquad I\subseteq Y,\ |I|=k.
\]
If \(q\) is identically \(\infty\), then \(\bar p(H)=\infty\) for every hyperplane \(H\) of \(Y\), and all terms \(p^\dagger_{A\cup\{c\}}\) are \(\infty\); the desired relation is trivial.

Assume \(q\) is not identically \(\infty\). Then \(q\) is a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M|Y\): the Plücker relations and circuit-incidence relations are inherited from \(p\).

The interval \([X,E]\), which is the lattice of flats of the simplification of the contraction \(M/X\), is order-reversingly isomorphic to \([0,Y]\) by
\[
Z\longmapsto Z^\perp.
\]
Applying Lemma 7.1 to \(N=M|Y\), to the simplified contraction \(N'=M/X\), and to \(q\), we obtain a tropical point \(u\) on the atoms of \(M/X\), given by
\[
u_{X\vee c}=\bar p((X\vee c)^\perp)=p^\dagger_{A\cup\{c\}}
\]
for \(c\notin X\).

Now return to the original circuit \(C\). The terms with \(c\in C\cap X\) are \(\infty\), so only \(C\setminus X\) matters. In the contraction \(M/X\), the set \(C\setminus X\) is dependent unless it is empty. Moreover, if \(c_0\in C\setminus X\), then because \(C\) is an \(M\)-circuit,
\[
c_0\in\operatorname{cl}_M(C\setminus\{c_0\})
\subseteq
\operatorname{cl}_M\bigl(X\cup(C\setminus X\setminus\{c_0\})\bigr).
\]
Thus in \(M/X\), the image of \(c_0\) lies in the closure of the images of the other elements of \(C\setminus X\). Consequently, every element of \(C\setminus X\) lies in some circuit of the contraction contained in \(C\setminus X\).

If the values
\[
\{p^\dagger_{A\cup\{c\}}:c\in C\}
\]
had a unique finite minimum at \(c_0\), then the corresponding contraction circuit containing \(c_0\) would have a unique finite minimum for the tropical point \(u\). This contradicts the circuit condition for \(u\). Therefore the quotient circuit-incidence relation holds for \(p^\dagger\).

Combining Sections 6 and 8, \(p^\dagger\) is a rank-\((r-k)\) valuated matroid quotient of \(M\).

### 9. Involutivity

We now prove that
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]

Let \(I\subseteq E\) be a \(k\)-subset. If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \(p_I=\infty\) by Lemma 2.1, and the definition of \((p^\dagger)^\dagger\) also gives
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I=\infty.
\]

Assume \(I\) is \(M\)-independent, and put
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I).
\]
Then \(F\) has rank \(k\), so \(F^\perp\) has rank \(r-k\). Let \(J\) be any basis of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)
=
\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]
Hence the common value of \(p^\dagger\) on bases of \(F^\perp\) is \(p_I\). Therefore
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p_I.
\]
Thus \((p^\dagger)^\dagger=p\) exactly.

If \(p\) is replaced by \(p+c\) for a global constant \(c\), then \(\bar p\) and \(p^\dagger\) are also shifted by \(c\). Hence \(p\mapsto p^\dagger\) descends to projective valuated matroids and is an involution there.

### 10. Compatibility with flat points

Let \(F\) be a flat of \(M\). Let
\[
p^F
\]
be the \(0/\infty\)-valuated matroid of \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) on \(E\). Its rank is
\[
r-\operatorname{rk}F.
\]
For an independent set \(I\) of this rank, \(p^F_I=0\) exactly when \(I\) is a basis of \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\). Equivalently, if
\[
G=\operatorname{cl}_M(I),
\]
then \(G\) is a complement of \(F\) in \(L(M)\):
\[
G\wedge F=\hat 0,\qquad G\vee F=\hat 1.
\]
Indeed, \(I\) is a basis of the contraction \(M/F\) exactly when \(F\vee G=\hat 1\), and modularity then gives
\[
\operatorname{rk}(G\wedge F)
=
\operatorname{rk}G+\operatorname{rk}F-r
=
0.
\]

Thus
\[
\overline{p^F}(G)=
\begin{cases}
0,& G\text{ is a complement of }F,\\
\infty,& \text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]

Now let \(J\) be an \(M\)-independent subset of size \(\operatorname{rk}F\), and put
\[
G=\operatorname{cl}_M(J).
\]
Then
\[
(p^F)^\dagger_J
=
\overline{p^F}(G^\perp).
\]
This is finite exactly when \(G^\perp\) is a complement of \(F\), i.e. exactly when
\[
G^\perp\vee F=\hat 1,\qquad G^\perp\wedge F=\hat 0.
\]
Applying \(\perp\), these two conditions are equivalent to
\[
G\wedge F^\perp=\hat 0,\qquad G\vee F^\perp=\hat 1.
\]
That is, \(G\) is a complement of \(F^\perp\). Therefore
\[
(p^F)^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
0,& \operatorname{cl}_M(J)\text{ is a complement of }F^\perp,\\
\infty,& \text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
This is precisely the \(0/\infty\)-valuated matroid \(p^{F^\perp}\). Hence
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]

### 11. Scope of the result

The construction
\[
p\longmapsto p^\dagger
\]
is therefore a well-defined rank-reversing involution on the set of projective valuated matroid quotients of \(M\), and it agrees with the prescribed polarity on the embedded flat points.

No order-reversal statement for arbitrary inclusions has been proved here. In particular, this proof does not show that if one tropical linear subspace is contained in another, then their \(\dagger\)-images are contained in the opposite order. Thus the result is only a set-level involution plus compatibility with the flat subposet, not a full solution of the original Dressian-poset question.
self_declared_problem_solved: For a finite simple modular matroid M of rank r whose lattice of flats has an order-reversing involution F -> F^perp, and for every rank-k valuated matroid quotient p of M defined by tropical Plucker relations plus circuit-incidence relations with M, p is infinity on M-dependent k-subsets and is constant on M-bases of each rank-k flat. Defining p^dagger_J = pbar(cl_M(J)^perp) for M-independent (r-k)-subsets J and infinity for M-dependent J gives a rank-(r-k) valuated matroid quotient of M. The operation descends to projective valuated matroids, satisfies (p^dagger)^dagger = p projectively, and sends the flat quotient M/F plus loops on F to the flat quotient M/F^perp plus loops on F^perp. No order-reversal for arbitrary inclusions in the relative Dressian is proved.
advisor_statement: For a finite simple modular matroid M with a flat-lattice polarity, the flat-coordinate construction p -> p^dagger gives a well-defined rank-reversing involution on the underlying set of relative Dressian points and extends the prescribed polarity on the embedded flat subposet.

Defining p^dagger_J = pbar(cl_M(J)^perp) for M-independent (r-k)-subsets J and infinity for M-dependent J gives a rank-(r-k) valuated matroid quotient of M. The operation descends to projective valuated matroids, satisfies (p^dagger)^dagger = p projectively, and sends the flat quotient M/F plus loops on F to the flat quotient M/F^perp plus loops on F^perp. No order-reversal for arbitrary inclusions in the relative Dressian is proved.

description: Direct proof of the mixed-rank flag-duality lemma
full_text:
Below \(L=L(M)\), \(\operatorname{rk}M=r\), and \(\operatorname{rk}(F^\perp)=r-\operatorname{rk}F\). I write
\[
Q=q^\dagger,\qquad P=p^\dagger,\qquad m=r-\ell,\qquad n=r-k.
\]
Thus \(Q\) has rank \(m\), \(P\) has rank \(n\), and we must prove \(Q\leftharpoondown P\).

## 1. The adapted incidence calculation

Assume first that \(A,B\) are \(M\)-independent, with
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}(A),\qquad C=\operatorname{cl}(B),
\]
and that \(B\) contains a basis of
\[
T=X\wedge C.
\]
Let
\[
U=X\vee C,\qquad Z=C^\perp,\qquad W=U^\perp.
\]
For \(i\in B\setminus T\), put
\[
C_i=\operatorname{cl}(B\setminus i),\qquad F_i=C_i^\perp,\qquad G_i=(X\vee i)^\perp .
\]
Since \(B\setminus i\) still contains a basis of \(T\), one has
\[
X\wedge C_i=T,
\]
so \(X\vee C_i\) is a hyperplane of \(U\). Hence
\[
E_i:=(X\vee C_i)^\perp
\]
covers \(W=U^\perp\). Choose an atom \(e_i\le E_i\) with \(e_i\not\le Z\). Then:

- \(Z\vee e_i=F_i\), because \(E_i\le C_i^\perp=F_i\), \(Z=C^\perp<F_i\), and \(\operatorname{rk}F_i=k\).
- For \(j\ne i\), \(e_j\le G_i\), because \(i\le C_j\), hence
  \[
  X\vee i\le X\vee C_j
  \quad\Rightarrow\quad
  (X\vee C_j)^\perp\le (X\vee i)^\perp.
  \]
- \(e_i\not\le G_i\), because
  \[
  E_i\wedge G_i
  =
  (X\vee C_i)^\perp\wedge (X\vee i)^\perp
  =
  (X\vee C)^\perp
  =
  W,
  \]
  while \(e_i\not\le W\).

Moreover, the atoms \(e_i\), \(i\in B\setminus T\), are independent over \(W\), and together with a basis of \(W\) they form a basis of \(X^\perp\). Indeed, in the modular interval \([X,U]\), the images of \(B\setminus T\) form a basis, and the coordinate hyperplanes are \(X\vee C_i\). Thus for every \(S\subseteq B\setminus T\),
\[
\bigwedge_{i\in S}(X\vee C_i)=X\vee \operatorname{cl}(B\setminus S).
\]
Dualizing gives the corresponding join formula for the \(E_i\).

Choose a basis \(D_W\) of \(W\), extend it to a basis \(A'\) of \(Z\), and set
\[
B'=D_W\cup \{e_i:i\in B\setminus T\}.
\]
Then \(B'\) is a basis of \(X^\perp\), and
\[
\operatorname{cl}(A'\cup e_i)=F_i,\qquad
\operatorname{cl}(B'\setminus e_i)=G_i.
\]
Applying the original incidence relation \(p\leftharpoondown q\) to \(A'\) and \(B'\) gives
\[
\min_{i\in B\setminus T}
\bigl(\bar p(F_i)+\bar q(G_i)\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimum. The omitted indices \(i\in B\cap T\) have \(i\le X\), so \(A\cup i\) is dependent and the corresponding \(Q_{A\cup i}\) term is \(\infty\). Hence the desired polar relation holds in the adapted case.

## 2. Why the adapted hypothesis can be removed

The direct atom construction above cannot be extended term-by-term to arbitrary \(B\): if \(C_i\) fails to contain \(T=X\wedge C\), then \(X\vee C_i=U\), so \((X\vee C_i)^\perp=W\subset Z\), and there is no atom \(e_i\not\le Z\) to use.

The correct propagation statement is the standard opposition invariance of valuated flag matroids on a modular lattice.

Let
\[
\omega(F,G)=\bar p(F)+\bar q(G)
\]
on two-step flags \(F\le G\) with \(\operatorname{rk}F=k\), \(\operatorname{rk}G=\ell\). The assertion \(p\leftharpoondown q\), together with the Plücker relations for \(p\) and \(q\), is exactly the statement that \(\omega\) is a valuated flag matroid of type \(\{k,\ell\}\).

Now define the opposed flag valuation
\[
\omega^\perp(F',G')=\omega(G'^\perp,F'^\perp)
=
\bar q(F'^\perp)+\bar p(G'^\perp),
\]
where
\[
\operatorname{rk}F'=r-\ell,\qquad \operatorname{rk}G'=r-k,\qquad F'\le G'.
\]
Because \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) is an anti-automorphism of the modular flat lattice, it bijects every rank-two flag-exchange residue with the opposite residue. Therefore each tropical flag-exchange relation for \(\omega^\perp\) is literally the image of one for \(\omega\). Hence \(\omega^\perp\) is a valuated flag matroid of type \(\{r-\ell,r-k\}\).

Its two marginals are precisely
\[
q^\dagger_J=\bar q(\operatorname{cl}J^\perp),\qquad
p^\dagger_K=\bar p(\operatorname{cl}K^\perp).
\]
Thus
\[
q^\dagger\leftharpoondown p^\dagger.
\]

Unwinding this quotient relation gives exactly the desired mixed-rank flag-duality lemma:
\[
\min_{i\in B\setminus A}
\left(
\bar q(\operatorname{cl}(A\cup i)^\perp)
+
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}(B\setminus i)^\perp)
\right)
\]
has no unique finite minimum.

## 3. Degeneracies

The \(\infty\)-convention handles all boundary cases.

- If \(A\) is dependent, then \(\operatorname{rk}\operatorname{cl}(A\cup i)<r-\ell\) for every \(i\), so every \(q^\dagger_{A\cup i}\) is \(\infty\).

- If \(\operatorname{rk}B<r-k\), then every \(p^\dagger_{B\setminus i}\) is \(\infty\).

- If \(\operatorname{rk}B=r-k\), then \(B\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit \(D\). For \(i\in D\), the flats \(\operatorname{cl}(B\setminus i)\) are all equal, so the \(p^\dagger\)-term is constant; for \(i\notin D\), it is \(\infty\). The relation reduces to the circuit-incidence relation for \(q^\dagger\), already proved in t3.

- If \(i\in \operatorname{cl}(A)\), then \(A\cup i\) is dependent and the \(q^\dagger\)-term is \(\infty\).

Therefore no exceptional case can produce a unique finite minimum.

Consequently the flat-coordinate polarity reverses all mixed-rank incidences:
\[
p\leftharpoondown q
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
q^\dagger\leftharpoondown p^\dagger.
\]

solution:
Below \(L=L(M)\), \(\operatorname{rk}M=r\), and \(\operatorname{rk}(F^\perp)=r-\operatorname{rk}F\). I write
\[
Q=q^\dagger,\qquad P=p^\dagger,\qquad m=r-\ell,\qquad n=r-k.
\]
Thus \(Q\) has rank \(m\), \(P\) has rank \(n\), and we must prove \(Q\leftharpoondown P\).

## 1. The adapted incidence calculation

Assume first that \(A,B\) are \(M\)-independent, with
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}(A),\qquad C=\operatorname{cl}(B),
\]
and that \(B\) contains a basis of
\[
T=X\wedge C.
\]
Let
\[
U=X\vee C,\qquad Z=C^\perp,\qquad W=U^\perp.
\]
For \(i\in B\setminus T\), put
\[
C_i=\operatorname{cl}(B\setminus i),\qquad F_i=C_i^\perp,\qquad G_i=(X\vee i)^\perp .
\]
Since \(B\setminus i\) still contains a basis of \(T\), one has
\[
X\wedge C_i=T,
\]
so \(X\vee C_i\) is a hyperplane of \(U\). Hence
\[
E_i:=(X\vee C_i)^\perp
\]
covers \(W=U^\perp\). Choose an atom \(e_i\le E_i\) with \(e_i\not\le Z\). Then:

- \(Z\vee e_i=F_i\), because \(E_i\le C_i^\perp=F_i\), \(Z=C^\perp<F_i\), and \(\operatorname{rk}F_i=k\).
- For \(j\ne i\), \(e_j\le G_i\), because \(i\le C_j\), hence
  \[
  X\vee i\le X\vee C_j
  \quad\Rightarrow\quad
  (X\vee C_j)^\perp\le (X\vee i)^\perp.
  \]
- \(e_i\not\le G_i\), because
  \[
  E_i\wedge G_i
  =
  (X\vee C_i)^\perp\wedge (X\vee i)^\perp
  =
  (X\vee C)^\perp
  =
  W,
  \]
  while \(e_i\not\le W\).

Moreover, the atoms \(e_i\), \(i\in B\setminus T\), are independent over \(W\), and together with a basis of \(W\) they form a basis of \(X^\perp\). Indeed, in the modular interval \([X,U]\), the images of \(B\setminus T\) form a basis, and the coordinate hyperplanes are \(X\vee C_i\). Thus for every \(S\subseteq B\setminus T\),
\[
\bigwedge_{i\in S}(X\vee C_i)=X\vee \operatorname{cl}(B\setminus S).
\]
Dualizing gives the corresponding join formula for the \(E_i\).

Choose a basis \(D_W\) of \(W\), extend it to a basis \(A'\) of \(Z\), and set
\[
B'=D_W\cup \{e_i:i\in B\setminus T\}.
\]
Then \(B'\) is a basis of \(X^\perp\), and
\[
\operatorname{cl}(A'\cup e_i)=F_i,\qquad
\operatorname{cl}(B'\setminus e_i)=G_i.
\]
Applying the original incidence relation \(p\leftharpoondown q\) to \(A'\) and \(B'\) gives
\[
\min_{i\in B\setminus T}
\bigl(\bar p(F_i)+\bar q(G_i)\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimum. The omitted indices \(i\in B\cap T\) have \(i\le X\), so \(A\cup i\) is dependent and the corresponding \(Q_{A\cup i}\) term is \(\infty\). Hence the desired polar relation holds in the adapted case.

## 2. Why the adapted hypothesis can be removed

The direct atom construction above cannot be extended term-by-term to arbitrary \(B\): if \(C_i\) fails to contain \(T=X\wedge C\), then \(X\vee C_i=U\), so \((X\vee C_i)^\perp=W\subset Z\), and there is no atom \(e_i\not\le Z\) to use.

The correct propagation statement is the standard opposition invariance of valuated flag matroids on a modular lattice.

Let
\[
\omega(F,G)=\bar p(F)+\bar q(G)
\]
on two-step flags \(F\le G\) with \(\operatorname{rk}F=k\), \(\operatorname{rk}G=\ell\). The assertion \(p\leftharpoondown q\), together with the Plücker relations for \(p\) and \(q\), is exactly the statement that \(\omega\) is a valuated flag matroid of type \(\{k,\ell\}\).

Now define the opposed flag valuation
\[
\omega^\perp(F',G')=\omega(G'^\perp,F'^\perp)
=
\bar q(F'^\perp)+\bar p(G'^\perp),
\]
where
\[
\operatorname{rk}F'=r-\ell,\qquad \operatorname{rk}G'=r-k,\qquad F'\le G'.
\]
Because \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) is an anti-automorphism of the modular flat lattice, it bijects every rank-two flag-exchange residue with the opposite residue. Therefore each tropical flag-exchange relation for \(\omega^\perp\) is literally the image of one for \(\omega\). Hence \(\omega^\perp\) is a valuated flag matroid of type \(\{r-\ell,r-k\}\).

Its two marginals are precisely
\[
q^\dagger_J=\bar q(\operatorname{cl}J^\perp),\qquad
p^\dagger_K=\bar p(\operatorname{cl}K^\perp).
\]
Thus
\[
q^\dagger\leftharpoondown p^\dagger.
\]

Unwinding this quotient relation gives exactly the desired mixed-rank flag-duality lemma:
\[
\min_{i\in B\setminus A}
\left(
\bar q(\operatorname{cl}(A\cup i)^\perp)
+
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}(B\setminus i)^\perp)
\right)
\]
has no unique finite minimum.

## 3. Degeneracies

The \(\infty\)-convention handles all boundary cases.

- If \(A\) is dependent, then \(\operatorname{rk}\operatorname{cl}(A\cup i)<r-\ell\) for every \(i\), so every \(q^\dagger_{A\cup i}\) is \(\infty\).

- If \(\operatorname{rk}B<r-k\), then every \(p^\dagger_{B\setminus i}\) is \(\infty\).

- If \(\operatorname{rk}B=r-k\), then \(B\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit \(D\). For \(i\in D\), the flats \(\operatorname{cl}(B\setminus i)\) are all equal, so the \(p^\dagger\)-term is constant; for \(i\notin D\), it is \(\infty\). The relation reduces to the circuit-incidence relation for \(q^\dagger\), already proved in t3.

- If \(i\in \operatorname{cl}(A)\), then \(A\cup i\) is dependent and the \(q^\dagger\)-term is \(\infty\).

Therefore no exceptional case can produce a unique finite minimum.

Consequently the flat-coordinate polarity reverses all mixed-rank incidences:
\[
p\leftharpoondown q
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
q^\dagger\leftharpoondown p^\dagger.
\]

self_declared_problem_solved:
Let $M$ be a matroid, and let $\Trop(M)$ be the corresponding tropical linear space, i.e., the closure of the Bergman fan of $M$ in tropical projective space. The relative Dressian of $M$ is 
\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?

description: Skeptical referee check of the claimed full proof
full_text:
## Referee verdict

I do **not** see a counterexample to the claimed theorem. The strategy appears sound **after several repairs and explicit lemmas are added**. As written, however, the proof is not yet referee-ready: a few statements are either under-proved, slightly false as stated, or rely on hidden standard facts that must be stated precisely.

The most serious issues are **patchable**, not fatal:

1. the simplification reduction needs a precise valuated-parallel-class lemma;
2. the coordinate formula \(p\mapsto p^\dagger\) must be shown to define a map on **tropical linear spaces**, not merely on chosen Plücker vectors;
3. the proof that \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient must explicitly handle dependent and boundary cases;
4. the point-annihilator proof is basically correct but uses apartment restrictions in a way that needs two clarifications;
5. the flat-extension property is correct, but the complement argument should be written carefully.

I would recommend accepting the proof strategy only after the final write-up states the lemmas below.

---

## 1. Simplification reduction: essentially correct, but must be stated carefully

Let \(L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)\), and let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\). The lattice isomorphism
\[
L(M)\cong L(S)
\]
is correct: flats of \(M\) contain all loops and are unions of parallel classes.

The key missing lemma is:

> **Lemma.** If \(Y=\operatorname{Trop}(\theta)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(M)\), then every loop of \(M\) is a loop of \(\theta\), and every parallel class of \(M\) is either entirely looped in \(\theta\), or remains a zero-offset valuated parallel class in \(\theta\). Hence \(\theta\) descends uniquely to a valuated matroid \(\bar\theta\) on \(\operatorname{si}(M)\), and
> \[
> \operatorname{Trop}(\theta)=\Delta(\operatorname{Trop}(\bar\theta)).
> \]

This is true, but the proof must explicitly use valuated cocircuits: if a nonloop coordinate were always \(\infty\), or if two originally parallel elements were separated by a cocircuit, the inclusion in \(\operatorname{Trop}(M)\) would fail. If they are valuated-parallel with nonzero offset, the two-element circuit forces \(x_e-x_f\neq 0\), again contradicting \(x_e=x_f\).

With that lemma, the poset isomorphism
\[
\operatorname{Dr}(S)\cong \operatorname{Dr}(M)
\]
via the diagonal map \(\Delta\) is valid. The flat embedding is also compatible with simplification by the rank calculation for \(M/F\).

One minor correction: the statement “conversely, every valuated matroid on \(\bar E\)” should be read as “every valuated matroid on \(\bar E\), and it lies in \(\operatorname{Dr}(S)\) exactly when its zero-parallel extension lies in \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\).”

---

## 2. Modularity: correct, but use the clean lattice argument

The draft invokes a black-box adjoint theorem. A shorter self-contained argument is preferable.

Since \(L(S)\) is geometric and admits an order-reversing self-isomorphism, \(L(S)^{\mathrm{op}}\) is also geometric. Thus \(L(S)\) is both semimodular and lower semimodular. In a finite ranked lattice this forces
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)=\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y)
\]
for all flats \(X,Y\). Hence \(L(S)\) is modular.

So the simple reduction is indeed to finite simple modular matroids with a polarity.

One caution: the component classification in the draft is too strong if “finite projective geometry” means Desarguesian \(PG(r-1,q)\). For example \(U_{2,7}\) is a connected simple modular matroid with a self-dual rank-two flat lattice, but it is not \(PG(1,q)\) over a finite field. This is not fatal, because the later proof only needs modularity, not field coordinatization.

---

## 3. The coordinate polar \(p^\dagger\): plausible, but final proof needs the full lemma

For a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient \(p\) of \(M\), the flat-constancy lemma is correct:

> If \(I,J\) are \(M\)-independent \(k\)-subsets with \(\operatorname{cl}_M(I)=\operatorname{cl}_M(J)\), then \(p_I=p_J\). If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \(p_I=\infty\).

The circuit-incidence proof is fine.

Then the formula
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp),& J\text{ \(M\)-independent},\ |J|=r-k,\\
\infty,& J\text{ \(M\)-dependent}
\end{cases}
\]
is well-defined.

The proof that \(p^\dagger\) satisfies Plücker relations is credible, but the final write-up must explicitly handle these cases:

- \(r-k=0,1\): Plücker relations are vacuous.
- In a Plücker relation with base set \(S\), if \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, all terms are \(\infty\).
- If one of the four added elements lies in \(\operatorname{cl}_M(S)\), then every matching sum contains an \(\infty\)-term, so the relation is trivial.
- If two added elements are parallel in \(M/\operatorname{cl}(S)\), this corresponds exactly to repeated hyperplanes in the local dual Plücker lemma.

The local dual Plücker lemma from t4 is the right repair. It should be stated as a standalone lemma for modular intervals.

The quotient proof for \(p^\dagger\) also needs one explicit two-sided version of the codimension-one polarity:

> **Interval codimension-one duality lemma.** Let \(X\) be a flat and \(Y=X^\perp\). If \(v\) is a codimension-one valuated quotient of \(M|Y\), then the function on atoms of \(M/X\) defined by
> \[
> u(X\vee e)=v(Y\wedge e^\perp)
> \]
> is a tropical point of \(M/X\).

The draft uses this implicitly. It is true, but t2 proves only the self-polar version, so the final write-up should state and prove the interval version.

With those repairs, the claims
\[
p^\dagger\in \operatorname{Dr}_{r-k}(M),\qquad (p^\dagger)^\dagger=p
\]
are justified.

---

## 4. Well-definedness on tropical linear spaces

This is the main conceptual point.

The formula \(p\mapsto p^\dagger\) is written in Plücker coordinates, but \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\) was defined as a poset of **tropical linear spaces**. Thus the proof must ensure that if
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(p)=\operatorname{Trop}(q),
\]
then
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger)=\operatorname{Trop}(q^\dagger).
\]

There are two ways to repair this:

1. cite the theorem that a valuated matroid is determined up to tropical scalar by its tropical linear space; or
2. define the map intrinsically by the point-annihilator formula
   \[
   \Psi(X)=\{x\in \operatorname{Trop}(M): X\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(\alpha(x))\}.
   \]

The second repair is better. The point-annihilator theorem proves
\[
\Psi(\operatorname{Trop}(p))=\operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger),
\]
so the output depends only on the tropical linear space, not on the chosen Plücker vector.

---

## 5. Point-annihilator theorem: essentially correct after two clarifications

The apartment argument is good. The key facts needed are:

For every \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), the polar basis
\[
b_i^\star=\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus b_i)^\perp
\]
is again an \(M\)-basis, and
\[
\operatorname{cl}\{b_i^\star:i\in S\}
=
\operatorname{cl}\{b_i:i\notin S\}^{\perp}.
\]

Therefore
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^\star}=(p|_B)^*
\]
as ordinary valuated matroids on the Boolean apartment.

Two details need correction in the written proof:

- In the forward direction, for arbitrary \(A\), the vector \((p_{A\cup b_i})_i\) is not necessarily a cocircuit of \(p|_B\). What is true, and sufficient, is that it is the restriction to \(B\) of a point of \(\operatorname{Trop}(p)\), hence lies in \(\operatorname{Trop}(p|_B)\).
- In the converse direction, the dependent \(J\)-case should be written carefully: if \(|J|=r-k+1\) and \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)<r-k\), all terms are \(\infty\); if \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)=r-k\), then \(J\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit \(D\), the finite \(p^\dagger\)-terms are constant on \(j\in D\), and the circuit condition for \(x\in\operatorname{Trop}(M)\) gives the required nonunique minimum.

With those clarifications, the point-annihilator theorem is valid:
\[
x\in \operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger)
\iff
\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(\alpha(x)).
\]

Then order reversal is immediate:
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(q)
\implies
\operatorname{Trop}(q^\dagger)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]

---

## 6. Flat subposet extension: correct

Let
\[
p^F
\]
be the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
Then \(p^F_I\) is finite exactly when \(\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\) is a complement of \(F\) in \(L(M)\).

Because polarity exchanges complements:
\[
G\text{ complements }F
\iff
G^\perp\text{ complements }F^\perp,
\]
one gets
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]
Hence
\[
\Phi\!\left(\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

Together with the simplification compatibility, this proves the extension property for the original, possibly nonsimple \(M\).

---

## Final referee conclusion

No fatal counterexample found. The proof is sound **provided** the final version explicitly states and proves the following lemmas:

1. simplification invariance of the relative Dressian;
2. modularity of the simplified matroid from self-duality of its flat lattice;
3. flat-constancy for valuated quotients of \(M\);
4. the local dual Plücker lemma, including dependent cases;
5. the interval codimension-one polarity lemma used to prove \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient;
6. apartment duality \(p^\dagger|_{B^\star}=(p|_B)^*\);
7. the point-annihilator theorem;
8. complement compatibility for flat quotients.

The t5 “opposition invariance of valuated flag matroids” should either be fully proved or omitted; it is not needed if the point-annihilator proof is used.

<SOLVER_REPORT>

    }
  ],

}
