description: writeup (full problem): For a finite matroid M whose lattice of flats admits an order-reversing involution F -> F^perp, there exists an order-reversing involution Phi on the relative Dressian Dr(M) extending the prescribed involution on the embedded copy of L(M)^op.
full_text:
## Original Problem

Let $M$ be a matroid, and let $\Trop(M)$ be the corresponding tropical linear space, i.e., the closure of the Bergman fan of $M$ in tropical projective space. The relative Dressian of $M$ is 
\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?

## Problem Solved

Let \(M\) be a finite matroid on a finite ground set \(E\), with lattice of flats \(L(M)\). Let \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\), and for a valuated matroid \(p\) on \(E\), let \(\Trop(p)\subseteq \mathbb T^E/\mathbb R(1,\dots,1)\) denote its tropical linear space. Let \(\Trop(M)\) mean the tropical linear space of the constant valuated matroid associated to \(M\). Define the relative Dressian
\[
\Dr(M)=\{\Trop(p): p \text{ is a valuated matroid quotient of } M\},
\]
ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. Equivalently, these are exactly the tropical linear spaces contained in \(\Trop(M)\). Suppose \(L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution
\[
F\longmapsto F^\perp.
\]
For each flat \(F\in L(M)\), let \(U_{0,F}\) denote the rank-zero matroid on \(F\), viewed as adding the elements of \(F\) as loops, so that
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}
\]
is a matroid on the original ground set \(E\). Then there exists an order-reversing involution
\[
\Phi:\Dr(M)\to \Dr(M)
\]
such that, for every flat \(F\in L(M)\),
\[
\Phi\bigl(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]
This is exactly the original question, under the standard finite-ground-set convention used for Dressians and valuated matroids; no weakening is made.

## Proof

### 1. Valuated-matroid conventions and standard facts

Let \(E\) be finite. A rank-\(k\) valuated matroid on \(E\) is a function
\[
p:\binom{E}{k}\to \mathbb T
\]
not identically \(\infty\), satisfying the tropical Plücker relations: for every \(S\in\binom{E}{k-2}\) and distinct \(a,b,c,d\in E\setminus S\),
\[
\min\bigl(
p_{S ab}+p_{S cd},\,
p_{S ac}+p_{S bd},\,
p_{S ad}+p_{S bc}
\bigr)
\]
is attained at least twice. We use the convention that “the minimum is attained at least twice” means “there is no unique finite minimum”; thus if all relevant terms are \(\infty\), the relation is satisfied.

The tropical linear space \(\Trop(p)\) is the set of \(x\in \mathbb T^E/\mathbb R(1,\dots,1)\) such that for every \(J\in\binom{E}{k+1}\),
\[
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(x_j+p_{J\setminus j}\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimum.

We use the following standard valuated-matroid facts.

1. **Relative spaces are valuated quotients.** If \(M\) is a matroid, regarded as the constant valuated matroid, then tropical linear subspaces of \(\Trop(M)\) are precisely the tropical linear spaces \(\Trop(p)\), where \(p\) is a valuated matroid quotient of \(M\).

2. **Inclusion is the quotient relation.** If \(p,q\) are valuated matroids of ranks \(k\le \ell\), then
   \[
   \Trop(p)\subseteq \Trop(q)
   \]
   if and only if \(p\) is a valuated quotient of \(q\). In coordinates, this quotient relation is given by the valuated flag incidence relations
   \[
   \min_{i\in B\setminus A}
   \bigl(
   p_{A\cup i}+q_{B\setminus i}
   \bigr)
   \]
   having no unique finite minimum for all \(|A|=k-1\) and \(|B|=\ell+1\).

3. **Uniqueness of Plücker coordinates.** A tropical linear space determines its valuated Plücker vector up to adding a global scalar. Hence any construction on valuated Plücker vectors that is invariant under global scalar addition gives a well-defined construction on tropical linear spaces.

4. **Circuit form for quotients of a constant matroid.** If \(p\) is a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of a matroid \(M\), then for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\),
   \[
   \min_{c\in C\setminus A} p_{A\cup c}
   \]
   has no unique finite minimum, where \(p_{A\cup c}=\infty\) if \(c\in A\). Conversely, these circuit-incidence relations characterize valuated quotients of \(M\).

5. **Boolean annihilator duality.** If \(\mu\) is an ordinary valuated matroid on a finite set \(\Omega\), and \(\mu^*\) is its valuated dual, then for \(u\in\mathbb T^\Omega\),
   \[
   u\in \Trop(\mu^*)
   \]
   if and only if
   \[
   \Trop(\mu)\subseteq
   H_u:=\left\{v\in\mathbb T^\Omega:
   \min_{\omega\in\Omega}(u_\omega+v_\omega)
   \text{ has no unique finite minimum}
   \right\}.
   \]

We now prove the theorem.

---

### 2. Reduction to the simplification

Let
\[
L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)
\]
be the set of loops of \(M\). Let
\[
S=\operatorname{si}(M)
\]
be the simplification of \(M\), obtained by deleting loops and replacing each non-loop parallel class by one element. Let
\[
\pi:E\setminus L_0\to \bar E
\]
be the quotient map to the set \(\bar E\) of non-loop parallel classes.

Every flat of \(M\) contains \(L_0\), and if it contains one element of a parallel class, it contains the whole parallel class. Hence flats of \(M\) are exactly the sets
\[
F=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F),
\]
where \(\bar F\) is a flat of \(S\). Thus there is a canonical lattice isomorphism
\[
\psi:L(M)\xrightarrow{\sim}L(S),
\qquad
\psi(F)=\pi(F\setminus L_0).
\]
The given involution \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) on \(L(M)\) therefore induces an order-reversing involution
\[
\bar F\longmapsto \bar F^\perp
\]
on \(L(S)\).

Define the diagonal map
\[
\Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\to\mathbb T^E
\]
by
\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty, & e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)}, & e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]
Loops of \(M\) force the corresponding coordinates of every point of \(\Trop(M)\) to be \(\infty\). If \(e,f\) are parallel non-loops, then \(\{e,f\}\) is a circuit, so every point of \(\Trop(M)\) satisfies \(x_e=x_f\). Conversely, after imposing these equations, the remaining circuit conditions are precisely those for the simplification \(S\). Therefore
\[
\Trop(M)=\Delta(\Trop(S)).
\]

Now let \(p\) be a valuated quotient of \(M\). Since \(\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(M)\), every loop of \(M\) is a loop of \(p\), and each original parallel class of \(M\) remains either entirely looped or a valuated parallel class with zero internal offset. Hence \(p\) descends uniquely to a valuated quotient \(\bar p\) of \(S\), and
\[
\Trop(p)=\Delta(\Trop(\bar p)).
\]
Conversely, every valuated quotient of \(S\) pulls back by adding the loops \(L_0\) and replacing each element of \(\bar E\) by a zero-offset parallel class. Thus \(\Delta\) gives a canonical poset isomorphism
\[
\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M).
\]

The flat subposet is compatible with this identification. If \(F=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F)\), then
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}
\]
is the zero-offset parallel extension of
\[
S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}.
\]
Hence
\[
\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta\bigl(\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\bigr).
\]

Therefore it suffices to prove the theorem when \(M\) is loopless and simple. At the end we transfer the resulting involution back through \(\Delta\).

---

### 3. The simple case is modular

Assume now that \(M\) is simple. Then \(L(M)\) is a finite geometric lattice: it is ranked, atomic, and semimodular.

Because \(L(M)\) admits an order-reversing involution, \(L(M)\) is isomorphic to its opposite lattice. Since \(L(M)\) is semimodular, its opposite lattice is also semimodular. Equivalently, \(L(M)\) is both upper and lower semimodular. In rank notation this means that for all flats \(X,Y\),
\[
\operatorname{rk}(X)+\operatorname{rk}(Y)
\ge
\operatorname{rk}(X\wedge Y)+\operatorname{rk}(X\vee Y),
\]
and also the reverse inequality. Therefore equality holds:
\[
\operatorname{rk}(X)+\operatorname{rk}(Y)
=
\operatorname{rk}(X\wedge Y)+\operatorname{rk}(X\vee Y).
\]
Thus \(L(M)\) is modular, and \(M\) is a simple modular matroid.

Henceforth, in the main construction, \(M\) is assumed simple modular of rank
\[
r=\operatorname{rk}M.
\]

---

### 4. Flat-constant coordinates for quotients of a modular matroid

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). We first prove that \(p\) is constant on \(M\)-bases of the same flat.

Let \(I\in\binom{E}{k}\) be \(M\)-dependent. Choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and \(c\in C\). Put \(A=I\setminus\{c\}\). Then
\[
C\setminus A=\{c\}.
\]
The circuit-incidence relation for \(p\) over \(M\) forces \(p_I=\infty\). Thus finite \(p\)-bases are \(M\)-independent.

Now suppose \(I=A\cup\{a\}\) and \(I'=A\cup\{b\}\) are \(M\)-independent \(k\)-subsets with the same closure:
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(I)=\operatorname{cl}_M(I').
\]
Then \(a\in \operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup\{b\})\), so there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing both \(a\) and \(b\). The circuit-incidence relation gives
\[
\min(p_{A\cup a},p_{A\cup b})
\]
with no unique finite minimum. Hence
\[
p_{A\cup a}=p_{A\cup b},
\]
allowing the common value \(\infty\).

The basis graph of a matroid restriction \(M|X\) is connected. Therefore, for every rank-\(k\) flat \(X\), the value \(p_I\) is independent of the choice of \(M\)-basis \(I\) of \(X\). Define
\[
\bar p(X):=p_I
\]
for any \(M\)-basis \(I\) of \(X\). If all such \(I\) have value \(\infty\), then \(\bar p(X)=\infty\).

---

### 5. The polar Plücker vector

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). Define a function \(p^\dagger\) on \((r-k)\)-subsets \(J\subseteq E\) by
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\bigr),
& J \text{ is } M\text{-independent},\\[2mm]
\infty,
& J \text{ is } M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]
Since \(J\) is \(M\)-independent of size \(r-k\), \(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)\) has rank \(r-k\), so \(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\) has rank \(k\), and \(\bar p\) is defined there.

We now record the main technical lemma.

#### Lemma 5.1 — polar coordinates define a quotient

Let \(M\) be simple modular of rank \(r\) with order-reversing involution \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) on \(L(M)\). If \(p\) is a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\), then:

1. \(p^\dagger\) is a rank-\((r-k)\) valuated matroid.
2. \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).
3. \((p^\dagger)^\dagger=p\), up to the usual global additive scalar.
4. If \(p\) is the constant valuated matroid of \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\), then \(p^\dagger\) is the constant valuated matroid of \(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}\).

##### Proof

We prove the four assertions.

First, we check the tropical Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\). Put
\[
m=r-k.
\]
Let \(S\in\binom{E}{m-2}\), and let \(a,b,c,d\in E\setminus S\) be distinct. If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, then every term in the Plücker relation for \(p^\dagger\) is \(\infty\), so the relation is automatic. Assume \(S\) is \(M\)-independent and set
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S),
\qquad
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+2\).

If one of \(a,b,c,d\) lies in \(X\), then every Plücker matching contains a dependent set, so all three Plücker sums are \(\infty\). Thus assume none lies in \(X\). For \(i\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define
\[
H_i=Y\wedge i^\perp=(X\vee i)^\perp.
\]
Each \(H_i\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\). If \(H_i=H_j\), then \(S\cup\{i,j\}\) is \(M\)-dependent, and the corresponding \(p^\dagger\)-coordinate is \(\infty\). If \(H_i\neq H_j\), modularity gives
\[
\operatorname{rk}(H_i\wedge H_j)=k,
\]
and
\[
p^\dagger_{Sij}
=
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j).
\]

Therefore the Plücker relation for \(p^\dagger\) is equivalent to the following local statement.

Let \(Y\) be a rank-\((k+2)\) flat, and let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\), possibly repeated. Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),& H_i\neq H_j,\\
\infty,& H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\, [13]+[24],\, [14]+[23]\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimum.

If two \(H_i\)'s are equal, two of the three displayed sums are identical and the third contains an \(\infty\)-term, so the relation is immediate. Assume the \(H_i\) are distinct. Put
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Since \(Y\) has rank \(k+2\), modularity implies
\[
\operatorname{rk}(Z)\in\{k,k-1,k-2\}.
\]

If \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k\), then all pairwise meets \(H_i\wedge H_j\) equal \(Z\), so all three sums are equal.

If \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-2\), put
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\neq i}H_j.
\]
Then each \(T_i\) has rank \(k-1\), and for \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\),
\[
H_i\wedge H_j=T_m\vee T_n.
\]
Choose a basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose elements \(t_i\in T_i\) such that
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup\{t_i\})=T_i.
\]
The ordinary tropical Plücker relation for \(p\) applied to
\[
A,t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4
\]
is exactly the desired relation.

Finally suppose \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-1\). Contract \(Z\) inside \(Y\). The interval \([Z,Y]\) becomes a rank-three simple modular matroid \(P\). The hyperplanes \(H_i/Z\) become rank-two flats, and the pairwise meets \(H_i\wedge H_j\) become points of \(P\). The function
\[
w(x)=\bar p(Z\vee x)
\]
on points \(x\) of \(P\) lies in \(\Trop(P)\), by the circuit-incidence relations for \(p\). Thus it remains only to know the following rank-three fact:

> If \(P\) is a simple modular rank-three matroid, \(w\in\Trop(P)\), and \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) are rank-two flats of \(P\), then
> \[
> \min\bigl(
> w(L_1\wedge L_2)+w(L_3\wedge L_4),\,
> w(L_1\wedge L_3)+w(L_2\wedge L_4),\,
> w(L_1\wedge L_4)+w(L_2\wedge L_3)
> \bigr)
> \]
> has no unique finite minimum.

This rank-three statement is elementary. If two \(L_i\)'s coincide, it is immediate. Otherwise, if \(P\) is connected, it is a projective plane. By the fine Bergman fan description, after adding a constant,
\[
w=\lambda \mathbf 1_a+\mu \mathbf 1_B
\]
for some point-line flag \(a\le B\) and \(\lambda,\mu\ge0\). Intersect each \(L_i\) with \(B\). The three displayed sums become the three perfect-matching costs on four colored objects, where a pair contributes only when the two colors agree. For four objects, the minimum of the three perfect-matching costs is never unique. If \(P\) is disconnected, it is either Boolean of rank three or a direct sum of a point and a rank-two line; in both cases the same assertion is an immediate check from the circuit condition on the rank-two line. This proves the local relation, and hence the Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\).

Next we verify that \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\). We use the circuit-incidence characterization. Let \(A\subseteq E\) have size \(m-1\), and let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit. If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, all terms are \(\infty\). Assume \(A\) is independent and put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A),
\qquad
Y=X^\perp.
\]
For \(c\notin X\),
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
=
\bar p\bigl((X\vee c)^\perp\bigr)
=
\bar p(Y\wedge c^\perp).
\]
The map \(Z\mapsto Z^\perp\) anti-identifies the interval \([X,E]\), which is the flat lattice of \(M/X\), with the interval \([0,Y]\), which is the flat lattice of \(M|Y\). On every rank-two flat of \(M/X\), the above values are precisely the codimension-one polar of the restriction of \(p\) to the corresponding corank-two pencil in \(Y\). The circuit-incidence relations for \(p\) say exactly that these values have no unique finite minimum on every circuit of that rank-two flat. Since \(M/X\) is modular, membership in \(\Trop(M/X)\) is detected on rank-two flats. Hence the function
\[
c\longmapsto p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
\]
lies in \(\Trop(M/X)\). Therefore its minimum on \(C\setminus X\), and hence on \(C\setminus A\), has no unique finite minimum. Thus \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).

The involutivity is immediate from the definition. If \(I\) is \(M\)-independent of size \(k\), then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(I)^\perp\bigr)
=
\bar p\bigl((\operatorname{cl}_M(I)^\perp)^\perp\bigr)
=
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(I))
=
p_I.
\]
If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, both sides are \(\infty\). Thus
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p
\]
up to the global scalar ambiguity of valuated Plücker coordinates.

Finally consider a flat \(F\). Let \(p^F\) be the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
A rank-\((r-\operatorname{rk}F)\) flat \(G\) has finite \(\bar p^F(G)\) precisely when \(G\) is a lattice complement of \(F\):
\[
G\vee F=E,
\qquad
G\wedge F=\varnothing.
\]
Because \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) is an order-reversing lattice involution, \(G\) is a complement of \(F\) if and only if \(G^\perp\) is a complement of \(F^\perp\). Hence
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]
This proves the lemma. \(\square\)

---

### 6. Polar bases

We now prove the order-reversing property.

Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis. Define its polar basis by
\[
b_i^*=\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus\{b_i\})^\perp.
\]
Because \(\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus\{b_i\})\) is a hyperplane, \(b_i^*\) is an atom, hence an element of \(E\). Modularity implies that \(B^*=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}\) is again an \(M\)-basis.

More precisely, for every \(S\subseteq\{1,\dots,r\}\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in S\}
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}^{\perp}.
\]
Indeed, the flats generated by subsets of the basis \(B\) form a Boolean sublattice, and polarity turns meets into joins. Thus
\[
\bigvee_{i\in S} b_i^*
=
\bigvee_{i\in S}\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus\{b_i\})^\perp
=
\left(
\bigwedge_{i\in S}\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus\{b_i\})
\right)^\perp
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}^{\perp}.
\]

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). Its restriction to the Boolean apartment \(B\) is the rank-\(k\) valuated matroid
\[
(p|_B)_T=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}},
\qquad |T|=k.
\]
Since \(B\) spans \(M\), it spans every quotient of \(M\), so \(p|_B\) has rank \(k\).

Now restrict \(p^\dagger\) to the polar basis \(B^*\). If \(|S|=r-k\), then
\[
(p^\dagger|_{B^*})_S
=
p^\dagger_{\{b_i^*:i\in S\}}
=
\bar p\left(
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in S\}^{\perp}
\right).
\]
Using the polar-basis identity,
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in S\}^{\perp}
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}.
\]
Therefore
\[
(p^\dagger|_{B^*})_S
=
p_{\{b_i:i\notin S\}}.
\]
This is exactly the ordinary valuated dual of \(p|_B\):
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=(p|_B)^*.
\]

---

### 7. The point-annihilator theorem

Let \(x\in\Trop(M)\). Define a rank-\((r-1)\) quotient \(\alpha(x)\) of \(M\) by
\[
\alpha(x)_I=
\begin{cases}
x_{\operatorname{cl}_M(I)^\perp},
& I\in\binom{E}{r-1}\text{ is }M\text{-independent},\\
\infty,
& I\text{ is }M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]
Equivalently, if \(H\) is a hyperplane of \(M\), then the coordinate of \(\alpha(x)\) at \(H\) is
\[
\alpha(x)_H=x_{H^\perp}.
\]
This is the special case \(k=1\) of the operation \(p\mapsto p^\dagger\).

We prove the key characterization.

#### Theorem 7.1 — point-annihilator characterization

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\), and let \(x\in\Trop(M)\). Then
\[
x\in\Trop(p^\dagger)
\]
if and only if
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq \Trop(\alpha(x)).
\]

##### Proof

First assume \(x\in\Trop(p^\dagger)\). Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis, and let \(B^*=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}\) be the polar basis. Define
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*})\in\mathbb T^B.
\]
Since \(x\in\Trop(p^\dagger)\), its restriction to \(B^*\) lies in
\[
\Trop(p^\dagger|_{B^*})
=
\Trop((p|_B)^*).
\]
By ordinary Boolean annihilator duality,
\[
\Trop(p|_B)\subseteq H_{u_B}.
\]

Let \(C_A\) be a valuated cocircuit of \(p\), indexed by a \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\):
\[
(C_A)_e=
\begin{cases}
p_{A\cup e},& e\notin A,\\
\infty,& e\in A.
\end{cases}
\]
Valuated cocircuits lie in \(\Trop(p)\). Hence the restriction \(C_A|_B\) lies in \(\Trop(p|_B)\). Therefore
\[
\min_{i=1}^r\bigl((C_A)_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimum.

But this is exactly the basis-incidence equation saying that \(C_A\in\Trop(\alpha(x))\). Since this holds for every valuated cocircuit \(C_A\), and since \(\Trop(p)\) is the tropical convex hull of its valuated cocircuits, we get
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq \Trop(\alpha(x)).
\]

Conversely, assume
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq \Trop(\alpha(x)).
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), with polar basis \(B^*\), and again set
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
We claim that
\[
u_B\in \Trop((p|_B)^*).
\]
By Boolean annihilator duality, it suffices to show that \(u_B\) is tropically orthogonal to every valuated cocircuit of \(p|_B\).

A valuated cocircuit of \(p|_B\) has the form
\[
D_S=(p_{S\cup b_i})_{i=1}^r
\]
for some \((k-1)\)-subset \(S\subseteq B\), again with the convention that the coordinate is \(\infty\) if \(b_i\in S\). This is the restriction to \(B\) of the corresponding valuated cocircuit \(C_S\) of \(p\). Since
\[
C_S\in\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(\alpha(x)),
\]
testing membership in \(\Trop(\alpha(x))\) on the basis \(B\) gives
\[
\min_{i=1}^r\bigl(p_{S\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimum. Thus \(D_S\) is orthogonal to \(u_B\), and hence
\[
u_B\in\Trop((p|_B)^*)
=
\Trop(p^\dagger|_{B^*}).
\]

Now let \(J\subseteq E\) be \(M\)-independent of size \(r-k+1\). Extend \(J\) to an \(M\)-basis \(B^*\). Since every \(M\)-basis is the polar basis of another \(M\)-basis, the preceding paragraph applies. Thus the ordinary circuit equation for \(J\) inside the Boolean apartment \(B^*\) gives
\[
\min_{j\in J}
\bigl(
x_j+p^\dagger_{J\setminus j}
\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimum.

If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent, then either every \(J\setminus j\) is \(M\)-dependent, in which case all terms are \(\infty\), or \(J\) has rank \(r-k\) and contains a unique \(M\)-circuit \(C\). In the latter case, the finite \(p^\dagger_{J\setminus j}\)-terms occur exactly for \(j\in C\), and they are all equal because the flats \(\operatorname{cl}_M(J\setminus j)\) are the same. Since \(x\in\Trop(M)\), the minimum of \(x_j\) on the circuit \(C\) has no unique finite minimum. Hence the circuit equation for \(p^\dagger\) again holds.

Therefore all defining equations for \(x\in\Trop(p^\dagger)\) hold, and so
\[
x\in\Trop(p^\dagger).
\]
This proves the theorem. \(\square\)

---

### 8. Construction of the involution in the simple modular case

For \(X\in\Dr(M)\), choose a valuated quotient \(p\) of \(M\) such that
\[
X=\Trop(p).
\]
Define
\[
\Phi(X)=\Trop(p^\dagger).
\]
This is well-defined because \(p\) is determined by \(X\) up to adding a global scalar, and the construction \(p\mapsto p^\dagger\) adds the same scalar.

By Lemma 5.1,
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p,
\]
so \(\Phi\) is an involution.

Now suppose
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(q).
\]
Let \(x\in\Trop(q^\dagger)\). By Theorem 7.1,
\[
\Trop(q)\subseteq\Trop(\alpha(x)).
\]
Since \(\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(q)\), we get
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(\alpha(x)).
\]
Applying Theorem 7.1 again gives
\[
x\in\Trop(p^\dagger).
\]
Thus
\[
\Trop(q^\dagger)\subseteq\Trop(p^\dagger),
\]
so \(\Phi\) is order-reversing.

Finally, Lemma 5.1 gives, for every flat \(F\),
\[
\Phi\bigl(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]
Therefore \(\Phi\) extends the prescribed flat-lattice involution.

The boundary ranks are included. The unique rank-\(r\) quotient of \(M\) is \(M\) itself, and its polar is the rank-zero all-loop matroid; conversely, the polar of the rank-zero all-loop matroid is \(M\). Thus \(\Phi\) exchanges the top and bottom elements of \(\Dr(M)\).

---

### 9. Returning to the original matroid

For a general finite matroid \(M\), let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\). We proved above that
\[
\Delta:\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M)
\]
is a canonical poset isomorphism compatible with the embedded flat subposets and with the induced polarities on \(L(S)\) and \(L(M)\).

Construct the order-reversing involution \(\Phi_S\) on \(\Dr(S)\) by the simple modular argument. Then define
\[
\Phi_M
=
\Delta\circ \Phi_S\circ \Delta^{-1}.
\]
Because \(\Delta\) is a poset isomorphism, \(\Phi_M\) is an order-reversing involution of \(\Dr(M)\). Compatibility of \(\Delta\) with flat quotients gives, for every flat \(F\in L(M)\),
\[
\Phi_M\bigl(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

Hence the answer to the question is affirmative.

Note that there is a natural inclusion $\\cLL(M)^{\\op} \\subset \\Dr(M)$ given by $F\\mapsto \\Trop(M/F\\oplus U_{0,F})$.\n\n\\medskip\n\\noindent \\textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\\Phi$ on $\\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\\mapsto F^\\perp$?",

Suppose L(M) has an order-reversing involution F ↦ F^perp. For every flat F, let U_{0,F} be the rank-zero matroid on F, so M/F ⊕ U_{0,F} is viewed as a matroid on the original ground set. Then there exists an order-reversing involution Phi: Dr(M) → Dr(M) such that Phi(Trop(M/F ⊕ U_{0,F})) = Trop(M/F^perp ⊕ U_{0,F^perp}) for every flat F. This is the original question under the standard finite-ground-set convention for Dressians and valuated matroids.",

solution:
## Original Problem

Let $M$ be a matroid, and let $\Trop(M)$ be the corresponding tropical linear space, i.e., the closure of the Bergman fan of $M$ in tropical projective space. The relative Dressian of $M$ is 
\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?

## Problem Solved

Let \(M\) be a finite matroid on a finite ground set \(E\), with lattice of flats \(L(M)\). Let \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\), and for a valuated matroid \(p\) on \(E\), let \(\Trop(p)\subseteq \mathbb T^E/\mathbb R(1,\dots,1)\) denote its tropical linear space. Let \(\Trop(M)\) mean the tropical linear space of the constant valuated matroid associated to \(M\). Define the relative Dressian
\[
\Dr(M)=\{\Trop(p): p \text{ is a valuated matroid quotient of } M\},
\]
ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. Equivalently, these are exactly the tropical linear spaces contained in \(\Trop(M)\). Suppose \(L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution
\[
F\longmapsto F^\perp.
\]
For each flat \(F\in L(M)\), let \(U_{0,F}\) denote the rank-zero matroid on \(F\), viewed as adding the elements of \(F\) as loops, so that
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}
\]
is a matroid on the original ground set \(E\). Then there exists an order-reversing involution
\[
\Phi:\Dr(M)\to \Dr(M)
\]
such that, for every flat \(F\in L(M)\),
\[
\Phi\bigl(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]
This is exactly the original question, under the standard finite-ground-set convention used for Dressians and valuated matroids; no weakening is made.

## Proof

### 1. Valuated-matroid conventions and standard facts

Let \(E\) be finite. A rank-\(k\) valuated matroid on \(E\) is a function
\[
p:\binom{E}{k}\to \mathbb T
\]
not identically \(\infty\), satisfying the tropical Plücker relations: for every \(S\in\binom{E}{k-2}\) and distinct \(a,b,c,d\in E\setminus S\),
\[
\min\bigl(
p_{S ab}+p_{S cd},\,
p_{S ac}+p_{S bd},\,
p_{S ad}+p_{S bc}
\bigr)
\]
is attained at least twice. We use the convention that “the minimum is attained at least twice” means “there is no unique finite minimum”; thus if all relevant terms are \(\infty\), the relation is satisfied.

The tropical linear space \(\Trop(p)\) is the set of \(x\in \mathbb T^E/\mathbb R(1,\dots,1)\) such that for every \(J\in\binom{E}{k+1}\),
\[
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(x_j+p_{J\setminus j}\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimum.

We use the following standard valuated-matroid facts.

1. **Relative spaces are valuated quotients.** If \(M\) is a matroid, regarded as the constant valuated matroid, then tropical linear subspaces of \(\Trop(M)\) are precisely the tropical linear spaces \(\Trop(p)\), where \(p\) is a valuated matroid quotient of \(M\).

2. **Inclusion is the quotient relation.** If \(p,q\) are valuated matroids of ranks \(k\le \ell\), then
   \[
   \Trop(p)\subseteq \Trop(q)
   \]
   if and only if \(p\) is a valuated quotient of \(q\). In coordinates, this quotient relation is given by the valuated flag incidence relations
   \[
   \min_{i\in B\setminus A}
   \bigl(
   p_{A\cup i}+q_{B\setminus i}
   \bigr)
   \]
   having no unique finite minimum for all \(|A|=k-1\) and \(|B|=\ell+1\).

3. **Uniqueness of Plücker coordinates.** A tropical linear space determines its valuated Plücker vector up to adding a global scalar. Hence any construction on valuated Plücker vectors that is invariant under global scalar addition gives a well-defined construction on tropical linear spaces.

4. **Circuit form for quotients of a constant matroid.** If \(p\) is a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of a matroid \(M\), then for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\),
   \[
   \min_{c\in C\setminus A} p_{A\cup c}
   \]
   has no unique finite minimum, where \(p_{A\cup c}=\infty\) if \(c\in A\). Conversely, these circuit-incidence relations characterize valuated quotients of \(M\).

5. **Boolean annihilator duality.** If \(\mu\) is an ordinary valuated matroid on a finite set \(\Omega\), and \(\mu^*\) is its valuated dual, then for \(u\in\mathbb T^\Omega\),
   \[
   u\in \Trop(\mu^*)
   \]
   if and only if
   \[
   \Trop(\mu)\subseteq
   H_u:=\left\{v\in\mathbb T^\Omega:
   \min_{\omega\in\Omega}(u_\omega+v_\omega)
   \text{ has no unique finite minimum}
   \right\}.
   \]

We now prove the theorem.

---

### 2. Reduction to the simplification

Let
\[
L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)
\]
be the set of loops of \(M\). Let
\[
S=\operatorname{si}(M)
\]
be the simplification of \(M\), obtained by deleting loops and replacing each non-loop parallel class by one element. Let
\[
\pi:E\setminus L_0\to \bar E
\]
be the quotient map to the set \(\bar E\) of non-loop parallel classes.

Every flat of \(M\) contains \(L_0\), and if it contains one element of a parallel class, it contains the whole parallel class. Hence flats of \(M\) are exactly the sets
\[
F=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F),
\]
where \(\bar F\) is a flat of \(S\). Thus there is a canonical lattice isomorphism
\[
\psi:L(M)\xrightarrow{\sim}L(S),
\qquad
\psi(F)=\pi(F\setminus L_0).
\]
The given involution \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) on \(L(M)\) therefore induces an order-reversing involution
\[
\bar F\longmapsto \bar F^\perp
\]
on \(L(S)\).

Define the diagonal map
\[
\Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\to\mathbb T^E
\]
by
\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty, & e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)}, & e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]
Loops of \(M\) force the corresponding coordinates of every point of \(\Trop(M)\) to be \(\infty\). If \(e,f\) are parallel non-loops, then \(\{e,f\}\) is a circuit, so every point of \(\Trop(M)\) satisfies \(x_e=x_f\). Conversely, after imposing these equations, the remaining circuit conditions are precisely those for the simplification \(S\). Therefore
\[
\Trop(M)=\Delta(\Trop(S)).
\]

Now let \(p\) be a valuated quotient of \(M\). Since \(\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(M)\), every loop of \(M\) is a loop of \(p\), and each original parallel class of \(M\) remains either entirely looped or a valuated parallel class with zero internal offset. Hence \(p\) descends uniquely to a valuated quotient \(\bar p\) of \(S\), and
\[
\Trop(p)=\Delta(\Trop(\bar p)).
\]
Conversely, every valuated quotient of \(S\) pulls back by adding the loops \(L_0\) and replacing each element of \(\bar E\) by a zero-offset parallel class. Thus \(\Delta\) gives a canonical poset isomorphism
\[
\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M).
\]

The flat subposet is compatible with this identification. If \(F=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F)\), then
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}
\]
is the zero-offset parallel extension of
\[
S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}.
\]
Hence
\[
\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta\bigl(\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\bigr).
\]

Therefore it suffices to prove the theorem when \(M\) is loopless and simple. At the end we transfer the resulting involution back through \(\Delta\).

---

### 3. The simple case is modular

Assume now that \(M\) is simple. Then \(L(M)\) is a finite geometric lattice: it is ranked, atomic, and semimodular.

Because \(L(M)\) admits an order-reversing involution, \(L(M)\) is isomorphic to its opposite lattice. Since \(L(M)\) is semimodular, its opposite lattice is also semimodular. Equivalently, \(L(M)\) is both upper and lower semimodular. In rank notation this means that for all flats \(X,Y\),
\[
\operatorname{rk}(X)+\operatorname{rk}(Y)
\ge
\operatorname{rk}(X\wedge Y)+\operatorname{rk}(X\vee Y),
\]
and also the reverse inequality. Therefore equality holds:
\[
\operatorname{rk}(X)+\operatorname{rk}(Y)
=
\operatorname{rk}(X\wedge Y)+\operatorname{rk}(X\vee Y).
\]
Thus \(L(M)\) is modular, and \(M\) is a simple modular matroid.

Henceforth, in the main construction, \(M\) is assumed simple modular of rank
\[
r=\operatorname{rk}M.
\]

---

### 4. Flat-constant coordinates for quotients of a modular matroid

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). We first prove that \(p\) is constant on \(M\)-bases of the same flat.

Let \(I\in\binom{E}{k}\) be \(M\)-dependent. Choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and \(c\in C\). Put \(A=I\setminus\{c\}\). Then
\[
C\setminus A=\{c\}.
\]
The circuit-incidence relation for \(p\) over \(M\) forces \(p_I=\infty\). Thus finite \(p\)-bases are \(M\)-independent.

Now suppose \(I=A\cup\{a\}\) and \(I'=A\cup\{b\}\) are \(M\)-independent \(k\)-subsets with the same closure:
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(I)=\operatorname{cl}_M(I').
\]
Then \(a\in \operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup\{b\})\), so there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing both \(a\) and \(b\). The circuit-incidence relation gives
\[
\min(p_{A\cup a},p_{A\cup b})
\]
with no unique finite minimum. Hence
\[
p_{A\cup a}=p_{A\cup b},
\]
allowing the common value \(\infty\).

The basis graph of a matroid restriction \(M|X\) is connected. Therefore, for every rank-\(k\) flat \(X\), the value \(p_I\) is independent of the choice of \(M\)-basis \(I\) of \(X\). Define
\[
\bar p(X):=p_I
\]
for any \(M\)-basis \(I\) of \(X\). If all such \(I\) have value \(\infty\), then \(\bar p(X)=\infty\).

---

### 5. The polar Plücker vector

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). Define a function \(p^\dagger\) on \((r-k)\)-subsets \(J\subseteq E\) by
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\bigr),
& J \text{ is } M\text{-independent},\\[2mm]
\infty,
& J \text{ is } M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]
Since \(J\) is \(M\)-independent of size \(r-k\), \(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)\) has rank \(r-k\), so \(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\) has rank \(k\), and \(\bar p\) is defined there.

We now record the main technical lemma.

#### Lemma 5.1 — polar coordinates define a quotient

Let \(M\) be simple modular of rank \(r\) with order-reversing involution \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) on \(L(M)\). If \(p\) is a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\), then:

1. \(p^\dagger\) is a rank-\((r-k)\) valuated matroid.
2. \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).
3. \((p^\dagger)^\dagger=p\), up to the usual global additive scalar.
4. If \(p\) is the constant valuated matroid of \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\), then \(p^\dagger\) is the constant valuated matroid of \(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}\).

##### Proof

We prove the four assertions.

First, we check the tropical Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\). Put
\[
m=r-k.
\]
Let \(S\in\binom{E}{m-2}\), and let \(a,b,c,d\in E\setminus S\) be distinct. If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, then every term in the Plücker relation for \(p^\dagger\) is \(\infty\), so the relation is automatic. Assume \(S\) is \(M\)-independent and set
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S),
\qquad
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+2\).

If one of \(a,b,c,d\) lies in \(X\), then every Plücker matching contains a dependent set, so all three Plücker sums are \(\infty\). Thus assume none lies in \(X\). For \(i\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define
\[
H_i=Y\wedge i^\perp=(X\vee i)^\perp.
\]
Each \(H_i\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\). If \(H_i=H_j\), then \(S\cup\{i,j\}\) is \(M\)-dependent, and the corresponding \(p^\dagger\)-coordinate is \(\infty\). If \(H_i\neq H_j\), modularity gives
\[
\operatorname{rk}(H_i\wedge H_j)=k,
\]
and
\[
p^\dagger_{Sij}
=
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j).
\]

Therefore the Plücker relation for \(p^\dagger\) is equivalent to the following local statement.

Let \(Y\) be a rank-\((k+2)\) flat, and let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\), possibly repeated. Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),& H_i\neq H_j,\\
\infty,& H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\, [13]+[24],\, [14]+[23]\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimum.

If two \(H_i\)'s are equal, two of the three displayed sums are identical and the third contains an \(\infty\)-term, so the relation is immediate. Assume the \(H_i\) are distinct. Put
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Since \(Y\) has rank \(k+2\), modularity implies
\[
\operatorname{rk}(Z)\in\{k,k-1,k-2\}.
\]

If \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k\), then all pairwise meets \(H_i\wedge H_j\) equal \(Z\), so all three sums are equal.

If \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-2\), put
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\neq i}H_j.
\]
Then each \(T_i\) has rank \(k-1\), and for \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\),
\[
H_i\wedge H_j=T_m\vee T_n.
\]
Choose a basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose elements \(t_i\in T_i\) such that
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(A\cup\{t_i\})=T_i.
\]
The ordinary tropical Plücker relation for \(p\) applied to
\[
A,t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4
\]
is exactly the desired relation.

Finally suppose \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-1\). Contract \(Z\) inside \(Y\). The interval \([Z,Y]\) becomes a rank-three simple modular matroid \(P\). The hyperplanes \(H_i/Z\) become rank-two flats, and the pairwise meets \(H_i\wedge H_j\) become points of \(P\). The function
\[
w(x)=\bar p(Z\vee x)
\]
on points \(x\) of \(P\) lies in \(\Trop(P)\), by the circuit-incidence relations for \(p\). Thus it remains only to know the following rank-three fact:

> If \(P\) is a simple modular rank-three matroid, \(w\in\Trop(P)\), and \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) are rank-two flats of \(P\), then
> \[
> \min\bigl(
> w(L_1\wedge L_2)+w(L_3\wedge L_4),\,
> w(L_1\wedge L_3)+w(L_2\wedge L_4),\,
> w(L_1\wedge L_4)+w(L_2\wedge L_3)
> \bigr)
> \]
> has no unique finite minimum.

This rank-three statement is elementary. If two \(L_i\)'s coincide, it is immediate. Otherwise, if \(P\) is connected, it is a projective plane. By the fine Bergman fan description, after adding a constant,
\[
w=\lambda \mathbf 1_a+\mu \mathbf 1_B
\]
for some point-line flag \(a\le B\) and \(\lambda,\mu\ge0\). Intersect each \(L_i\) with \(B\). The three displayed sums become the three perfect-matching costs on four colored objects, where a pair contributes only when the two colors agree. For four objects, the minimum of the three perfect-matching costs is never unique. If \(P\) is disconnected, it is either Boolean of rank three or a direct sum of a point and a rank-two line; in both cases the same assertion is an immediate check from the circuit condition on the rank-two line. This proves the local relation, and hence the Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\).

Next we verify that \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\). We use the circuit-incidence characterization. Let \(A\subseteq E\) have size \(m-1\), and let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit. If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, all terms are \(\infty\). Assume \(A\) is independent and put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A),
\qquad
Y=X^\perp.
\]
For \(c\notin X\),
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
=
\bar p\bigl((X\vee c)^\perp\bigr)
=
\bar p(Y\wedge c^\perp).
\]
The map \(Z\mapsto Z^\perp\) anti-identifies the interval \([X,E]\), which is the flat lattice of \(M/X\), with the interval \([0,Y]\), which is the flat lattice of \(M|Y\). On every rank-two flat of \(M/X\), the above values are precisely the codimension-one polar of the restriction of \(p\) to the corresponding corank-two pencil in \(Y\). The circuit-incidence relations for \(p\) say exactly that these values have no unique finite minimum on every circuit of that rank-two flat. Since \(M/X\) is modular, membership in \(\Trop(M/X)\) is detected on rank-two flats. Hence the function
\[
c\longmapsto p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
\]
lies in \(\Trop(M/X)\). Therefore its minimum on \(C\setminus X\), and hence on \(C\setminus A\), has no unique finite minimum. Thus \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).

The involutivity is immediate from the definition. If \(I\) is \(M\)-independent of size \(k\), then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(I)^\perp\bigr)
=
\bar p\bigl((\operatorname{cl}_M(I)^\perp)^\perp\bigr)
=
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(I))
=
p_I.
\]
If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, both sides are \(\infty\). Thus
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p
\]
up to the global scalar ambiguity of valuated Plücker coordinates.

Finally consider a flat \(F\). Let \(p^F\) be the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
A rank-\((r-\operatorname{rk}F)\) flat \(G\) has finite \(\bar p^F(G)\) precisely when \(G\) is a lattice complement of \(F\):
\[
G\vee F=E,
\qquad
G\wedge F=\varnothing.
\]
Because \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) is an order-reversing lattice involution, \(G\) is a complement of \(F\) if and only if \(G^\perp\) is a complement of \(F^\perp\). Hence
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]
This proves the lemma. \(\square\)

---

### 6. Polar bases

We now prove the order-reversing property.

Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis. Define its polar basis by
\[
b_i^*=\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus\{b_i\})^\perp.
\]
Because \(\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus\{b_i\})\) is a hyperplane, \(b_i^*\) is an atom, hence an element of \(E\). Modularity implies that \(B^*=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}\) is again an \(M\)-basis.

More precisely, for every \(S\subseteq\{1,\dots,r\}\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in S\}
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}^{\perp}.
\]
Indeed, the flats generated by subsets of the basis \(B\) form a Boolean sublattice, and polarity turns meets into joins. Thus
\[
\bigvee_{i\in S} b_i^*
=
\bigvee_{i\in S}\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus\{b_i\})^\perp
=
\left(
\bigwedge_{i\in S}\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus\{b_i\})
\right)^\perp
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}^{\perp}.
\]

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). Its restriction to the Boolean apartment \(B\) is the rank-\(k\) valuated matroid
\[
(p|_B)_T=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}},
\qquad |T|=k.
\]
Since \(B\) spans \(M\), it spans every quotient of \(M\), so \(p|_B\) has rank \(k\).

Now restrict \(p^\dagger\) to the polar basis \(B^*\). If \(|S|=r-k\), then
\[
(p^\dagger|_{B^*})_S
=
p^\dagger_{\{b_i^*:i\in S\}}
=
\bar p\left(
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in S\}^{\perp}
\right).
\]
Using the polar-basis identity,
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in S\}^{\perp}
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}.
\]
Therefore
\[
(p^\dagger|_{B^*})_S
=
p_{\{b_i:i\notin S\}}.
\]
This is exactly the ordinary valuated dual of \(p|_B\):
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=(p|_B)^*.
\]

---

### 7. The point-annihilator theorem

Let \(x\in\Trop(M)\). Define a rank-\((r-1)\) quotient \(\alpha(x)\) of \(M\) by
\[
\alpha(x)_I=
\begin{cases}
x_{\operatorname{cl}_M(I)^\perp},
& I\in\binom{E}{r-1}\text{ is }M\text{-independent},\\
\infty,
& I\text{ is }M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]
Equivalently, if \(H\) is a hyperplane of \(M\), then the coordinate of \(\alpha(x)\) at \(H\) is
\[
\alpha(x)_H=x_{H^\perp}.
\]
This is the special case \(k=1\) of the operation \(p\mapsto p^\dagger\).

We prove the key characterization.

#### Theorem 7.1 — point-annihilator characterization

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\), and let \(x\in\Trop(M)\). Then
\[
x\in\Trop(p^\dagger)
\]
if and only if
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq \Trop(\alpha(x)).
\]

##### Proof

First assume \(x\in\Trop(p^\dagger)\). Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis, and let \(B^*=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}\) be the polar basis. Define
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*})\in\mathbb T^B.
\]
Since \(x\in\Trop(p^\dagger)\), its restriction to \(B^*\) lies in
\[
\Trop(p^\dagger|_{B^*})
=
\Trop((p|_B)^*).
\]
By ordinary Boolean annihilator duality,
\[
\Trop(p|_B)\subseteq H_{u_B}.
\]

Let \(C_A\) be a valuated cocircuit of \(p\), indexed by a \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\):
\[
(C_A)_e=
\begin{cases}
p_{A\cup e},& e\notin A,\\
\infty,& e\in A.
\end{cases}
\]
Valuated cocircuits lie in \(\Trop(p)\). Hence the restriction \(C_A|_B\) lies in \(\Trop(p|_B)\). Therefore
\[
\min_{i=1}^r\bigl((C_A)_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimum.

But this is exactly the basis-incidence equation saying that \(C_A\in\Trop(\alpha(x))\). Since this holds for every valuated cocircuit \(C_A\), and since \(\Trop(p)\) is the tropical convex hull of its valuated cocircuits, we get
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq \Trop(\alpha(x)).
\]

Conversely, assume
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq \Trop(\alpha(x)).
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), with polar basis \(B^*\), and again set
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
We claim that
\[
u_B\in \Trop((p|_B)^*).
\]
By Boolean annihilator duality, it suffices to show that \(u_B\) is tropically orthogonal to every valuated cocircuit of \(p|_B\).

A valuated cocircuit of \(p|_B\) has the form
\[
D_S=(p_{S\cup b_i})_{i=1}^r
\]
for some \((k-1)\)-subset \(S\subseteq B\), again with the convention that the coordinate is \(\infty\) if \(b_i\in S\). This is the restriction to \(B\) of the corresponding valuated cocircuit \(C_S\) of \(p\). Since
\[
C_S\in\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(\alpha(x)),
\]
testing membership in \(\Trop(\alpha(x))\) on the basis \(B\) gives
\[
\min_{i=1}^r\bigl(p_{S\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimum. Thus \(D_S\) is orthogonal to \(u_B\), and hence
\[
u_B\in\Trop((p|_B)^*)
=
\Trop(p^\dagger|_{B^*}).
\]

Now let \(J\subseteq E\) be \(M\)-independent of size \(r-k+1\). Extend \(J\) to an \(M\)-basis \(B^*\). Since every \(M\)-basis is the polar basis of another \(M\)-basis, the preceding paragraph applies. Thus the ordinary circuit equation for \(J\) inside the Boolean apartment \(B^*\) gives
\[
\min_{j\in J}
\bigl(
x_j+p^\dagger_{J\setminus j}
\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimum.

If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent, then either every \(J\setminus j\) is \(M\)-dependent, in which case all terms are \(\infty\), or \(J\) has rank \(r-k\) and contains a unique \(M\)-circuit \(C\). In the latter case, the finite \(p^\dagger_{J\setminus j}\)-terms occur exactly for \(j\in C\), and they are all equal because the flats \(\operatorname{cl}_M(J\setminus j)\) are the same. Since \(x\in\Trop(M)\), the minimum of \(x_j\) on the circuit \(C\) has no unique finite minimum. Hence the circuit equation for \(p^\dagger\) again holds.

Therefore all defining equations for \(x\in\Trop(p^\dagger)\) hold, and so
\[
x\in\Trop(p^\dagger).
\]
This proves the theorem. \(\square\)

---

### 8. Construction of the involution in the simple modular case

For \(X\in\Dr(M)\), choose a valuated quotient \(p\) of \(M\) such that
\[
X=\Trop(p).
\]
Define
\[
\Phi(X)=\Trop(p^\dagger).
\]
This is well-defined because \(p\) is determined by \(X\) up to adding a global scalar, and the construction \(p\mapsto p^\dagger\) adds the same scalar.

By Lemma 5.1,
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p,
\]
so \(\Phi\) is an involution.

Now suppose
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(q).
\]
Let \(x\in\Trop(q^\dagger)\). By Theorem 7.1,
\[
\Trop(q)\subseteq\Trop(\alpha(x)).
\]
Since \(\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(q)\), we get
\[
\Trop(p)\subseteq\Trop(\alpha(x)).
\]
Applying Theorem 7.1 again gives
\[
x\in\Trop(p^\dagger).
\]
Thus
\[
\Trop(q^\dagger)\subseteq\Trop(p^\dagger),
\]
so \(\Phi\) is order-reversing.

Finally, Lemma 5.1 gives, for every flat \(F\),
\[
\Phi\bigl(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]
Therefore \(\Phi\) extends the prescribed flat-lattice involution.

The boundary ranks are included. The unique rank-\(r\) quotient of \(M\) is \(M\) itself, and its polar is the rank-zero all-loop matroid; conversely, the polar of the rank-zero all-loop matroid is \(M\). Thus \(\Phi\) exchanges the top and bottom elements of \(\Dr(M)\).

---

### 9. Returning to the original matroid

For a general finite matroid \(M\), let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\). We proved above that
\[
\Delta:\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M)
\]
is a canonical poset isomorphism compatible with the embedded flat subposets and with the induced polarities on \(L(S)\) and \(L(M)\).

Construct the order-reversing involution \(\Phi_S\) on \(\Dr(S)\) by the simple modular argument. Then define
\[
\Phi_M
=
\Delta\circ \Phi_S\circ \Delta^{-1}.
\]
Because \(\Delta\) is a poset isomorphism, \(\Phi_M\) is an order-reversing involution of \(\Dr(M)\). Compatibility of \(\Delta\) with flat quotients gives, for every flat \(F\in L(M)\),
\[
\Phi_M\bigl(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

Hence the answer to the question is affirmative.

description: Self-contained simplification invariance theorem for relative Dressians
full_text:
## Simplification reduction

Let \(M\) be a finite matroid on ground set \(E\). Let

\[
L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)
\]

be its loop set, let \(\bar E\) be the set of non-loop parallel classes of \(M\), and let

\[
\pi:E\setminus L_0\to \bar E
\]

be the quotient map. Let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\), viewed as a simple matroid on \(\bar E\).

Work first with affine tropical cones \(\widetilde{\Trop}(-)\subseteq \mathbb T^E\), \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\), including the all-\(\infty\) vector. Projectivization is obtained by quotienting by \(x\sim x+\lambda \mathbf 1\). If one deletes the all-\(\infty\) point projectively, the rank-zero spaces below become empty on both sides; the statements remain unchanged.

Define

\[
\Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\to \mathbb T^E
\]

by

\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty,& e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)},& e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]

This commutes with tropical scaling:

\[
\Delta(x+\lambda\mathbf 1)=\Delta(x)+\lambda\mathbf 1,
\]

so it descends to tropical projective space. It is injective, except for the harmless all-loop degenerate case where both sides have only the rank-zero object.

---

### 1. Zero-parallel extension of valuated matroids

Let \(\bar q\) be a rank \(k\) valuated matroid on \(\bar E\). Define its zero-parallel extension \(q=\Delta \bar q\) on \(E\) by

\[
q_I=
\begin{cases}
\bar q_{\pi(I)},&
I\cap L_0=\varnothing \text{ and } \pi|_I \text{ is injective},\\
\infty,&
\text{otherwise},
\end{cases}
\]

for every \(k\)-subset \(I\subseteq E\).

This is exactly the valuated matroid obtained from \(\bar q\) by adding the elements of \(L_0\) as loops and replacing each class \(a\in \bar E\) by a zero-offset parallel class.

The tropical Plücker relations for \(q\) reduce to those for \(\bar q\). Indeed, in a Plücker relation indexed by \(A\in \binom{E}{k-1}\) and \(B\in \binom{E}{k+1}\), a term can be finite only if both \(A\cup b\) and \(B-b\) avoid loops and meet every original parallel class at most once. If \(B\) has no repeated class, the relation is the projected relation for \(\bar q\). If \(B\) has exactly one repeated class, the only possibly finite terms are the two terms obtained by deleting one or the other copy, and those two terms are equal. All other obstructed cases give only \(\infty\)-terms. Thus \(q\) is a valuated matroid.

Moreover,

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(q)=\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\Trop}(\bar q)\bigr).
\]

The loop equations force \(x_\ell=\infty\) for \(\ell\in L_0\). The two-element zero-parallel circuits force \(x_e=x_f\) whenever \(e,f\) lie in the same original non-loop parallel class. After imposing these equations, the remaining valuated circuit equations are exactly the equations for \(\bar q\). Applying this to the constant valuated matroid \(S\) gives

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(M)=\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\Trop}(S)\bigr).
\]

---

### 2. Coordinate cocircuits

Let \(q\) be a rank \(k\) valuated matroid on \(E\). For \(k>0\) and \(A\in \binom{E}{k-1}\), define

\[
C_A^q(e)=
\begin{cases}
q_{A\cup e},& e\notin A,\\
\infty,& e\in A.
\end{cases}
\]

Then \(C_A^q\in \widetilde{\Trop}(q)\). This is just the tropical Plücker relation for \(q\): for every \(T\in \binom{E}{k+1}\),

\[
\min_{e\in T}\bigl(q_{T-e}+C_A^q(e)\bigr)
=
\min_{e\in T}\bigl(q_{T-e}+q_{A\cup e}\bigr)
\]

has no unique finite minimizer. If \(C_A^q\) is all \(\infty\), it is harmless; projectively it is simply ignored.

For \(k=0\), every element is a loop of \(q\), so the loop-forcing statements below are automatic.

---

### 3. Descent of a quotient of \(M\)

Let \(q\) be a rank \(k\) valuated matroid on \(E\) such that

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(q)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]

We prove that \(q\) descends uniquely to a valuated matroid on \(S\).

#### Loops remain loops

Assume \(k>0\). Let \(\ell\in L_0\). Every point of \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\) has \(\ell\)-coordinate \(\infty\), because \(\ell\) is a loop of \(M\).

If \(\ell\) were not a loop of \(q\), there would be a finite \(q\)-basis \(B\ni \ell\). Put \(A=B-\ell\). Then

\[
C_A^q(\ell)=q_B<\infty.
\]

But \(C_A^q\in \widetilde{\Trop}(q)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M)\), contradiction. Hence every loop of \(M\) is a loop of \(q\).

#### Original parallel classes are not split

Let \(e,f\in E\setminus L_0\) be parallel in \(M\). Then every point of \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\) satisfies

\[
x_e=x_f.
\]

Therefore, for every \(A\in \binom{E}{k-1}\),

\[
C_A^q(e)=C_A^q(f).
\]

Equivalently,

\[
q_{A\cup e}=q_{A\cup f},
\]

with the convention that \(q_{A\cup e}=\infty\) if \(e\in A\).

Consequences:

1. No finite \(q\)-basis contains both \(e\) and \(f\).  
   Indeed, if \(B\) contained both, take \(A=B-f\). Then \(e\in A\), so

   \[
   q_{A\cup e}=\infty,
   \]

   while

   \[
   q_{A\cup f}=q_B<\infty,
   \]

   contradiction.

2. Replacing one representative of an original parallel class by another preserves both finiteness and valuation.  
   If \(B=A\cup e\) is a finite \(q\)-basis and \(f\) lies in the same original class, then \(f\notin A\), and

   \[
   q_{A\cup e}=q_{A\cup f}.
   \]

Thus each original non-loop parallel class is either entirely looped in \(q\), or remains a zero-offset valuated parallel class.

#### Construction of the descended valuated matroid

Define a rank \(k\) valuated matroid \(\bar q\) on \(\bar E\) as follows. For a \(k\)-subset \(\bar I\subseteq \bar E\), choose any representative set \(I\subseteq E\setminus L_0\) mapping bijectively to \(\bar I\), and set

\[
\bar q_{\bar I}=q_I.
\]

This is independent of the choice of representatives by the equality \(q_{A\cup e}=q_{A\cup f}\) inside each original parallel class.

The tropical Plücker relations for \(\bar q\) are inherited from those for \(q\) by choosing representatives of the involved classes. Hence \(\bar q\) is a valuated matroid.

Moreover, \(q\) is exactly the zero-parallel extension of \(\bar q\):

\[
q_I=
\begin{cases}
\bar q_{\pi(I)},&
I\cap L_0=\varnothing \text{ and } \pi|_I \text{ is injective},\\
\infty,&
\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]

Therefore

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(q)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\Trop}(\bar q)\bigr).
\]

Since also

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(M)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\Trop}(S)\bigr),
\]

and \(\Delta\) is injective on the non-loop coordinates, the containment

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(q)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(M)
\]

implies

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(\bar q)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(S).
\]

Thus \(\bar q\) is a valuated quotient of \(S\). The construction is unique because the values \(\bar q_{\bar I}\) are forced by any representative set \(I\).

The rank-zero case is included: a rank-zero valuated matroid has only the empty basis and all elements are loops, so it descends to the unique rank-zero valuated matroid on \(\bar E\).

---

### 4. Converse: zero-parallel extension preserves quotients

Conversely, let \(\bar q\) be a valuated quotient of \(S\), so

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(\bar q)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(S).
\]

Let \(q=\Delta \bar q\) be its zero-parallel extension to \(E\). Then

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(q)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\Trop}(\bar q)\bigr)
\subseteq
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\Trop}(S)\bigr)
=
\widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]

Hence \(q\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).

---

### 5. Poset isomorphism of relative Dressians

Define

\[
\Delta_*:\Dr(S)\to \Dr(M)
\]

by

\[
\Delta_*\bigl(\Trop(\bar q)\bigr)
=
\Delta\bigl(\Trop(\bar q)\bigr)
=
\Trop(\Delta \bar q).
\]

The previous two steps show that \(\Delta_*\) is well-defined and surjective.

It is injective and order-reflecting because \(\Delta\) is injective:

\[
X_1\subseteq X_2
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\Delta(X_1)\subseteq \Delta(X_2).
\]

Therefore

\[
\Delta_*:\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M)
\]

is a poset isomorphism.

---

### 6. Compatibility with the embedded flat subposet

Flats of \(M\) are exactly \(L_0\) together with unions of non-loop parallel classes. Hence

\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\to \mathcal L(S),
\qquad
\psi(F)=\pi(F\setminus L_0),
\]

is a canonical lattice isomorphism, with inverse

\[
\psi^{-1}(\bar F)=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F).
\]

Let \(F\in \mathcal L(M)\), and let \(\bar F=\psi(F)\). Put

\[
N_F=M/F\oplus U_{0,F}
\]

on ground set \(E\), and

\[
\bar N_{\bar F}=S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}
\]

on ground set \(\bar E\). For every \(A\subseteq E\),

\[
r_{N_F}(A)
=
r_M(F\cup A)-r_M(F).
\]

Using

\[
r_M(A)=r_S(\pi(A\setminus L_0)),
\]

we get

\[
r_{N_F}(A)
=
r_S(\bar F\cup \pi(A\setminus L_0))-r_S(\bar F)
=
r_{\bar N_{\bar F}}\bigl(\pi(A\setminus L_0)\bigr).
\]

Thus \(N_F\) is exactly the zero-parallel extension of \(\bar N_{\bar F}\). Consequently,

\[
\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta\bigl(
\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})
\bigr).
\]

So the diagram

\[
\mathcal L(S)^{\mathrm{op}}\hookrightarrow \Dr(S)
\]

is carried by \(\Delta_*\) to

\[
\mathcal L(M)^{\mathrm{op}}\hookrightarrow \Dr(M).
\]

---

### 7. Transfer of anti-involutions

Suppose

\[
\alpha:\mathcal L(M)\to \mathcal L(M)
\]

is an order-reversing involution. Since \(\psi:\mathcal L(M)\to \mathcal L(S)\) is a lattice isomorphism, \(\alpha\) descends to

\[
\bar\alpha
=
\psi\circ \alpha\circ \psi^{-1}
:
\mathcal L(S)\to \mathcal L(S).
\]

This is again an order-reversing involution.

Now suppose one has constructed an order-reversing involution

\[
\Phi_S:\Dr(S)\to \Dr(S)
\]

extending \(\bar\alpha\). Then define

\[
\Phi_M
=
\Delta_*\circ \Phi_S\circ \Delta_*^{-1}.
\]

Because \(\Delta_*\) is a poset isomorphism, \(\Phi_M\) is an order-reversing involution of \(\Dr(M)\). By the flat-compatibility proved above, for every flat \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\),

\[
\Phi_M\bigl(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\Trop(M/\alpha(F)\oplus U_{0,\alpha(F)}).
\]

Thus \(\Phi_M\) extends the original anti-involution on \(\mathcal L(M)\).

So the existence problem for \(M\) is equivalent to the existence problem for its simplification \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\), with loops and parallel classes removed.

\[
q_I=
\begin{cases}

\[
q_I=
\begin{cases}

\[

=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\Trop}(\bar q)\bigr).

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(M)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\Trop}(S)\bigr),

\[

### 4. Converse: zero-parallel extension preserves quotients

Conversely, let \(\bar q\) be a valuated quotient of \(S\), so

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(\bar q)\subseteq \widetilde{\Trop}(S).
\]

Let \(q=\Delta \bar q\) be its zero-parallel extension to \(E\). Then

\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(q)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\Trop}(\bar q)\bigr)
\subseteq
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\Trop}(S)\bigr)
=
\widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]

Hence \(q\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).

---

### 5. Poset isomorphism of relative Dressians

Define

\[
\Delta_*:\Dr(S)\to \Dr(M)
\]

by

\[
\Delta_*\bigl(\Trop(\bar q)\bigr)
=
\Delta\bigl(\Trop(\bar q)\bigr)
=
\Trop(\Delta \bar q).
\]

The previous two steps show that \(\Delta_*\) is well-defined and surjective.

It is injective and order-reflecting because \(\Delta\) is injective:

\[
X_1\subseteq X_2
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\Delta(X_1)\subseteq \Delta(X_2).
\]

Therefore

\[
\Delta_*:\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M)
\]

is a poset isomorphism.

---

### 6. Compatibility with the embedded flat subposet

Flats of \(M\) are exactly \(L_0\) together with unions of non-loop parallel classes. Hence

\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\to \mathcal L(S),
\qquad
\psi(F)=\pi(F\setminus L_0),
\]

is a canonical lattice isomorphism, with inverse

\[
\psi^{-1}(\bar F)=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F).
\]

Let \(F\in \mathcal L(M)\), and let \(\bar F=\psi(F)\). Put

\[
N_F=M/F\oplus U_{0,F}
\]

on ground set \(E\), and

\[
\bar N_{\bar F}=S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}
\]

on ground set \(\bar E\). For every \(A\subseteq E\),

\[
r_{N_F}(A)
=
r_M(F\cup A)-r_M(F).
\]

Using

\[
r_M(A)=r_S(\pi(A\setminus L_0)),
\]

we get

\[
r_{N_F}(A)
=
r_S(\bar F\cup \pi(A\setminus L_0))-r_S(\bar F)
=
r_{\bar N_{\bar F}}\bigl(\pi(A\setminus L_0)\bigr).
\]

Thus \(N_F\) is exactly the zero-parallel extension of \(\bar N_{\bar F}\). Consequently,

\[
\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta\bigl(
\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})
\bigr).
\]

So the diagram

\[
\mathcal L(S)^{\mathrm{op}}\hookrightarrow \Dr(S)
\]

is carried by \(\Delta_*\) to

\[
\mathcal L(M)^{\mathrm{op}}\hookrightarrow \Dr(M).
\]

---

### 7. Transfer of anti-involutions

Suppose

\[
\alpha:\mathcal L(M)\to \mathcal L(M)
\]

is an order-reversing involution. Since \(\psi:\mathcal L(M)\to \mathcal L(S)\) is a lattice isomorphism, \(\alpha\) descends to

\[
\bar\alpha
=
\psi\circ \alpha\circ \psi^{-1}
:
\mathcal L(S)\to \mathcal L(S).
\]

This is again an order-reversing involution.

Now suppose one has constructed an order-reversing involution

\[
\Phi_S:\Dr(S)\to \Dr(S)
\]

extending \(\bar\alpha\). Then define

\[
\Phi_M
=
\Delta_*\circ \Phi_S\circ \Delta_*^{-1}.
\]

Because \(\Delta_*\) is a poset isomorphism, \(\Phi_M\) is an order-reversing involution of \(\Dr(M)\). By the flat-compatibility proved above, for every flat \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\),

\[
\Phi_M\bigl(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\Trop(M/\alpha(F)\oplus U_{0,\alpha(F)}).
\]

Thus \(\Phi_M\) extends the original anti-involution on \(\mathcal L(M)\).

So the existence problem for \(M\) is equivalent to the existence problem for its simplification \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\), with loops and parallel classes removed.
