solution:
## Original Problem

Let $M$ be a matroid, and let $\Trop(M)$ be the corresponding tropical linear space, i.e., the closure of the Bergman fan of $M$ in tropical projective space. The relative Dressian of $M$ is 
\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?

## Problem Solved

We prove the following fully self-contained statement.

Let \(M\) be a finite matroid on a finite ground set \(E\). Let \(\mathcal L(M)\) denote its lattice of flats, ordered by inclusion, and suppose \(\mathcal L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution
\[
\alpha:\mathcal L(M)\to \mathcal L(M),\qquad F\mapsto F^\perp .
\]
Work over the min-plus tropical semifield
\[
\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\},
\]
with tropical addition \(a\oplus b=\min(a,b)\) and tropical multiplication \(a\odot b=a+b\). Throughout, a tropical minimum condition “the minimum is attained at least twice” means: **there is no unique finite minimizer**; in particular, an all-\(\infty\) minimum is allowed.

For a rank-\(d\) valuated matroid \(p\) on \(E\), write \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \mathbb T^E\) for its affine tropical linear space, including the all-\(\infty\) vector, and write
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(p)
=
\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\setminus\{\infty^E\}\bigr)/\mathbb R(1,\dots,1)
\]
for its projectivization, possibly empty when \(d=0\). For an ordinary matroid \(N\), \(\operatorname{Trop}(N)\) means \(\operatorname{Trop}(p_N)\), where \(p_N\) is the constant valuated matroid finite exactly on bases of \(N\). A valuated matroid \(p\) is called a valuated quotient of the constant valuated matroid \(M\) exactly when
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M),
\]
equivalently \(\operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(M)\) after projectivization. Define
\[
\operatorname{Dr}(M)
=
\{\operatorname{Trop}(p): p \text{ is a valuated matroid quotient of }M\},
\]
ordered by inclusion of projective tropical linear spaces. We allow the empty projective tropical linear space coming from rank \(0\).

Then there exists an inclusion-reversing involution
\[
\Phi:\operatorname{Dr}(M)\to \operatorname{Dr}(M)
\]
such that, for every flat \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\),
\[
\Phi\!\left(\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}),
\]
where \(U_{0,F}\) is the rank-zero all-loop matroid on \(F\), so \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) is regarded as a matroid on the original ground set \(E\) by adding the elements of \(F\) back as loops.

This is exactly the original problem, with all affine/projective and rank-zero conventions made explicit; it is not a relaxation.

## Proof

### 0. Conventions and standard facts

A rank-\(d\) valuated matroid \(p\) on \(E\) is a function
\[
p:\binom Ed\to \mathbb T
\]
not identically \(\infty\), satisfying the tropical Plücker relations. We use the three-term form: for every \(S\in\binom E{d-2}\) and distinct \(a,b,c,d\notin S\),
\[
\min\bigl(
p_{Sab}+p_{Scd},\;
p_{Sac}+p_{Sbd},\;
p_{Sad}+p_{Sbc}
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. The rank \(0\) and full-rank cases have the evident empty families of relations.

The affine tropical linear space of \(p\) is
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)
=
\left\{
x\in\mathbb T^E:
\forall S\in\binom E{d+1},\ 
\min_{e\in S}\bigl(x_e+p_{S\setminus e}\bigr)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]
For \(d=0\), this gives \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)=\{\infty^E\}\). For \(d=|E|\), there are no equations, so \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)=\mathbb T^E\).

We use the following standard facts.

1. **Quotients and containment.** For valuated matroids \(p,q\) on the same ground set, \(p\) is a valuated quotient of \(q\) if and only if
   \[
   \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q).
   \]
   Equivalently after projectivization,
   \[
   \operatorname{Trop}(p)\subseteq \operatorname{Trop}(q).
   \]

2. **Circuit-incidence criterion for quotients of an ordinary matroid.** If \(M\) is an ordinary matroid and \(p\) is rank \(k\), then \(p\) is a valuated quotient of the constant valuated matroid \(M\) if and only if, for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\),
   \[
   \min_{c\in C\setminus A} p_{A\cup c}
   \]
   has no unique finite minimizer, with the convention that non-\(k\)-subsets have value \(\infty\).

3. **Coordinate cocircuits.** For rank \(k\) \(p\) and \(A\in\binom E{k-1}\), define
   \[
   C_A^p(e)=
   \begin{cases}
   p_{A\cup e},& e\notin A,\\
   \infty,& e\in A.
   \end{cases}
   \]
   If \(C_A^p\) is not identically \(\infty\), then \(C_A^p\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\). If it is identically \(\infty\), it is harmless in all minimum conditions.

4. **Tropical linear spaces determine Plücker vectors.** A projective tropical linear space \(\operatorname{Trop}(p)\) determines the valuated Plücker vector \(p\) up to adding a global scalar. Hence any construction on \(p\) that is compatible with global scalar translation descends to \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\).

5. **Superlevel-flat criterion for Bergman fans.** For an ordinary matroid \(N\), a vector \(w\in\mathbb T^{E(N)}\) lies in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N)\) if and only if, for every real \(\lambda\), the superlevel set
   \[
   \{e:w_e\ge \lambda\}
   \]
   is a flat of \(N\). This is equivalent to the no-unique-finite-minimum condition on every circuit.

---

# Part I. Reduction to the simplification

Let
\[
L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)
\]
be the set of loops of \(M\). Let \(\bar E\) be the set of non-loop parallel classes of \(M\), and let
\[
\pi:E\setminus L_0\to \bar E
\]
be the quotient map. Let
\[
S=\operatorname{si}(M)
\]
be the simplification of \(M\), viewed as a matroid on \(\bar E\).

## 1. Flat lattices

Every flat of \(M\) contains \(L_0\), and if a flat contains one element of a non-loop parallel class, it contains the whole class. Thus every flat of \(M\) is of the form
\[
F=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F)
\]
for a unique flat \(\bar F\) of \(S\). Explicitly,
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\to \mathcal L(S),\qquad
\psi(F)=\pi(F\setminus L_0)
\]
is a lattice isomorphism, with inverse
\[
\psi^{-1}(\bar F)=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F).
\]
This follows from the rank identity
\[
r_M(A)=r_S(\pi(A\setminus L_0)).
\]

Therefore the given order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(M)\) induces an order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(S)\):
\[
\bar\alpha
=
\psi\circ \alpha\circ \psi^{-1}.
\]

## 2. Modularity of \(S\)

The flat lattice \(\mathcal L(S)\) is a finite geometric lattice: it is finite, ranked, atomistic, and semimodular. Because \(\mathcal L(S)\) is anti-isomorphic to itself, its opposite lattice is also semimodular. Equivalently, \(\mathcal L(S)\) is both semimodular and lower semimodular.

Let \(\rho\) be its rank function. Semimodularity gives
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)\ge \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y),
\]
while lower semimodularity gives the reverse inequality. Hence equality holds for all flats \(X,Y\):
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)=\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Thus \(\mathcal L(S)\) is modular, and \(S\) is a finite simple modular matroid.

## 3. Diagonal zero-parallel extension

Define
\[
\Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\to \mathbb T^E
\]
by
\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty,& e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)},& e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]
This descends to projective tropical spaces.

If \(\bar p\) is a valuated matroid on \(\bar E\), its zero-parallel extension \(p=\Delta_*\bar p\) to \(E\) is defined by
\[
p_I=
\begin{cases}
\bar p_{\pi(I)},&
I\cap L_0=\varnothing\text{ and }\pi|_I\text{ is injective},\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\bar p)\bigr).
\]
Indeed, loops force coordinates equal to \(\infty\), zero-parallel pairs force equality of coordinates, and after imposing those equations the remaining circuit equations are exactly the circuit equations for \(\bar p\).

In particular,
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(S)\bigr).
\]

## 4. Descent of arbitrary quotients

Let \(q\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid on \(E\) such that
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M).
\]
We show that \(q\) is the zero-parallel extension of a unique valuated matroid \(\bar q\) on \(\bar E\).

First, every loop of \(M\) is a loop of \(q\). If \(e\in L_0\) were not a loop of \(q\), choose a finite \(q\)-basis \(B\ni e\), put \(A=B\setminus e\), and consider the coordinate cocircuit \(C_A^q\). Its \(e\)-coordinate is finite. But \(C_A^q\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\), while every vector in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\) has \(e\)-coordinate \(\infty\), contradiction.

Second, original parallel classes are not split. If \(e,f\) are parallel non-loops in \(M\), then \(\{e,f\}\) is an \(M\)-circuit, so every vector in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\) satisfies \(x_e=x_f\). Hence for every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\),
\[
C_A^q(e)=C_A^q(f).
\]
This implies:

- no finite \(q\)-basis contains two elements from the same original parallel class;
- replacing an element of a finite \(q\)-basis by another element of the same original parallel class preserves finiteness and the valuation;
- an entire original parallel class is either looped in \(q\), or is a zero-offset valuated parallel class.

Therefore we may define a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid \(\bar q\) on \(\bar E\) by choosing representatives:
\[
\bar q_{\bar I}=q_I,
\]
where \(I\subseteq E\setminus L_0\) maps bijectively to \(\bar I\). This is well-defined by the preceding paragraph, and the tropical Plücker relations for \(\bar q\) are obtained by lifting them to \(q\). Moreover \(q=\Delta_*\bar q\), so
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\bar q)\bigr).
\]
Because
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)
\subseteq
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)
=
\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(S)\bigr)
\]
and \(\Delta\) is injective, we get
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\bar q)
\subseteq
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(S).
\]
Thus \(\bar q\) is a valuated quotient of \(S\).

Consequently \(\Delta\) induces a poset isomorphism
\[
\Delta_*:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Dr}(M),
\qquad
\operatorname{Trop}(\bar p)\mapsto \operatorname{Trop}(\Delta_*\bar p).
\]

## 5. Compatibility with flat quotients

Let \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\), and put \(\bar F=\psi(F)\). Then
\[
F=L_0\cup \pi^{-1}(\bar F).
\]
Let
\[
N_F=M/F\oplus U_{0,F}
\]
as a matroid on \(E\), and let
\[
\bar N_{\bar F}=S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}
\]
as a matroid on \(\bar E\). For \(A\subseteq E\),
\[
r_{N_F}(A)
=
r_M(F\cup A)-r_M(F)
=
r_S(\bar F\cup \pi(A\setminus L_0))-r_S(\bar F)
=
r_{\bar N_{\bar F}}(\pi(A\setminus L_0)).
\]
Thus \(N_F\) is the zero-parallel extension of \(\bar N_{\bar F}\). Therefore
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(N_F)
=
\Delta\bigl(\operatorname{Trop}(\bar N_{\bar F})\bigr).
\]
So it remains to solve the theorem for finite simple modular matroids.

---

# Part II. The finite simple modular case

Assume now that \(M\) is finite, simple, loopless, modular, of rank \(r\), and that
\[
F\mapsto F^\perp
\]
is an order-reversing involution of \(\mathcal L(M)\). Since the involution reverses rank,
\[
r(F^\perp)=r-r(F).
\]

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\).

## 1. Flat-constancy of \(p\)

By the circuit-incidence criterion, for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\),
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A}p_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

First, if \(I\in\binom Ek\) is \(M\)-dependent, choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and \(c\in C\). Put \(A=I\setminus c\). Then \(C\setminus A=\{c\}\), so the minimum consists of the single term \(p_I\). Therefore \(p_I=\infty\).

Second, suppose \(I=A\cup a\) and \(I'=A\cup b\) are \(M\)-independent \(k\)-sets with the same \(M\)-closure. Then there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing both \(a\) and \(b\). Applying circuit-incidence to \(A\) and \(C\) gives
\[
p_{A\cup a}=p_{A\cup b}.
\]
Since the basis graph of the restriction \(M|F\) is connected for every flat \(F\), it follows that \(p_I\) is constant on the \(M\)-bases \(I\) of a fixed rank-\(k\) flat \(F\).

Thus, for each rank-\(k\) flat \(F\), define
\[
\bar p(F):=p_I,
\]
where \(I\) is any \(M\)-basis of \(F\).

Also, every \(M\)-basis contains a finite \(p\)-basis. Indeed, start from a finite \(p\)-basis \(I\), extend it to an \(M\)-basis, and move to any other \(M\)-basis along the basis graph of \(M\). Across an exchange \(B'=B''+b-e\), if the current finite \(p\)-basis \(I\subseteq B'\) does not contain \(b\), keep it. If \(b\in I\), put \(A=I\setminus b\). The fundamental circuit of \(e\) with respect to \(B'\) contains \(b\), and circuit-incidence supplies another \(c\) in that circuit with \(p_{A\cup c}<\infty\). Then \(A\cup c\subseteq B''\) is a finite \(p\)-basis.

## 2. Definition of \(p^\dagger\)

Put
\[
n=r-k.
\]
Define a function \(p^\dagger:\binom En\to\mathbb T\) by
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\bigr),
& J\text{ is }M\text{-independent},\\
\infty,& J\text{ is }M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]
This is not identically \(\infty\): if \(p_I<\infty\) and \(F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I)\), then any \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\) satisfies
\[
p^\dagger_J=\bar p(F)<\infty.
\]

The boundary ranks are immediate:

- If \(k=0\), then \(p^\dagger\) is a scalar translate of the constant valuated matroid \(M\).
- If \(k=r\), then \(p^\dagger\) is the unique rank-zero valuated matroid, up to scalar.

Henceforth assume \(0<k<r\).

## 3. Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\)

It is enough to check the three-term tropical Plücker relations. Let
\[
S\in\binom E{n-2}
\]
and let \(a,b,c,d\notin S\) be distinct.

If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, then all terms are \(\infty\). So assume \(S\) is independent, and put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S).
\]
If some \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\) lies in \(X\), then every Plücker matching contains a dependent \(n\)-set involving \(e\), so all three Plücker sums are \(\infty\). Thus assume \(a,b,c,d\notin X\).

Put
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Since \(r(X)=n-2\), we have \(r(Y)=k+2\). For \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define the hyperplane of \(Y\)
\[
H_e=(X\vee e)^\perp.
\]
For distinct \(e,f\),
\[
p^\dagger_{Sef}
=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_e\wedge H_f),& H_e\ne H_f,\\
\infty,& H_e=H_f.
\end{cases}
\]
Indeed, \(H_e=H_f\) exactly when \(S\cup\{e,f\}\) is \(M\)-dependent.

Thus the Plücker relation follows from the following local lemma.

### Lemma 3.1: Four hyperplanes in a modular interval

Let \(Y\) be a rank-\((k+2)\) flat of \(M\), and let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),& H_i\ne H_j,\\
\infty,& H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\ [13]+[24],\ [14]+[23]\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

#### Proof

If two hyperplanes coincide, say \(H_1=H_2\), then \([12]=\infty\), while
\[
[13]=[23],\qquad [14]=[24].
\]
Hence the last two Plücker sums are equal, and the relation holds.

Assume now that the \(H_i\) are distinct. Since \(M\) is modular, distinct hyperplanes of \(Y\) meet in rank \(k\). Put
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Its rank is \(k\), \(k-1\), or \(k-2\).

If \(r(Z)=k\), then every \(H_i\wedge H_j\) equals \(Z\), so all three sums equal \(2\bar p(Z)\).

If \(r(Z)=k-2\), define
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\ne i}H_j.
\]
Then each \(T_i\) has rank \(k-1\), and \(T_i\wedge T_j=Z\). If \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\), then
\[
T_m\vee T_n=H_i\wedge H_j.
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose \(t_i\in T_i\setminus Z\). Then
\[
A\cup\{t_m,t_n\}
\]
is an \(M\)-basis of \(H_i\wedge H_j\). The ordinary tropical Plücker relation for \(p\), applied to \(A,t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4\), is precisely the desired relation.

It remains to treat \(r(Z)=k-1\). Consider the rank-three interval \([Z,Y]\), and let \(N\) be its simplification. The points of \(N\) are the rank-\(k\) flats \(P\) satisfying
\[
Z<P<Y.
\]
Define
\[
w(P)=\bar p(P).
\]
We claim that \(w\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N)\). Let \(D\) be a circuit of \(N\). Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and for each \(P\in D\), choose \(e_P\in P\setminus Z\). A circuit of the contraction \(M/Z\) lifts to an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq Z\cup\{e_P:P\in D\}
\]
with
\[
C\setminus Z=\{e_P:P\in D\}.
\]
Applying circuit-incidence for \(p\) to \(A\) and \(C\), the terms from \(C\cap Z\) are \(\infty\), and the remaining terms are
\[
p_{A\cup e_P}=\bar p(P)=w(P).
\]
Thus \(\min_{P\in D}w(P)\) has no unique finite minimizer. Therefore \(w\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N)\).

Now the images of \(H_i\) in \(N\) are hyperplanes \(L_i\), and \(H_i\wedge H_j\) corresponds to the point \(L_i\wedge L_j\). The desired relation follows from the next lemma. ∎

### Lemma 3.2: Rank-three edge lemma

Let \(N\) be a finite simple modular rank-three matroid, let \(w\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N)\), and let \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) be hyperplanes of \(N\). Define
\[
\langle ij\rangle=
\begin{cases}
w(L_i\wedge L_j),& L_i\ne L_j,\\
\infty,& L_i=L_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl(
\langle12\rangle+\langle34\rangle,\;
\langle13\rangle+\langle24\rangle,\;
\langle14\rangle+\langle23\rangle
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

#### Proof

If two hyperplanes coincide, the same repeated-hyperplane argument as above applies.

Assume the \(L_i\) are distinct. If \(w\equiv\infty\), the claim is trivial. Otherwise subtract a global scalar so that \(\min w=0\). By the superlevel-flat criterion, the nonempty proper superlevel sets of \(w\) form a chain of flats in a rank-three geometry, hence have the form
\[
a\le B,
\]
where \(a\) is a point and \(B\) is a hyperplane, with one or both levels possibly absent. Thus
\[
w(x)=\alpha\,\mathbf 1_{\{x=a\}}+\beta\,\mathbf 1_{\{x\le B\}}
\]
for some \(\alpha,\beta\in[0,\infty]\).

First suppose none of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\). Put
\[
y_i=L_i\wedge B.
\]
For distinct \(i,j\),
\[
L_i\wedge L_j\le B
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
y_i=y_j.
\]
Thus a pair \(\{i,j\}\) contributes nonzero cost exactly when the colors \(y_i,y_j\) agree; the cost is \(\beta\), or \(\alpha+\beta\) if the common color is \(a\). For four colored objects, the minimum perfect-matching cost is never unique: according to the color multiplicities \(1+1+1+1\), \(2+1+1\), \(2+2\), \(3+1\), or \(4\), either all matchings tie or at least two matchings avoid the repeated color and tie for the minimum.

Now suppose \(L_1=B\). For \(j=2,3,4\), put
\[
y_j=L_j\wedge B,
\]
and set
\[
c(y)=
\begin{cases}
\alpha+\beta,& y=a,\\
\beta,& y\ne a.
\end{cases}
\]
The three matching costs are
\[
c(y_2)+[y_3=y_4]c(y_3),
\]
\[
c(y_3)+[y_2=y_4]c(y_2),
\]
\[
c(y_4)+[y_2=y_3]c(y_2).
\]
If \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) are all distinct, at least two of the three costs are \(\beta\). If exactly two are equal, the two matchings pairing \(B\) with one of that equal pair tie for the minimum. If all three are equal, all three costs are equal. Hence the minimum is never uniquely finite. ∎

This proves the tropical Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\).

## 4. Quotient relations for \(p^\dagger\)

We now prove that \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\). By the circuit-incidence criterion, it suffices to prove that for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \((n-1)\)-subset \(A\),
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A}p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, then all terms are \(\infty\). So assume \(A\) is independent. Put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A),\qquad Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(r(X)=n-1\) and \(r(Y)=k+1\).

We need one lemma.

### Lemma 4.1: Codimension-one polarity lemma

Let \(X\) be a rank-\((n-1)\) flat and \(Y=X^\perp\). Let \(N\) be the simplification of the contraction \(M/X\). The points of \(N\) are the flats \(P\) covering \(X\). Define
\[
u(P)=\bar p(P^\perp).
\]
Then
\[
u\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N).
\]

#### Proof

By the superlevel-flat criterion, it is enough to prove that every superlevel set
\[
\mathcal S_\lambda=\{P:u(P)\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(N\).

We first prove \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is line-closed. Let \(P_1,P_2\in\mathcal S_\lambda\), and let \(P_3\) be a point on the line \(P_1\vee P_2\) in \(N\). If \(P_3=P_1\) or \(P_3=P_2\), there is nothing to prove.

Assume \(P_1,P_2,P_3\) are distinct. Put
\[
H_i=P_i^\perp\le Y.
\]
Then \(H_i\) are hyperplanes of \(Y\). Since \(P_3\le P_1\vee P_2\), polarity gives
\[
H_3=P_3^\perp\supseteq (P_1\vee P_2)^\perp
=
H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
Let
\[
K=H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
Then \(r(K)=k-1\). Choose an \(M\)-basis \(B\) of \(K\), and choose \(e_i\in H_i\setminus K\) with
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(B\cup e_i)=H_i.
\]
The three \(H_i/K\) are distinct points on a rank-two line, so they form a circuit in the simplification of \(M/K\). Hence there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
D\subseteq K\cup\{e_1,e_2,e_3\}
\]
with
\[
D\setminus K=\{e_1,e_2,e_3\}.
\]
Apply circuit-incidence for \(p\) to the \((k-1)\)-set \(B\) and the circuit \(D\). Terms from \(D\cap K\) are \(\infty\), and
\[
p_{B\cup e_i}=\bar p(H_i)=u(P_i).
\]
Thus
\[
\min\{u(P_1),u(P_2),u(P_3)\}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Since \(u(P_1),u(P_2)\ge \lambda\), we must have \(u(P_3)\ge \lambda\). Thus \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is line-closed.

In a finite simple modular matroid, every line-closed set of points is a flat. Indeed, let \(S\) be line-closed. If \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_m\}\subseteq S\) is independent, we show by induction on \(m\) that every point of \(\operatorname{cl}(B)\) lies in \(S\). The cases \(m\le 2\) are exactly line-closedness. For \(m\ge 3\), put
\[
G=\operatorname{cl}(b_1,\dots,b_{m-1}),\qquad F=G\vee b_m.
\]
By induction, every point of \(G\) lies in \(S\). Let \(p\) be a point of \(F\). If \(p\le G\) or \(p=b_m\), we are done. Otherwise, the line \(\ell=p\vee b_m\) satisfies, by modularity,
\[
r(\ell\wedge G)=r(\ell)+r(G)-r(F)=2+(m-1)-m=1.
\]
So \(\ell\wedge G\) is a point \(q\in S\). Since \(q,b_m\in S\), line-closedness gives every point of \(\ell=q\vee b_m\), including \(p\), in \(S\).

Therefore \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is a flat for every \(\lambda\), so \(u\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(N)\). ∎

We now finish the quotient proof. Let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit. Elements of \(C\cap X\) contribute \(\infty\). For \(c\notin X\),
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
=
\bar p((X\vee c)^\perp)
=
u(X\vee c).
\]
The vector \(u\) lifts from the simplification of \(M/X\) to a vector in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M/X)\). The set \(C\setminus X\) is a union of circuits in \(M/X\): for every \(e\in C\setminus X\),
\[
e\in \operatorname{cl}_{M/X}\bigl((C\setminus X)\setminus e\bigr).
\]
If the minimum of \(p^\dagger_{A\cup c}\) over \(c\in C\setminus A\) had a unique finite minimizer \(e\), then some circuit of \(M/X\) contained in \(C\setminus X\) and containing \(e\) would have a unique finite minimum, contradicting \(u\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M/X)\). Hence \(p^\dagger\) satisfies the quotient circuit-incidence relations.

Thus \(p^\dagger\) is a rank-\(r-k\) valuated quotient of \(M\).

## 5. Involutivity

Because \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\), the same flat-constancy argument applies to it.

Let \(I\in\binom Ek\). If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \(p_I=\infty\), and by definition
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I=\infty.
\]
If \(I\) is \(M\)-independent, put
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I).
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]
Therefore
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]

## 6. Compatibility with flat quotients

Let \(F\) be a flat of \(M\), and let \(p^F\) be the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
Its rank is \(r-r(F)\). For a rank-\((r-r(F))\) flat \(G\),
\[
\bar p^F(G)<\infty
\]
if and only if \(G\) is a lattice complement of \(F\):
\[
G\wedge F=\hat 0,\qquad G\vee F=E.
\]
Now let \(H\) be a rank-\(r(F)\) flat. Then \((p^F)^\dagger\) is finite on an \(M\)-basis of \(H\) precisely when \(H^\perp\) is a complement of \(F\). By polarity, this is equivalent to \(H\) being a complement of \(F^\perp\). Therefore
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}
\]
up to the same global scalar normalization. Hence dagger sends the flat quotient indexed by \(F\) to the flat quotient indexed by \(F^\perp\).

---

# Part III. Point-annihilator duality and order reversal

We now prove that dagger reverses inclusions.

## 1. Boolean apartments and polar bases

Let
\[
B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}
\]
be an \(M\)-basis. Define
\[
H_i=\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus b_i),
\qquad
b_i^*=H_i^\perp.
\]
Since \(H_i\) is a hyperplane, \(b_i^*\) is an atom, hence an element of \(E\).

For every \(S\subseteq [r]\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\in S\}
=
\bigwedge_{i\notin S}H_i.
\]
Applying polarity gives
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\in S\}^{\perp}
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\notin S\}.
\]
In particular,
\[
B^*=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}
\]
is again an \(M\)-basis, and \((B^*)^*=B\).

For a rank-\(k\) quotient \(p\), define the restriction to the Boolean apartment \(B\):
\[
\mu_B(T)=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}},
\qquad |T|=k.
\]
By the finite-basis lemma above, \(\mu_B\) is a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid on \([r]\). Moreover,
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=\mu_B^*,
\]
the ordinary dual valuated matroid. Indeed, for \(|R|=r-k\),
\[
p^\dagger_{\{b_i^*:i\in R\}}
=
\bar p\!\left(\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in R\}^{\perp}\right)
=
\bar p\!\left(\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin R\}\right)
=
p_{\{b_i:i\notin R\}}
=
\mu_B([r]\setminus R).
\]

## 2. Ordinary tropical annihilator duality

Let \(\mu\) be a valuated matroid on a finite set \(N\). For \(u\in\mathbb T^N\), define the tropical hyperplane
\[
H_u=
\left\{
v\in\mathbb T^N:
\min_{i\in N}(u_i+v_i)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]
Then
\[
u\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu^*)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu)\subseteq H_u.
\]

Indeed, the circuit equations for \(\mu^*\) are exactly orthogonality of \(u\) to the coordinate cocircuits of \(\mu\). The affine tropical linear space \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu)\) is the closed tropical convex hull of those coordinate cocircuits, and \(H_u\) is a closed tropically convex set. Hence \(H_u\) contains \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu)\) exactly when it contains all coordinate cocircuits, which is exactly \(u\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu^*)\). The rank \(0\) and full-rank cases agree with this statement under the all-\(\infty\) convention.

## 3. The affine polar hyperplane \(\widetilde H_x\)

For \(x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\), define
\[
\widetilde H_x\subseteq \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)
\]
by
\[
y\in\widetilde H_x
\]
if and only if, for every \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\),
\[
\min_i\bigl(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(x=\infty^E\), then \(\widetilde H_x=\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\).

## 4. Affine point-annihilator theorem

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). For every
\[
x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M),
\]
we have
\[
x\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]

### Proof for \(0<k<r\)

Assume first that
\[
x\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger).
\]
Let \(y\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\). Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\). Put
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
Then
\[
u_B\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger|_{B^*})
=
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*).
\]
Also
\[
(y_{b_1},\dots,y_{b_r})\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B).
\]
By ordinary tropical annihilator duality,
\[
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Since this holds for every \(M\)-basis \(B\), we have \(y\in\widetilde H_x\). Thus
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]

Conversely, assume
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), and again put
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
We prove
\[
u_B\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*).
\]

Let \(T\subseteq [r]\) have size \(r-k+1\), and put \(S=[r]\setminus T\), so \(|S|=k-1\). Let
\[
A=\{b_i:i\in S\}.
\]
The coordinate cocircuit \(C_A^p\) lies in \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\), unless it is identically \(\infty\), in which case the desired relation is automatic. Since \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x\), applying the defining equation of \(\widetilde H_x\) for the basis \(B\) gives
\[
\min_{i\in T}\bigl(p_{A\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. But this is exactly the circuit equation for \(u_B\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(\mu_B^*)\), because
\[
(\mu_B^*)_{T\setminus i}
=
\mu_B([r]\setminus(T\setminus i))
=
\mu_B(S\cup i)
=
p_{A\cup b_i}.
\]
Thus
\[
x|_{B^*}\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger|_{B^*}).
\]
Since \(B\mapsto B^*\) is an involution on \(M\)-bases, \(x\) restricts to \(\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)\) on every \(M\)-basis.

Now check the global circuit equations for \(p^\dagger\). Let \(J\subseteq E\) have size \(r-k+1\).

If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, extend \(J\) to an \(M\)-basis \(C\). Since \(x|_C\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger|_C)\), the circuit equation on \(J\) holds.

If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent and \(r_M(J)<r-k\), then all \(J\setminus j\) are \(M\)-dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\). If \(r_M(J)=r-k\), then \(J\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit \(C\). The finite terms in the \(p^\dagger\)-circuit equation are exactly those indexed by \(j\in C\), and for those \(j\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(J\setminus j)=\operatorname{cl}_M(J),
\]
so \(p^\dagger_{J\setminus j}\) is constant. The equation reduces to the \(M\)-circuit equation
\[
\min_{j\in C}x_j
\]
having no unique finite minimizer, which holds because \(x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\).

Therefore
\[
x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger).
\]

### Boundary ranks

If \(k=0\), then
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)=\{\infty^E\},
\]
and \(p^\dagger\) is a scalar translate of \(M\). Hence both sides of the equivalence hold for every \(x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\).

If \(k=r\), then \(p\) is a scalar translate of \(M\), and \(p^\dagger\) has rank \(0\). Thus
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger)=\{\infty^E\}.
\]
If \(x=\infty^E\), then \(\widetilde H_x=\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\). Conversely, if
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(M)\subseteq \widetilde H_x,
\]
take an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), an index \(i\), and \(A=B\setminus b_i\). The coordinate cocircuit \(C_A^p\) has only one possibly finite coordinate on \(B\), namely at \(b_i\). The defining equation of \(\widetilde H_x\) for \(B\) forces
\[
x_{b_i^*}=\infty.
\]
As \(B\) and \(i\) vary, the elements \(b_i^*\) cover \(E\). Hence \(x=\infty^E\). This proves the boundary case.

Thus the affine point-annihilator theorem holds for all \(k\).

## 5. Order reversal

Suppose
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)
\subseteq
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q).
\]
Let \(x\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q^\dagger)\). By the point-annihilator theorem,
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]
Therefore
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]
Applying the point-annihilator theorem again gives
\[
x\in \widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger).
\]
Hence
\[
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(q^\dagger)
\subseteq
\widetilde{\operatorname{Trop}}(p^\dagger).
\]
After projectivization,
\[
\operatorname{Trop}(q^\dagger)
\subseteq
\operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]

Because \(p\mapsto p^\dagger\) is involutive and respects global scalar translation, it defines an inclusion-reversing involution
\[
\Phi_M:\operatorname{Dr}(M)\to\operatorname{Dr}(M),
\qquad
\Phi_M(\operatorname{Trop}(p))=\operatorname{Trop}(p^\dagger),
\]
for finite simple modular \(M\). By the compatibility with flat quotients,
\[
\Phi_M\!\left(\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

---

# Part IV. Transfer back to an arbitrary finite matroid

Return to the original finite matroid \(M\). Let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\). From Part I, we have:

- a lattice isomorphism
  \[
  \psi:\mathcal L(M)\xrightarrow{\sim}\mathcal L(S),
  \]
  transporting \(F\mapsto F^\perp\) to an order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(S)\);

- a poset isomorphism
  \[
  \Delta_*:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Dr}(M);
  \]

- compatibility with flat quotients:
  \[
  \Delta_*\!\left(\operatorname{Trop}(S/\psi(F)\oplus U_{0,\psi(F)})\right)
  =
  \operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}).
  \]

Since \(S\) is finite simple modular, Part III gives an inclusion-reversing involution
\[
\Phi_S:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\to\operatorname{Dr}(S)
\]
extending the induced flat-lattice involution. Define
\[
\Phi_M
=
\Delta_*\circ \Phi_S\circ \Delta_*^{-1}.
\]
Then \(\Phi_M\) is an inclusion-reversing involution of \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\).

Finally, for every flat \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\),
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Phi_M\!\left(\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
&=
\Delta_*\Phi_S\!\left(
\operatorname{Trop}(S/\psi(F)\oplus U_{0,\psi(F)})
\right)\\
&=
\Delta_*\!\left(
\operatorname{Trop}(S/\psi(F^\perp)\oplus U_{0,\psi(F^\perp)})
\right)\\
&=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\end{aligned}
\]
This proves the theorem. ∎
self_declared_problem_solved: For every finite matroid M on ground set E whose lattice of flats admits an order-reversing involution F -> F^perp, with Dr(M) defined as the poset of projective tropical linear spaces Trop(p) for valuated matroid quotients p of the constant valuated matroid M, ordered by inclusion and with the rank-zero empty projective tropical space allowed, there is an inclusion-reversing involution Phi on Dr(M) such that Phi(Trop(M/F plus U_{0,F})) = Trop(M/F^perp plus U_{0,F^perp}) for every flat F of M.
advisor_statement: For every finite matroid M whose lattice of flats admits an order-reversing involution F -> F^perp, the relative Dressian Dr(M), ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces, admits an inclusion-reversing involution Phi extending F -> F^perp on the embedded flat subposet F -> Trop(M/F ⊕ U_{0,F}).
writeup_declared_problem_solved: For every finite matroid M on ground set E whose lattice of flats admits an order-reversing involution F -> F^perp, with Dr(M) defined as the poset of projective tropical linear spaces Trop(p) for valuated matroid quotients p of the constant valuated matroid M, ordered by inclusion and with the rank-zero empty projective tropical space allowed, there is an inclusion-reversing involution Phi on Dr(M) such that Phi(Trop(M/F plus U_{0,F})) = Trop(M/F^perp plus U_{0,F^perp}) for every flat F of M.

description: writeup (full problem): For every finite matroid M whose lattice of flats admits an order-reversing involution F -> F^perp, the relative Dressian Dr(M), ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces contained in Trop(M), admits an inclusion-reversing involution extending the given involution on the embedded copy of L(M)^op.
full_text:
## Original Problem

Let $M$ be a matroid, and let $\Trop(M)$ be the corresponding tropical linear space, i.e., the closure of the Bergman fan of $M$ in tropical projective space. The relative Dressian of $M$ is 
\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?

## Problem Solved

Let \(M\) be a finite matroid on a finite ground set \(E\). Write \(\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}\), with min-plus tropical conventions, and let \(\Trop(M)\) denote the projective tropical linear space obtained from the affine Bergman cone of \(M\) by deleting the all-\(\infty\) vector and quotienting by tropical scalar translation. Let \(\Dr(M)\) be the poset, ordered by inclusion, whose elements are projective tropical linear spaces \(X\subseteq\Trop(M)\), equivalently \(X=\Trop(p)\) for a valuated matroid quotient \(p\) of the constant valuated matroid associated to \(M\). For a flat \(F\) of \(M\), let \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) mean the matroid on the original ground set \(E\) whose restriction to \(E\setminus F\) is the contraction \(M/F\) and whose elements in \(F\) are loops. If the lattice of flats \(\mathcal L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution \(F\mapsto F^\perp\), then there exists an inclusion-reversing involution
\[
\Phi:\Dr(M)\to\Dr(M)
\]
such that, for every flat \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\),
\[
\Phi\!\left(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]
This establishes the original finite-ground-set problem without relaxation.

## Proof

### 1. Affine tropical conventions and standard facts

We work first affinely. Let
\[
\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}.
\]
All tropical arithmetic is min-plus. Throughout, a statement that a minimum is “attained twice” means precisely:

> the displayed minimum has no unique finite minimizer.

Thus an all-\(\infty\) minimum is allowed.

A rank \(d\) valuated matroid \(q\) on \(E\) is a function
\[
q:\binom Ed\to\mathbb T
\]
not identically \(\infty\), satisfying the tropical Plücker relations. We extend \(q\) by \(q_I=\infty\) whenever \(|I|\ne d\). Its affine tropical linear space is
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(q)
=
\left\{
z\in\mathbb T^E:
\forall J\in\binom E{d+1},\
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(z_j+q_{J-j}\bigr)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}
\right\}.
\]
This affine cone includes the all-\(\infty\) vector \(\infty^E\).

Boundary ranks are interpreted as follows.

- If \(d=0\), then the equations for one-element sets force every coordinate of \(z\) to be \(\infty\). Hence
  \[
  \widetilde{\Trop}(q)=\{\infty^E\}.
  \]
- If \(d=|E|\), there are no equations, so
  \[
  \widetilde{\Trop}(q)=\mathbb T^E.
  \]

The projective tropical linear space \(\Trop(q)\) is obtained from \(\widetilde{\Trop}(q)\) by deleting \(\infty^E\) and quotienting by tropical scalar translation \(z\sim z+\lambda\mathbf 1\), \(\lambda\in\mathbb R\). In particular, a rank-zero affine tropical space projectivizes to the empty projective space.

For an ordinary matroid \(M\) of rank \(r\), we identify \(M\) with the constant valuated matroid
\[
p^M_B=
\begin{cases}
0,&B\text{ is an \(M\)-basis},\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
Then \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M):=\widetilde{\Trop}(p^M)\) is the affine Bergman cone of \(M\), including \(\infty^E\).

We use the following standard facts from valuated matroid theory.

**Standard fact 1: quotients and containment.**  
For valuated matroids \(p,q\) on the same finite ground set,
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde{\Trop}(q)
\]
if and only if \(p\) is a valuated matroid quotient of \(q\).

When \(q=M\) is an ordinary matroid regarded as a constant valuated matroid, this quotient condition is equivalent, for rank \(k>0\) \(p\), to the circuit-incidence relations
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A} p_{A\cup c}
\]
having no unique finite minimizer for every circuit \(C\) of \(M\) and every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\). Rank-zero valuated matroids are quotients of every \(M\), since their affine tropical space is \(\{\infty^E\}\).

The support
\[
\operatorname{supp}(p)=\{I\in\binom Ek:p_I<\infty\}
\]
is the set of bases of an ordinary matroid \(N\), and if \(p\) is a quotient of \(M\), then \(N\) is an ordinary quotient of \(M\). Equivalently,
\[
\operatorname{rk}_N(A)=\operatorname{rk}_N(\operatorname{cl}_M(A))
\qquad
\text{for every }A\subseteq E.
\]

**Standard fact 2: coordinate cocircuits.**  
If \(p\) has rank \(k>0\), then for every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\), the vector
\[
D_A^p(e)=
\begin{cases}
p_{A\cup e},&e\notin A,\\
\infty,&e\in A,
\end{cases}
\]
belongs to \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\), possibly as the all-\(\infty\) vector.

**Standard fact 3: ordinary affine annihilator duality.**  
Let \(\mu\) be a valuated matroid on a finite set \(N\), and let \(\mu^*\) be its ordinary valuated dual:
\[
\mu^*_R=\mu_{N\setminus R}.
\]
For \(u\in\mathbb T^N\), define
\[
H_u=\{v\in\mathbb T^N:\min_{i\in N}(u_i+v_i)
\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}\}.
\]
Then
\[
u\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu^*)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu)\subseteq H_u.
\]
This includes the rank-zero and full-rank cases.

**Standard fact 4: Plücker uniqueness.**  
A tropical linear space \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\) determines the valuated Plücker vector \(p\) up to addition of a global tropical scalar.

We shall also use the standard Bergman fan criterion: for a finite simple matroid \(G\), a vector \(w\in\mathbb T^{E(G)}\) lies in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(G)\) if and only if, for every real \(\lambda\), the superlevel set
\[
\{e:w_e\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(G\).

---

### 2. Reduction to the simplification

Let \(M\) be a finite matroid on \(E\). Let
\[
L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)
\]
be its set of loops. Let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\) be the simplification of \(M\). Thus the ground set \(\bar E\) of \(S\) is the set of non-loop parallel classes of \(M\), and we write
\[
\pi:E\setminus L_0\to\bar E
\]
for the quotient map.

Define
\[
\Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\to\mathbb T^E
\]
by
\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty,&e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)},&e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]
This map commutes with tropical scalar translation, so it descends to projective tropical spaces.

We prove that \(\Delta\) induces a poset isomorphism
\[
\Delta_*:\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M).
\]

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\). If \(k=0\), then \(p\) descends uniquely to the rank-zero valuated matroid on \(\bar E\). Assume \(k>0\).

First, every loop of \(M\) remains a loop of \(p\). Indeed, suppose \(\ell\in L_0\) belonged to a finite \(p\)-basis \(B\). Let \(A=B-\ell\). Then the coordinate cocircuit \(D_A^p\) belongs to \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\), but
\[
D_A^p(\ell)=p_B<\infty.
\]
This contradicts the loop equation for \(\ell\) in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\), which forces the \(\ell\)-coordinate to be \(\infty\). Hence \(p_{A\cup \ell}=\infty\) for all \(A\).

Second, every finite \(p\)-basis is \(M\)-independent. If a finite \(p\)-basis \(I\) contained an \(M\)-circuit \(C\), choose \(c\in C\) and set \(A=I-c\). Then \(D_A^p\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\), but on the circuit \(C\) its only finite coordinate is
\[
D_A^p(c)=p_I<\infty,
\]
because \(C-c\subseteq A\). This violates the circuit equation for \(C\) in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\).

Third, original parallel classes cannot be split, and there is no internal valuated offset. Let \(e,f\) be parallel nonloops of \(M\). The two-element set \(\{e,f\}\) is a circuit of \(M\), so every vector in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\) satisfies equality of the two coordinates. Hence for every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\),
\[
D_A^p(e)=D_A^p(f).
\]
Consequently,
\[
p_{A\cup e}=p_{A\cup f},
\]
with the convention that the value is \(\infty\) if the union is not a \(k\)-set.

This has two consequences. No finite \(p\)-basis contains both \(e\) and \(f\): if \(B\) did, take \(A=B-f\), so \(e\in A\), and obtain
\[
p_B=D_A^p(f)=D_A^p(e)=\infty.
\]
Also, if \(B=A\cup e\) is a finite \(p\)-basis and \(f\) is in the same original parallel class, then \(f\notin A\), and
\[
p_{A\cup f}=p_{A\cup e}.
\]

Therefore \(p\) descends to a unique valuated matroid \(\bar p\) on \(\bar E\), defined by
\[
\bar p_{\bar I}=p_I
\]
for any representative set \(I\subseteq E\setminus L_0\) mapping bijectively to \(\bar I\). The preceding paragraph shows that this is independent of the representatives. The Plücker relations for \(\bar p\) are inherited from those for \(p\), and the quotient circuit-incidence relations for \(S\) are inherited by choosing representatives of circuits of \(S\). Hence \(\bar p\) is a valuated quotient of \(S\).

Conversely, let \(q\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(S\). Define its zero-parallel extension \(q^\uparrow\) to \(E\) by
\[
q^\uparrow_I=
\begin{cases}
q_{\pi(I)},&
I\cap L_0=\varnothing\text{ and }\pi|_I\text{ is injective},\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
The tropical Plücker relations for \(q^\uparrow\) reduce either to the corresponding relations for \(q\), or to relations with two equal finite terms, or to all-\(\infty\) relations. Hence \(q^\uparrow\) is a valuated matroid. Its affine tropical linear space is
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(q^\uparrow)=\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\Trop}(q)\bigr).
\]
Indeed, loop equations impose \(\infty\) on \(L_0\), two-element zero-parallel circuits impose equality within each original parallel class, and after those equations are imposed the remaining circuit equations are exactly the equations for \(q\).

In particular, since
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(M)=\Delta\bigl(\widetilde{\Trop}(S)\bigr),
\]
if \(q\) is a quotient of \(S\), then \(q^\uparrow\) is a quotient of \(M\).

Thus \(\Delta\) identifies affine quotient tropical linear spaces of \(S\) with those of \(M\). After projectivizing, it gives a poset isomorphism
\[
\Delta_*:\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M).
\]
If \(M\) is all loops, then \(S\) is the empty matroid and the projective space \(\Trop(M)\) is empty; the Dressian has only the empty object, and the desired involution is trivial. Henceforth the all-loop case is harmless.

We also need compatibility with the embedded flat subposet. Flats of \(M\) are exactly \(L_0\) together with unions of nonloop parallel classes. Thus there is a lattice isomorphism
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\xrightarrow{\sim}\mathcal L(S),
\qquad
\psi(F)=\pi(F\setminus L_0).
\]
Let \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\) and let \(\bar F=\psi(F)\). The matroid \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) is the zero-parallel extension of \(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}\). Indeed, for every \(A\subseteq E\),
\[
\operatorname{rk}_{M/F\oplus U_{0,F}}(A)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(F\cup A)-\operatorname{rk}_M(F),
\]
while
\[
\operatorname{rk}_M(B)
=
\operatorname{rk}_S\bigl(\pi(B\setminus L_0)\bigr).
\]
Therefore
\[
\operatorname{rk}_{M/F\oplus U_{0,F}}(A)
=
\operatorname{rk}_{S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}}
\bigl(\pi(A\setminus L_0)\bigr).
\]
Hence
\[
\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta\!\left(\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\right).
\]

So it suffices to prove the theorem for simple matroids.

---

### 3. The simple case is modular

Assume now that \(M\) is simple. Its lattice of flats \(\mathcal L(M)\) is a finite geometric lattice, hence semimodular:
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)
\ge
\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y)
\]
for all flats \(X,Y\), where \(\rho\) is the lattice rank.

If \(\mathcal L(M)\) admits an order-reversing involution
\[
F\mapsto F^\perp,
\]
then \(\mathcal L(M)\) is anti-isomorphic to itself. Hence its dual lattice is also semimodular. Semimodularity of the dual gives the reverse inequality
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)
\le
\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
Thus equality holds:
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)
=
\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y)
\]
for all flats \(X,Y\). Therefore \(M\) is modular.

If \(r=\operatorname{rk}(M)\), then the order-reversing involution satisfies
\[
\operatorname{rk}(F^\perp)=r-\operatorname{rk}(F).
\]
In particular, hyperplanes are sent to atoms, and because \(M\) is simple, atoms are single elements.

It remains to prove the theorem when \(M\) is finite, simple, loopless, modular, of rank \(r\), and equipped with a flat-lattice polarity \(F\mapsto F^\perp\).

---

### 4. Flat constancy for quotients in the simple modular case

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\).

If \(I\in\binom Ek\) is \(M\)-dependent, then
\[
p_I=\infty.
\]
Indeed, choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and \(c\in C\). Let \(A=I-c\). Then \(C\setminus A=\{c\}\), so the circuit-incidence relation forces
\[
p_I=p_{A\cup c}=\infty.
\]

Next suppose \(I,I'\in\binom Ek\) are \(M\)-independent and span the same rank \(k\) flat. It is enough to treat adjacent bases
\[
I=A\cup a,
\qquad
I'=A\cup b.
\]
Since
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(Aa)=\operatorname{cl}_M(Ab),
\]
there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing both \(a\) and \(b\). Applying circuit incidence to \(C\) and \(A\), we get
\[
\min\bigl(p_{A\cup a},p_{A\cup b}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. Hence
\[
p_{A\cup a}=p_{A\cup b}.
\]
Connectivity of the basis graph of the restriction of \(M\) to a flat gives:

> If \(I,J\) are \(M\)-bases of the same rank \(k\) flat \(X\), then \(p_I=p_J\).

Thus for every rank \(k\) flat \(X\) of \(M\), we may define
\[
\bar p(X)=p_I
\]
for any \(M\)-basis \(I\) of \(X\).

---

### 5. The dagger construction

Let \(p\) be a rank \(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\), and set
\[
n=r-k.
\]
Define a rank \(n\) function \(p^\dagger\) on \(E\) by
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p\bigl(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp\bigr),
&J\in\binom En\text{ is \(M\)-independent},\\
\infty,
&J\in\binom En\text{ is \(M\)-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]
When \(k=0\), this is a scalar translate of the constant valuated matroid \(M\). When \(k=r\), this is the rank-zero valuated matroid. We now prove that for all \(k\), \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).

The boundary cases \(k=0\) and \(k=r\) are immediate from the definition, so for the Plücker and quotient-incidence proofs assume \(0<k<r\).

---

### 6. Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\)

It suffices to check the three-term valuated Plücker relations. Let
\[
S\in\binom E{n-2},
\]
and let \(a,b,c,d\notin S\) be distinct. We must prove that
\[
\min\left(
p^\dagger_{Sab}+p^\dagger_{Scd},
p^\dagger_{Sac}+p^\dagger_{Sbd},
p^\dagger_{Sad}+p^\dagger_{Sbc}
\right)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, then all six \(n\)-subsets in the displayed relation are \(M\)-dependent, so all three sums are \(\infty\). Assume \(S\) is \(M\)-independent and set
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S).
\]
If one of \(a,b,c,d\) lies in \(X\), then in every opposite pairing one of the two \(n\)-sets is \(M\)-dependent, so again all three sums are \(\infty\).

Thus assume
\[
a,b,c,d\notin X.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(X)=n-2\). Let
\[
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+2\). For \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\), define
\[
H_e=(X\vee e)^\perp.
\]
Each \(H_e\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\). For distinct \(e,f\),
\[
p^\dagger_{Sef}
=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_e\wedge H_f),&H_e\ne H_f,\\
\infty,&H_e=H_f.
\end{cases}
\]
Indeed, \(H_e=H_f\) is equivalent to \(X\vee e=X\vee f\), which means \(Sef\) is \(M\)-dependent; otherwise modularity gives \(\operatorname{rk}(H_e\wedge H_f)=k\).

So the Plücker relation follows from the following local lemma.

#### Lemma 6.1: local dual Plücker lemma

Let \(Y\) be a rank \(k+2\) flat of \(M\), and let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),
& H_i\ne H_j\text{ and }\operatorname{rk}(H_i\wedge H_j)=k,\\
\infty,
&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\ [13]+[24],\ [14]+[23]\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

**Proof.** If two hyperplanes repeat, say \(H_1=H_2\), then \([12]=\infty\), while
\[
[13]=[23],
\qquad
[14]=[24].
\]
Thus the second and third sums are equal, and the claim follows.

Assume the \(H_i\) are distinct. In a modular rank \(k+2\) interval, two distinct hyperplanes meet in rank \(k\), so \([ij]=\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j)\). Let
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
Intersecting four hyperplanes in a rank \(k+2\) modular flat gives
\[
\operatorname{rk}(Z)\in\{k-2,k-1,k\}.
\]

If \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k\), then every pairwise meet \(H_i\wedge H_j\) is a rank \(k\) flat containing \(Z\), hence equals \(Z\). All three opposite sums are therefore equal.

Suppose \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-2\). For each \(i\), put
\[
T_i=\bigwedge_{j\ne i}H_j.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(T_i)=k-1\). If \(\{i,j,m,n\}=\{1,2,3,4\}\), modularity gives
\[
T_m\vee T_n=H_i\wedge H_j.
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\), and choose \(t_i\in T_i\setminus Z\). Then
\[
A\cup\{t_m,t_n\}
\]
is an \(M\)-basis of \(T_m\vee T_n=H_i\wedge H_j\), so
\[
[ij]=p_{A\cup t_m\cup t_n}.
\]
The desired relation is exactly the ordinary tropical Plücker relation for \(p\) applied to
\[
A,t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4.
\]

It remains to treat \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-1\). The interval \([Z,Y]\) is a finite simple modular rank-three matroid. Its points are the rank \(k\) flats \(P\) with
\[
Z<P<Y.
\]
Define
\[
w(P)=\bar p(P).
\]
We claim \(w\in\widetilde{\Trop}([Z,Y])\). Let \(D\) be a circuit in the interval. Choose an \(M\)-basis \(A\) of \(Z\). For each point \(P\in D\), choose \(e_P\in P\setminus Z\). By circuit lifting in the contraction by \(Z\), there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq Z\cup\{e_P:P\in D\}
\]
whose image is \(D\). Applying circuit incidence for \(p\) to \(C\) and \(A\), elements of \(C\cap Z\) contribute \(\infty\), and we get
\[
\min_{P\in D}w(P)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. Thus \(w\in\widetilde{\Trop}([Z,Y])\).

Now apply the following rank-three edge lemma to the lines \(H_i/Z\) in the rank-three interval \([Z,Y]\). It gives precisely the desired relation. ∎

#### Lemma 6.2: rank-three edge lemma

Let \(G\) be a finite simple modular rank-three matroid. Let \(w\in\widetilde{\Trop}(G)\). Let \(L_1,L_2,L_3,L_4\) be lines of \(G\). For distinct lines, write \(L_i\wedge L_j\) for their intersection point, and set
\[
\langle ij\rangle=
\begin{cases}
w(L_i\wedge L_j),&L_i\ne L_j,\\
\infty,&L_i=L_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl(
\langle12\rangle+\langle34\rangle,\,
\langle13\rangle+\langle24\rangle,\,
\langle14\rangle+\langle23\rangle
\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

**Proof.** If two lines repeat, the same argument as in Lemma 6.1 gives two equal opposite sums, so assume the \(L_i\) are distinct.

If \(w\equiv\infty\), the claim is immediate. Otherwise subtract the finite minimum of \(w\), so \(\min w=0\). By the superlevel-flat criterion, the nontrivial superlevel sets of \(w\) form a chain of flats. In rank three, this chain has the form
\[
a\le B,
\]
where \(a\) is a point and \(B\) is a line, with either level allowed to be absent. Therefore
\[
w(x)=\alpha\,\mathbf 1_{\{x=a\}}+\beta\,\mathbf 1_{\{x\le B\}}
\]
for some \(\alpha,\beta\in[0,\infty]\), allowing \(\alpha=0\) or \(\beta=0\).

First suppose none of the \(L_i\) equals \(B\). Put
\[
y_i=L_i\wedge B.
\]
For distinct \(i,j\),
\[
L_i\wedge L_j\le B
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
y_i=y_j.
\]
Moreover,
\[
L_i\wedge L_j=a
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
y_i=y_j=a.
\]
Thus a pair \(\{i,j\}\) contributes positive cost only when the colors \(y_i,y_j\) agree. For four colored objects, among the three perfect matchings, the minimum equal-color cost is never unique: the color multiplicities are
\[
1+1+1+1,\quad 2+1+1,\quad 2+2,\quad 3+1,\quad 4,
\]
and in every case either all matchings tie or at least two matchings avoid the repeated color and tie for the minimum. This remains valid when some costs are \(\infty\).

Now suppose one line equals \(B\), say \(L_1=B\). For \(j=2,3,4\), let
\[
y_j=L_j\wedge B.
\]
Define
\[
c(y)=
\begin{cases}
\alpha+\beta,&y=a,\\
\beta,&y\ne a.
\end{cases}
\]
The three matching costs are
\[
c(y_2)+\mathbf 1_{y_3=y_4}c(y_3),
\]
\[
c(y_3)+\mathbf 1_{y_2=y_4}c(y_2),
\]
\[
c(y_4)+\mathbf 1_{y_2=y_3}c(y_2).
\]
If \(y_2,y_3,y_4\) are all distinct, then at least two of the three \(c(y_j)\) equal the minimum value \(\beta\). If exactly two of them are equal, the two matchings pairing \(B\) with one of those equal colors tie for the minimum. If all three are equal, all three costs are equal. Hence the minimum is never uniquely finite. ∎

Lemmas 6.1 and 6.2 prove the tropical Plücker relations for \(p^\dagger\). Therefore \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated matroid.

---

### 7. Quotient incidence for \(p^\dagger\)

We now prove that \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\). Let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit, and let
\[
A\in\binom E{n-1}.
\]
We must show that
\[
\min_{c\in C\setminus A}p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

If \(A\) is \(M\)-dependent, then every \(A\cup c\) is \(M\)-dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\). Assume \(A\) is \(M\)-independent. Put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A),
\qquad
Y=X^\perp.
\]
Then \(\operatorname{rk}(X)=n-1\) and \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+1\).

We need a codimension-one polarity lemma.

#### Lemma 7.1: codimension-one polarity lemma

Let \(N\) be the simplification of the contraction \(M/X\). Its points are the flats \(P\) of \(M\) covering \(X\). Define
\[
u(P)=\bar p(P^\perp).
\]
Then \(u\in\widetilde{\Trop}(N)\).

**Proof.** By the superlevel-flat criterion, it suffices to prove that for every real \(\lambda\), the set
\[
\mathcal S_\lambda=\{P:u(P)\ge\lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(N\).

A set of points in a finite simple modular matroid is a flat if and only if it is line-closed. Indeed, if a line-closed set \(S\) spans a flat \(F\), choose an independent spanning set
\[
a_1,\dots,a_m\in S.
\]
Induct on \(m\). The cases \(m\le 2\) are exactly line-closedness. For \(m\ge3\), let
\[
H=\operatorname{cl}(a_1,\dots,a_{m-1}).
\]
By induction, all points of \(H\) are in \(S\). If \(x\in F\setminus H\) and \(x\ne a_m\), modularity implies that the line \(\operatorname{cl}(x,a_m)\) meets \(H\) in a point \(h\). Then \(h,a_m\in S\), so line-closedness gives \(x\in S\). Hence \(S\) is a flat.

Now let \(P_1,P_2\in\mathcal S_\lambda\), and let \(P_3\) be a point on the line \(P_1\vee P_2\) in the contraction. Set
\[
H_i=P_i^\perp\subseteq Y.
\]
These are hyperplanes of the rank \(k+1\) flat \(Y\), and
\[
P_3\le P_1\vee P_2
\]
implies
\[
H_3\supseteq H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
If \(H_3=H_1\) or \(H_3=H_2\), then \(u(P_3)=u(P_1)\) or \(u(P_2)\), so \(P_3\in\mathcal S_\lambda\).

Otherwise \(H_1,H_2,H_3\) are distinct hyperplanes of \(Y\) containing
\[
K=H_1\wedge H_2,
\]
which has rank \(k-1\). Choose an \(M\)-basis \(B\) of \(K\), and choose \(e_i\in H_i\setminus K\) with
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(B\cup e_i)=H_i.
\]
In the rank-two interval \([K,Y]\), the three points \(H_1,H_2,H_3\) form a circuit. Lifting that circuit to \(M\) and applying circuit incidence for \(p\) to \(B\), we get
\[
\min\{u(P_1),u(P_2),u(P_3)\}
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. Since \(u(P_1),u(P_2)\ge\lambda\), this forces \(u(P_3)\ge\lambda\). Hence \(\mathcal S_\lambda\) is line-closed, therefore a flat. Thus \(u\in\widetilde{\Trop}(N)\). ∎

Return to the circuit \(C\). Elements of \(C\cap X\) are loops in the contraction \(M/X\), and for them \(A\cup c\) is dependent, so they contribute \(\infty\). For \(c\notin X\), let
\[
P_c=\operatorname{cl}_M(X\cup c).
\]
Then \(P_c\) is a point of \(M/X\), and
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}=u(P_c).
\]
Parallel elements in \(M/X\) have the same value \(u(P_c)\), and loops have value \(\infty\), so \(u\) lifts from the simplification \(N\) to a vector in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M/X)\).

If \(C\subseteq X\), all terms are \(\infty\). Otherwise set
\[
D=C\setminus X.
\]
Every element of \(D\) lies in a circuit of \(M/X\) contained in \(D\): for \(c\in D\), since \(c\in\operatorname{cl}_M(C-c)\), we have
\[
c\in\operatorname{cl}_{M/X}(D-c).
\]
Thus \(c\) is not a coloop of the restriction \((M/X)|D\).

If the minimum of \(p^\dagger_{A\cup c}\) over \(c\in C\setminus A\) had a unique finite minimizer \(c\), then a circuit of \(M/X\) contained in \(D\) and containing \(c\) would violate the fact that the lifted \(u\) lies in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(M/X)\). Therefore the circuit-incidence condition holds for \(p^\dagger\).

Hence
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)\subseteq\widetilde{\Trop}(M),
\]
so \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).

---

### 8. Involutivity and flat quotients

Since \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\), the same flat-constancy argument applies to \(p^\dagger\). We prove
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]

Let \(I\in\binom Ek\). If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, then both sides have value \(\infty\). If \(I\) is \(M\)-independent, let
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I).
\]
Then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp).
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp)
=
\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]
Thus dagger is involutive.

Now let \(F\) be a flat of \(M\), and let \(p^F\) be the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
Its rank is \(r-\operatorname{rk}(F)\). For a rank \(r-\operatorname{rk}(F)\) flat \(G\) of \(M\), the value \(\bar p^F(G)\) is finite exactly when an \(M\)-basis of \(G\) is a basis of \(M/F\), equivalently
\[
G\vee F=E.
\]
Since
\[
\operatorname{rk}(G)+\operatorname{rk}(F)=r
\]
and \(M\) is modular, this is equivalent to
\[
G\wedge F=\varnothing.
\]
Thus finite bases of \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\) correspond exactly to lattice complements of \(F\).

The polarity sends complements of \(F\) to complements of \(F^\perp\). Therefore
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp},
\]
where \(p^{F^\perp}\) is the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}.
\]

---

### 9. The affine point-annihilator theorem

Let \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\). Define the affine polar hyperplane \(\widetilde H_x\subseteq\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\) as follows. For every \(M\)-basis
\[
B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\},
\]
put
\[
b_i^*=\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i)^\perp.
\]
Since \(M\) is simple, \(b_i^*\) is an element of \(E\). We say \(y\in\widetilde H_x\) if for every \(M\)-basis \(B\),
\[
\min_i\bigl(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

We prove:

#### Theorem 9.1: affine point-annihilator duality

For every rank \(k\) valuated quotient \(p\) of \(M\) and every \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\),
\[
x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x.
\]

**Proof.** We first treat \(0<k<r\).

Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis. For \(S\subseteq\{1,\dots,r\}\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\in S\}
=
\bigwedge_{i\notin S}\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_i).
\]
Indeed, if an element is not in \(\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\in S\}\), then its fundamental circuit over \(B\) uses some \(b_j\) with \(j\notin S\), so it is not in \(\operatorname{cl}_M(B-b_j)\).

Applying the polarity gives
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\in S\}^{\perp}
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\notin S\}.
\]
Thus
\[
B^*=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}
\]
is again an \(M\)-basis, and \(B\mapsto B^*\) is an involution on \(M\)-bases.

Define a rank \(k\) valuated matroid \(\mu_B\) on \([r]=\{1,\dots,r\}\) by
\[
\mu_B(T)=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}},
\qquad |T|=k.
\]
Because the support of \(p\) is an ordinary quotient of \(M\), every \(M\)-basis has support-rank \(k\); hence \(\mu_B\) has rank \(k\). Its Plücker relations are inherited from those of \(p\).

The restriction of \(p^\dagger\) to \(B^*\) is the ordinary valuated dual of \(\mu_B\). For \(|R|=r-k\),
\[
p^\dagger_{\{b_i^*:i\in R\}}
=
\bar p\!\left(
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in R\}^{\perp}
\right)
=
\bar p\!\left(
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin R\}
\right)
=
\mu_B([r]\setminus R).
\]
So
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=\mu_B^*.
\]

Assume first that
\[
x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]
Let \(y\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\). Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B\) and set
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
Then
\[
u_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*),
\qquad
y|_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B).
\]
By ordinary affine annihilator duality,
\[
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Since this holds for every \(M\)-basis \(B\), we have \(y\in\widetilde H_x\). Thus
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x.
\]

Conversely, assume
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x.
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), and again set
\[
u_B=(x_{b_1^*},\dots,x_{b_r^*}).
\]
We prove
\[
u_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*).
\]

Let \(T\subseteq[r]\) have size \(r-k+1\), and put \(S=[r]\setminus T\), so \(|S|=k-1\). Let
\[
A=\{b_i:i\in S\}.
\]
Consider the global coordinate cocircuit
\[
D_A^p(e)=
\begin{cases}
p_{A\cup e},&e\notin A,\\
\infty,&e\in A.
\end{cases}
\]
If \(D_A^p\) is all \(\infty\), the desired equation is automatic. Otherwise
\[
D_A^p\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p)\subseteq\widetilde H_x.
\]
Testing the defining equation of \(\widetilde H_x\) on the basis \(B\) gives
\[
\min_i\bigl(D_A^p(b_i)+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
=
\min_{i\in T}\bigl(p_{A\cup b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
with no unique finite minimizer.

But this is exactly the circuit equation for \(u_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*)\), because
\[
(\mu_B^*)_{T-i}
=
\mu_B([r]\setminus(T-i))
=
\mu_B(S\cup\{i\})
=
p_{A\cup b_i}.
\]
Thus \(u_B\in\widetilde{\Trop}(\mu_B^*)\).

Since \(B\mapsto B^*\) is a bijection on \(M\)-bases, \(x\) restricts to \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)\) on every Boolean apartment coming from an \(M\)-basis.

We now verify all circuit equations of \(p^\dagger\). Let
\[
J\in\binom E{r-k+1}.
\]
If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, extend it to an \(M\)-basis and use the apartment calculation.

If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent and \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)<r-k\), then every \(J-j\) is \(M\)-dependent, so all terms are \(\infty\).

Finally suppose \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent and \(\operatorname{rk}_M(J)=r-k\). Then \(J\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit \(D\). The finite terms in the \(p^\dagger\)-equation occur exactly for \(j\in D\). For those \(j\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M(J-j)
\]
is independent of \(j\), so the Plücker coefficient is constant. The equation reduces to the circuit equation
\[
\min_{j\in D}x_j
\]
for \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\), which holds. Therefore
\[
x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger).
\]

This proves the theorem for \(0<k<r\).

Now consider the boundary cases.

If \(k=0\), then
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p)=\{\infty^E\},
\]
while \(p^\dagger\) is a scalar translate of \(M\), so
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)=\widetilde{\Trop}(M).
\]
For every \(x\in\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\), both sides of the equivalence hold.

If \(k=r\), then \(p\) is a scalar translate of \(M\), and \(p^\dagger\) has rank zero:
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(p^\dagger)=\{\infty^E\}.
\]
If \(x=\infty^E\), then \(\widetilde H_x=\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\). Conversely, suppose
\[
\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\subseteq\widetilde H_x.
\]
Fix an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) and an index \(i\). Let
\[
A=B-b_i.
\]
The coordinate cocircuit \(D_A^p\) lies in \(\widetilde{\Trop}(p)=\widetilde{\Trop}(M)\). Testing \(D_A^p\in\widetilde H_x\) on the basis \(B\), the only possibly finite term is
\[
p_B+x_{b_i^*}.
\]
Hence this term cannot be finite, so \(x_{b_i^*}=\infty\). Varying \(B\) and \(i\), and using that polar bases cover all elements of \(E\), we get \(x=\infty^E\). Thus the equivalence holds for \(k=r\) as well. ∎

Projectivizing Theorem 9.1 is immediate: delete \(\infty^E\) and quotient by tropical scalar translation. The equations defining \(\widetilde H_x\) are homogeneous, so the projective hyperplane \(H_x\) is well-defined. In rank zero, the affine singleton \(\{\infty^E\}\) becomes the empty projective tropical space; in top rank \(k=r\), the dagger is rank zero and therefore projectively empty.

---

### 10. The involution in the simple modular case

Let \(X\in\Dr(M)\). Define
\[
\Phi(X)=\{[x]\in\Trop(M): X\subseteq H_x\},
\]
where \(H_x\) is the projectivization of \(\widetilde H_x\).

If \(X=\Trop(p)\), then Theorem 9.1 gives
\[
\Phi(X)=\Trop(p^\dagger).
\]
Therefore \(\Phi(X)\) is again an element of \(\Dr(M)\). This definition is intrinsic in \(X\); equivalently, by the standard Plücker uniqueness theorem, changing \(p\) by a global tropical scalar does not change \(\Trop(p^\dagger)\).

Because dagger is involutive,
\[
\Phi^2=\operatorname{id}.
\]
If \(X\subseteq Y\), then
\[
Y\subseteq H_x
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
X\subseteq H_x,
\]
so
\[
\Phi(Y)\subseteq\Phi(X).
\]
Thus \(\Phi\) is inclusion-reversing.

Finally, for every flat \(F\),
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]
Hence
\[
\Phi\!\left(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]
This proves the theorem for finite simple modular matroids with a flat-lattice polarity.

---

### 11. Return to the original matroid

Let \(M\) now be an arbitrary finite matroid, and let
\[
S=\operatorname{si}(M).
\]
The lattice isomorphism
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\xrightarrow{\sim}\mathcal L(S)
\]
transports the given order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(M)\) to an order-reversing involution on \(\mathcal L(S)\):
\[
\bar F\longmapsto \bar F^\perp
=
\psi\bigl(\psi^{-1}(\bar F)^\perp\bigr).
\]
By the simple case, there is an inclusion-reversing involution
\[
\Phi_S:\Dr(S)\to\Dr(S)
\]
extending this induced flat-lattice involution.

From Section 2, the diagonal map gives a poset isomorphism
\[
\Delta_*:\Dr(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\Dr(M).
\]
Define
\[
\Phi_M
=
\Delta_*\circ \Phi_S\circ \Delta_*^{-1}.
\]
Then \(\Phi_M\) is an inclusion-reversing involution of \(\Dr(M)\).

Let \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\), and let \(\bar F=\psi(F)\). Section 2 gives
\[
\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})
=
\Delta_*\!\left(\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\right).
\]
Therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Phi_M\!\left(\Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\right)
&=
\Delta_*\!\left(
\Phi_S\bigl(\Trop(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\bigr)
\right)\\
&=
\Delta_*\!\left(
\Trop(S/\bar F^\perp\oplus U_{0,\bar F^\perp})
\right)\\
&=
\Trop(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\end{aligned}
\]
Thus \(\Phi_M\) extends the original flat-lattice involution.

The theorem follows. ∎
