solution:
## Theorem

Let \(M\) be a **finite** matroid on ground set \(E\). Suppose its lattice of flats \(\mathcal L(M)\) admits an order-reversing involution
\[
F\longmapsto F^\perp .
\]
Then the relative Dressian \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\), ordered by inclusion of projective tropical linear spaces contained in \(\operatorname{Trop}(M)\), admits an inclusion-reversing involution \(\Phi\). Moreover, under the natural embedding
\[
\mathcal L(M)^{\mathrm{op}}\hookrightarrow \operatorname{Dr}(M),
\qquad
F\longmapsto \operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}),
\]
one has
\[
\Phi\bigl(\operatorname{Trop}(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
\operatorname{Trop}(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp})
\]
for every flat \(F\).

This proof is for the usual finite-ground-set convention. It does not address infinite matroids.

---

## 1. Conventions and standard facts

Work first with affine cones. Let
\[
\mathbb T=\mathbb R\cup\{\infty\}
\]
with min-plus conventions. If \(p\) is a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid on \(E\), write \(\widetilde L(p)\subseteq \mathbb T^E\) for its affine tropical linear space, including the all-\(\infty\) vector. Thus
\[
x\in \widetilde L(p)
\]
iff for every \((k+1)\)-subset \(J\subseteq E\),
\[
\min_{j\in J}\bigl(x_j+p_{J\setminus j}\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. The projective tropical linear space is
\[
L(p)=\bigl(\widetilde L(p)\setminus\{\infty^E\}\bigr)/\mathbb R\mathbf 1.
\]
Rank-zero affine tropical spaces are \(\{\infty^E\}\), hence become empty projectively.

For an ordinary matroid \(N\), also write \(N\) for its constant valuated matroid:
\[
N_B=
\begin{cases}
0,&B\text{ a basis of }N,\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]

We use the following standard valuated-matroid facts.

1. **Quotient/containment criterion.** A valuated matroid \(p\) is a valuated quotient of an ordinary matroid \(M\) iff
   \[
   \widetilde L(p)\subseteq \widetilde L(M).
   \]
   Equivalently, if \(p\) has rank \(k>0\), then for every \(M\)-circuit \(C\) and every \((k-1)\)-subset \(A\subseteq E\),
   \[
   \min_{c\in C} D_A^p(c)
   \]
   has no unique finite minimizer, where
   \[
   D_A^p(e)=
   \begin{cases}
   p_{A\cup e},&e\notin A,\\
   \infty,&e\in A.
   \end{cases}
   \]

2. **Coordinate cocircuits.** For \(k>0\), each \(D_A^p\) lies in \(\widetilde L(p)\), possibly as the all-\(\infty\) vector.

3. **Ordinary tropical annihilator duality.** If \(\mu\) is a valuated matroid on a finite set \(R\), and
   \[
   H_u=\{v\in\mathbb T^R:\min_{i\in R}(u_i+v_i)\text{ has no unique finite minimizer}\},
   \]
   then
   \[
   u\in \widetilde L(\mu^*)
   \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
   \widetilde L(\mu)\subseteq H_u .
   \]

4. **Bergman superlevel criterion.** For an ordinary matroid \(N\),
   \[
   w\in \widetilde L(N)
   \]
   iff every superlevel set \(\{e:w_e\ge \lambda\}\), \(\lambda\in\mathbb R\), is a flat of \(N\).

---

## 2. Reduction to the simplification

Let \(L_0=\operatorname{cl}_M(\varnothing)\) be the loop set of \(M\). Let \(\bar E\) be the set of nonloop parallel classes, let
\[
\pi:E\setminus L_0\to \bar E
\]
be the quotient map, and let
\[
S=\operatorname{si}(M)
\]
be the simplification.

Define
\[
\Delta:\mathbb T^{\bar E}\to \mathbb T^E
\]
by
\[
(\Delta x)_e=
\begin{cases}
\infty,&e\in L_0,\\
x_{\pi(e)},&e\notin L_0.
\end{cases}
\]
This commutes with tropical scaling and therefore descends to projective tropical spaces.

### Lemma 2.1: \(\Delta\) induces \(\operatorname{Dr}(S)\cong \operatorname{Dr}(M)\)

If \(\bar q\) is a rank-\(k\) valuated matroid on \(\bar E\), define its zero-parallel extension \(q=\Delta\bar q\) by
\[
q_I=
\begin{cases}
\bar q_{\pi(I)},&
I\cap L_0=\varnothing\text{ and }\pi|_I\text{ is injective},\\
\infty,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
The tropical Plücker relations for \(q\) reduce to those for \(\bar q\), with the only new cases being loop terms or repeated parallel classes; those give either all-\(\infty\) relations or two equal finite terms. Hence \(q\) is a valuated matroid, and
\[
\widetilde L(q)=\Delta(\widetilde L(\bar q)).
\]
In particular,
\[
\widetilde L(M)=\Delta(\widetilde L(S)).
\]

Conversely, let \(q\) be a valuated quotient of \(M\). If \(q\) has rank \(0\), it descends trivially. Suppose its rank is \(k>0\). Since every coordinate cocircuit \(D_A^q\) lies in \(\widetilde L(q)\subseteq \widetilde L(M)\), the equations defining \(\widetilde L(M)\) force:

- loops of \(M\) remain loops of \(q\);
- if \(e,f\) are parallel nonloops of \(M\), then
  \[
  q_{A\cup e}=q_{A\cup f}
  \]
  for every \((k-1)\)-set \(A\), with the usual \(\infty\) convention;
- no finite \(q\)-basis contains two elements from the same \(M\)-parallel class.

Also, finite \(q\)-bases are \(M\)-independent: otherwise an \(M\)-circuit inside such a basis would give a coordinate cocircuit with a unique finite minimum on that circuit.

Thus \(q\) descends uniquely to a valuated matroid \(\bar q\) on \(\bar E\), defined by choosing representatives:
\[
\bar q_{\bar I}=q_I.
\]
The preceding equalities make this well-defined, and the Plücker relations are inherited from \(q\). Moreover \(q\) is exactly the zero-parallel extension of \(\bar q\), so
\[
\widetilde L(q)=\Delta(\widetilde L(\bar q)).
\]
Since \(\widetilde L(q)\subseteq \widetilde L(M)=\Delta(\widetilde L(S))\) and \(\Delta\) is injective on simplified coordinates,
\[
\widetilde L(\bar q)\subseteq \widetilde L(S).
\]
Therefore \(\bar q\) is a quotient of \(S\).

Hence \(\Delta\) induces a poset isomorphism
\[
\Delta_*:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Dr}(M).
\]

### Compatibility with flats

Flats of \(M\) are precisely \(L_0\) together with unions of nonloop parallel classes. Thus
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\to\mathcal L(S),
\qquad
\psi(F)=\pi(F\setminus L_0),
\]
is a lattice isomorphism.

If \(\bar F=\psi(F)\), then
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}
\]
is the zero-parallel extension of
\[
S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F}.
\]
Indeed, for \(A\subseteq E\),
\[
\operatorname{rk}_{M/F\oplus U_{0,F}}(A)
=
\operatorname{rk}_M(F\cup A)-\operatorname{rk}_M(F)
=
\operatorname{rk}_S(\bar F\cup \pi(A\setminus L_0))-\operatorname{rk}_S(\bar F).
\]
Therefore
\[
\Delta_*\bigl(L(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\bigr)
=
L(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}).
\]

Thus it suffices to prove the theorem for finite simple matroids.

---

## 3. Polarity forces modularity in the simple case

Assume now that \(M\) is finite and simple of rank \(r\). Then \(\mathcal L(M)\) is a finite geometric lattice, hence semimodular:
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)\ge \rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y).
\]
If \(\mathcal L(M)\) has an order-reversing involution \(F\mapsto F^\perp\), then
\[
\rho(F^\perp)=r-\rho(F).
\]
Applying semimodularity to \(X^\perp,Y^\perp\) gives the reverse inequality. Hence
\[
\rho(X)+\rho(Y)=\rho(X\wedge Y)+\rho(X\vee Y)
\]
for all flats \(X,Y\). Thus \(\mathcal L(M)\) is modular, and \(M\) is a finite simple modular matroid.

So from now on, in the core construction, \(M\) is finite, simple, modular, of rank \(r\), with polarity \(F\mapsto F^\perp\).

---

## 4. Flat constancy of quotients

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\).

First, if \(I\in\binom Ek\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \(p_I=\infty\). Indeed, choose an \(M\)-circuit \(C\subseteq I\) and \(c\in C\). Put \(A=I\setminus c\). In the quotient circuit-incidence relation for \(C,A\), the only possibly finite term is \(p_I\), so it must be \(\infty\).

Second, if two independent \(k\)-sets \(I=A\cup a\) and \(I'=A\cup b\) have the same \(M\)-closure, then there is an \(M\)-circuit
\[
C\subseteq A\cup\{a,b\}
\]
containing both \(a\) and \(b\). Applying circuit-incidence to \(C,A\) gives
\[
p_{A\cup a}=p_{A\cup b}.
\]
By connectivity of the basis graph of \(M|F\), it follows that \(p\) is constant on the \(M\)-bases of any rank-\(k\) flat \(F\). Write this common value as
\[
\bar p(F).
\]

We will also use that every \(M\)-basis contains a finite \(p\)-basis. Start with one finite \(p\)-basis and extend it to an \(M\)-basis. Then move to any other \(M\)-basis along the basis graph. Across an exchange \(B'=B-b+e\), if the current finite \(p\)-basis uses \(b\), apply the circuit-incidence relation to the fundamental circuit of \(e\) over \(B\) to replace \(b\) by another element of \(B'\) while keeping finite \(p\)-value.

---

## 5. The dagger construction in the simple modular case

Let \(p\) be a rank-\(k\) valuated quotient of \(M\), and put
\[
n=r-k.
\]
Define a rank-\(n\) function \(p^\dagger\) by
\[
p^\dagger_J=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(\operatorname{cl}_M(J)^\perp),&J\in\binom En\text{ is }M\text{-independent},\\
\infty,&J\in\binom En\text{ is }M\text{-dependent}.
\end{cases}
\]
For \(k=0\), this is a scalar translate of the constant valuated matroid \(M\). For \(k=r\), it is a rank-zero valuated matroid. Assume below \(0<k<r\).

### Core lemma: \(p^\dagger\) is a quotient of \(M\)

The proof has two local ingredients.

#### Local dual Plücker lemma

Let \(Y\) be a rank-\((k+2)\) flat of \(M\), and let \(H_1,H_2,H_3,H_4\) be hyperplanes of \(Y\). Define
\[
[ij]=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_i\wedge H_j),&H_i\ne H_j,\\
\infty,&H_i=H_j.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\min\bigl([12]+[34],\ [13]+[24],\ [14]+[23]\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

Sketch of proof. If two hyperplanes coincide, one bracket is \(\infty\) and the other two matching sums are equal. Otherwise set
\[
Z=H_1\wedge H_2\wedge H_3\wedge H_4.
\]
By modularity, \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)\in\{k-2,k-1,k\}\). If \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k\), all pairwise meets equal \(Z\). If \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-2\), choosing elements in the triple intersections reduces the displayed relation to an ordinary tropical Plücker relation for \(p\). If \(\operatorname{rk}(Z)=k-1\), the interval \([Z,Y]\) is a rank-three simple modular matroid; the induced weight \(w(P)=\bar p(P)\) on its points lies in its Bergman fan by circuit-incidence for \(p\), and a rank-three matching lemma gives the displayed relation. The rank-three lemma follows from the superlevel-flat criterion: in rank three, a tropical point is supported by a point-line flag, and the three perfect-matchings of four colored objects cannot have a unique minimum.

#### Codimension-one polarity lemma

Let \(X\) be a flat of rank \(n-1\), and let \(N=\operatorname{si}(M/X)\). Its points are the flats \(P\) covering \(X\). Define
\[
u(P)=\bar p(P^\perp).
\]
Then
\[
u\in \widetilde L(N).
\]

Sketch of proof. By the superlevel-flat criterion, it suffices to show that every set
\[
\{P:u(P)\ge \lambda\}
\]
is a flat of \(N\). Since \(N\) is simple modular, it is enough to prove line-closure. If \(P_1,P_2\) lie in the superlevel set and \(P_3\) is a point on the line \(P_1\vee P_2\), then \(H_i=P_i^\perp\) are hyperplanes of \(Y=X^\perp\) with
\[
H_3\supseteq H_1\wedge H_2.
\]
Choosing a basis of \(H_1\wedge H_2\) and representatives in the three \(H_i\), one lifts the rank-two circuit in the contraction to an \(M\)-circuit. Circuit-incidence for \(p\) gives that
\[
\min\{u(P_1),u(P_2),u(P_3)\}
\]
has no unique finite minimizer. Hence if \(u(P_1),u(P_2)\ge\lambda\), then \(u(P_3)\ge\lambda\). Thus the superlevel sets are line-closed, hence flats.

Now verify the two conditions for \(p^\dagger\).

### Plücker relations

Let \(S\subseteq E\) have size \(n-2\), and let \(a,b,c,d\notin S\) be distinct. If \(S\) is \(M\)-dependent, all terms are \(\infty\). If \(S\) is independent but one of \(a,b,c,d\) lies in \(\operatorname{cl}_M(S)\), again every matching has an \(\infty\)-term.

Otherwise put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(S),
\qquad
Y=X^\perp,
\]
so \(\operatorname{rk}(Y)=k+2\), and for \(e\in\{a,b,c,d\}\) set
\[
H_e=(X\vee e)^\perp.
\]
Then \(H_e\) is a hyperplane of \(Y\), and for distinct \(e,f\),
\[
p^\dagger_{S\cup e\cup f}
=
\begin{cases}
\bar p(H_e\wedge H_f),&H_e\ne H_f,\\
\infty,&H_e=H_f.
\end{cases}
\]
Thus the Plücker relation for \(p^\dagger\) is exactly the local dual Plücker lemma.

### Quotient circuit-incidence

Let \(C\) be an \(M\)-circuit, and let \(A\subseteq E\) have size \(n-1\). If \(A\) is dependent, all terms are \(\infty\). Otherwise put
\[
X=\operatorname{cl}_M(A).
\]
For \(c\in X\), \(A\cup c\) is dependent, so \(p^\dagger_{A\cup c}=\infty\). For \(c\notin X\),
\[
p^\dagger_{A\cup c}
=
\bar p((X\vee c)^\perp).
\]
By the codimension-one polarity lemma, the function
\[
X\vee c\longmapsto \bar p((X\vee c)^\perp)
\]
lies in \(\widetilde L(\operatorname{si}(M/X))\).

If the minimum of \(p^\dagger_{A\cup c}\) over \(c\in C\) had a unique finite minimizer \(c_0\), then in \(M/X\) the element \(c_0\) would lie in a circuit contained in \(C\setminus X\), and that circuit would have a unique finite minimum, contradicting the preceding paragraph. Hence the quotient circuit-incidence relations hold.

Therefore \(p^\dagger\) is a valuated quotient of \(M\).

---

## 6. Involutivity and compatibility with flats

### Involutivity

Let \(I\in\binom Ek\). If \(I\) is \(M\)-dependent, then \(p_I=\infty\), and by definition \((p^\dagger)^\dagger_I=\infty\).

If \(I\) is independent, set
\[
F=\operatorname{cl}_M(I).
\]
Choose an \(M\)-basis \(J\) of \(F^\perp\). Then
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger_I
=
\overline{p^\dagger}(F^\perp)
=
p^\dagger_J
=
\bar p((F^\perp)^\perp)
=
\bar p(F)
=
p_I.
\]
Thus
\[
(p^\dagger)^\dagger=p.
\]

### Flat quotients

For a flat \(F\), let \(p^F\) be the constant valuated matroid of
\[
M/F\oplus U_{0,F}.
\]
If \(G\) is a rank-\((r-\operatorname{rk}F)\) flat, then \(\bar p^F(G)<\infty\) iff \(G\) is a lattice complement of \(F\):
\[
G\wedge F=\hat 0,
\qquad
G\vee F=\hat 1.
\]
Indeed, an \(M\)-basis of \(G\) is a basis of \(M/F\) precisely when \(F\vee G=E\), and modularity converts this, with the rank condition, into \(F\wedge G=\hat 0\).

The polarity sends complements of \(F\) to complements of \(F^\perp\). Therefore
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]

---

## 7. Order reversal via the point-annihilator theorem

We now prove that \(p\mapsto p^\dagger\) reverses inclusions of tropical linear spaces.

Let \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\) be an \(M\)-basis. Define
\[
b_i^*=\operatorname{cl}_M(B\setminus b_i)^\perp.
\]
Because \(M\) is simple, \(b_i^*\) is an element of \(E\). Modularity gives, for every \(S\subseteq\{1,\dots,r\}\),
\[
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i^*:i\in S\}
=
\operatorname{cl}_M\{b_i:i\notin S\}^{\perp}.
\]
Hence
\[
B^*=\{b_1^*,\dots,b_r^*\}
\]
is again an \(M\)-basis, and \(B\mapsto B^*\) is an involution on \(M\)-bases.

For \(x\in \widetilde L(M)\), define the affine polar hyperplane \(\widetilde H_x\subseteq \widetilde L(M)\) by
\[
y\in \widetilde H_x
\]
iff for every \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\),
\[
\min_i\bigl(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*}\bigr)
\]
has no unique finite minimizer.

### Point-annihilator theorem

For every rank-\(k\) valuated quotient \(p\) of \(M\) and every \(x\in \widetilde L(M)\),
\[
x\in \widetilde L(p^\dagger)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\widetilde L(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]

Proof sketch. For an \(M\)-basis \(B=\{b_1,\dots,b_r\}\), define a valuated matroid \(\mu_B\) on \([r]\) by
\[
\mu_B(T)=p_{\{b_i:i\in T\}},
\qquad |T|=k.
\]
Every \(M\)-basis contains a finite \(p\)-basis, so \(\mu_B\) has rank \(k\). The Boolean-apartment calculation above gives
\[
p^\dagger|_{B^*}=\mu_B^*.
\]

If \(x\in \widetilde L(p^\dagger)\) and \(y\in\widetilde L(p)\), then
\[
x|_{B^*}\in \widetilde L(\mu_B^*),
\qquad
y|_B\in \widetilde L(\mu_B).
\]
Ordinary tropical annihilator duality gives
\[
\min_i(y_{b_i}+x_{b_i^*})
\]
with no unique finite minimizer. Since this holds for every \(B\), \(y\in\widetilde H_x\).

Conversely, assume \(\widetilde L(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x\). Fix \(B\). To show
\[
x|_{B^*}\in \widetilde L(\mu_B^*),
\]
use ordinary annihilator duality. The coordinate cocircuits of \(\mu_B\) are restrictions of global coordinate cocircuits \(D_A^p\). Since \(D_A^p\in \widetilde L(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x\), testing the defining equation of \(\widetilde H_x\) on \(B\) gives exactly the cocircuit equations for \(x|_{B^*}\in \widetilde L(\mu_B^*)\).

Thus \(x\) restricts correctly to every Boolean apartment. To verify the remaining equations for \(p^\dagger\), let \(J\subseteq E\) have size \(r-k+1\). If \(J\) is \(M\)-independent, extend it to an \(M\)-basis and use the apartment calculation. If \(J\) is \(M\)-dependent, then either every \(J\setminus j\) is dependent, or \(J\) has a unique \(M\)-circuit and the equation reduces to the ordinary circuit equation for \(x\in\widetilde L(M)\). Hence \(x\in\widetilde L(p^\dagger)\).

Boundary ranks \(k=0\) and \(k=r\) are handled affinely: for \(k=0\), \(\widetilde L(p)=\{\infty^E\}\) and \(p^\dagger\) is a scalar translate of \(M\); for \(k=r\), \(p\) is a scalar translate of \(M\) and \(p^\dagger\) has rank \(0\), and the only \(x\in\widetilde L(M)\) with \(\widetilde L(M)\subseteq \widetilde H_x\) is \(x=\infty^E\).

### Inclusion reversal

Suppose
\[
\widetilde L(p)\subseteq \widetilde L(q).
\]
If \(x\in\widetilde L(q^\dagger)\), then by the point-annihilator theorem,
\[
\widetilde L(q)\subseteq \widetilde H_x.
\]
Hence
\[
\widetilde L(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x,
\]
so again by the point-annihilator theorem,
\[
x\in\widetilde L(p^\dagger).
\]
Therefore
\[
\widetilde L(q^\dagger)\subseteq \widetilde L(p^\dagger).
\]

Thus, in the finite simple modular case,
\[
\Phi_M(L(p))=L(p^\dagger)
\]
defines an inclusion-reversing involution of \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\). It is well-defined on tropical linear spaces because the point-annihilator description is intrinsic:
\[
\widetilde L(p^\dagger)
=
\{x\in \widetilde L(M):\widetilde L(p)\subseteq \widetilde H_x\}.
\]
Involutivity follows from \((p^\dagger)^\dagger=p\), and flat compatibility follows from
\[
(p^F)^\dagger=p^{F^\perp}.
\]

Projectivizing gives the desired order-reversing involution on the projective relative Dressian in the simple modular case.

---

## 8. Return to an arbitrary finite matroid

Let \(M\) now be any finite matroid with a flat-lattice polarity \(F\mapsto F^\perp\). Let \(S=\operatorname{si}(M)\). The lattice isomorphism
\[
\psi:\mathcal L(M)\xrightarrow{\sim}\mathcal L(S)
\]
transports the polarity on \(\mathcal L(M)\) to a polarity on \(\mathcal L(S)\). Since \(S\) is simple, the preceding modularity argument applies, and the simple modular construction gives an order-reversing involution
\[
\Phi_S:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\to\operatorname{Dr}(S).
\]

Using the simplification isomorphism
\[
\Delta_*:\operatorname{Dr}(S)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Dr}(M),
\]
define
\[
\Phi_M
=
\Delta_*\circ \Phi_S\circ \Delta_*^{-1}.
\]
This is an inclusion-reversing involution of \(\operatorname{Dr}(M)\).

Finally, if \(F\in\mathcal L(M)\) and \(\bar F=\psi(F)\), then
\[
\Delta_*\bigl(L(S/\bar F\oplus U_{0,\bar F})\bigr)
=
L(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}),
\]
and the polarity sends \(\bar F\) to \(\psi(F^\perp)\). Hence
\[
\Phi_M\bigl(L(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\bigr)
=
L(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}).
\]

Therefore \(\Phi_M\) extends the prescribed order-reversing involution on the embedded copy of \(\mathcal L(M)^{\mathrm{op}}\subseteq \operatorname{Dr}(M)\). ∎

---

self_declared_problem_solved:
Let $M$ be a matroid, and let $\Trop(M)$ be the corresponding tropical linear space, i.e., the closure of the Bergman fan of $M$ in tropical projective space. The relative Dressian of $M$ is 
\[ \Dr(M) :=\{\text{ tropical linear subspaces of $\Trop M$ }\}, \]
which is partially ordered by inclusion of tropical linear spaces. 

Suppose $M$ admits an order-reversing involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$ on the lattice of flats $\cLL(M)$. Note that there is a natural inclusion $\cLL(M)^{\op} \subset \Dr(M)$ given by $F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})$.

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{Question.}  Is there an order-reversing involution $\Phi$ on $\Dr(M)$ that extends the involution $F\mapsto F^\perp$?
